entries.intro: {mob_type} got into the stores again last night. Third time this season.
details: Knocked over a whole shelf of provisions. Took what they wanted and left the rest scattered.
We patched the gap they came through last time, but they found another way in.
reactions: At this rate we'll spend more time repairing than storing.

statCheck.pass: They're coming from the same direction every time. There's a den or a nest out that way, has to be.
statCheck.fail: I don't know where they come from. They just show up.

entries.intro: I keep seeing {wildlife_type} around the garden. Cute, but they're eating everything.
details: I planted extra this year thinking I'd have plenty. I'm barely breaking even thanks to them.
Built a little fence and they just go around it. Persistent creatures.
reactions: I can't even be properly angry. They're just doing what they do.

entries.intro: Haven't seen {mob_type} around lately. That's either good news or they've moved somewhere worse.
details: For a while there you couldn't walk the outer paths without spotting tracks.
Now it's been quiet for weeks. I'm not complaining, but the silence makes me nervous.
reactions: I'll take it while it lasts.

statCheck.pass: Could be seasonal. They tend to pull back when food is easier to find deeper in the wilds.
statCheck.fail: I'm no tracker. Just glad they're not here.

entries.intro: I keep finding {wildlife_type} droppings near my back door. Every single morning.
details: I've chased them off twice now and they just come back. They're not even afraid of me.
Whatever they're eating back there, they're clearly finding plenty of it.
reactions: It's more annoying than anything. I just wish they'd pick a different spot.

statCheck.pass: They're probably after grubs in the loose soil by the foundation. Move the compost pile and they'll lose interest.
statCheck.fail: Animals do what they want. I've given up trying to understand why.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been getting bolder. Found tracks closer to the paths than I'd like.
details: Used to be you'd only see signs of them well past the tree line. Not anymore.
I mentioned it to a few people and they shrugged it off. That bothers me more than the tracks do.
reactions: I'm not panicking about it. But I'm paying attention.

statCheck.pass: When prey moves closer to settlements, predators follow. It's not aggression: it's just the food chain shifting.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they're coming closer. I just know I don't like it.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been quiet lately: almost suspiciously quiet.
details: Nobody's reported any trouble for weeks. The night patrols have been uneventful.
I should be relieved, and I mostly am. But quiet spells make me nervous.
reactions: I'll take the peace while it lasts and not complain about it.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got into the livestock pen last week. Didn't kill anything: just panicked the animals and knocked over a trough.
details: By the time I got outside they were already gone. The goats were just standing there looking offended.
I found the hole in the fence where they got in. Chewed straight through a post like it was nothing.
reactions: More annoying than scary, honestly. Spent the afternoon fixing the fence instead of doing what I'd planned.

statCheck.pass: The chew marks were low on the post: young one, probably. Adults go over fences, not through them.
statCheck.fail: I couldn't tell you how many there were or where they went. Just that they made a mess.

entries.intro: We lost two chickens to a {wildlife_type} this month. Found feathers but nothing else.
details: My neighbor said I should've built a covered run. Easy to say when it's not your chickens or your lumber.
The worst part is not knowing when it'll come back. You just wait and check every morning.
reactions: It's not the {wildlife_type}'s fault: it's hungry, same as anything. I'm just tired of being the one it's hungry for.

statCheck.pass: If it took two in one month from the same spot, it's denning nearby. Probably within a few hundred paces of the pen.
statCheck.fail: I wouldn't know where to start looking. The woods are thick out there.

entries.intro: Found {mob_type} tracks right behind the house yesterday. Fresh ones, pressed deep in the mud.
details: I check back there every morning now. Haven't seen anything since, but the tracks were clear enough.
Told my neighbor about it and she said she'd seen prints near her woodpile too. So it's been making the rounds.
reactions: It's more unsettling than dangerous, but I lock up the shed now. Better cautious than sorry.

statCheck.pass: Deep prints like that mean it was moving slowly: exploring, not running. It's comfortable in the area, which isn't great.
statCheck.fail: I'm no tracker. I just know what {mob_type} prints look like because I've seen them before.

entries.intro: I've accepted that the {mob_type} are just part of living here. Like rain, or taxes.
details: You take precautions, you keep your distance, and most of the time they leave you alone.
It's the newcomers who panic. The rest of us just work around them.
reactions: They were here before us and they'll be here after. Might as well get along.

statCheck.pass: Most {mob_type} follow patterns. Learn the patterns and you can coexist without much trouble.
statCheck.fail: I just stay out of their way. That's the extent of my strategy.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} chased me around a tree for ten minutes last week. Just going in circles, both of us.
details: I don't know who looked more ridiculous. Probably me, since I was the one screaming.
They eventually got bored and wandered off. I sat down and didn't move for a while.
reactions: I can laugh about it now. Couldn't at the time.

statCheck.pass: {mob_type} chase in straight lines and give up once they lose sight of you. Circling a tree is actually a decent strategy if you keep the trunk between you.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they stopped. Maybe they got dizzy.

entries.intro: The {wildlife_type} got into the food stores again. Cleaned out half a shelf before anyone noticed.
details: We've tried blocking the gaps, setting traps, even leaving scraps outside as a distraction. Nothing works for long.
At this point I think they know the layout better than I do.
reactions: I'm starting to budget for what they take. It's just an operating cost now.

statCheck.pass: {wildlife_type} can squeeze through gaps half their size. You'd need to line the walls with sheet metal to actually keep them out.
statCheck.fail: I have no idea how they keep getting in. It's like they walk through walls.

entries.intro: Something's been digging under the back fence at night. I'm pretty sure it's {mob_type}.
details: The holes are too big for anything normal. Whatever it is, it's strong.
I filled them in twice already and they just get dug out again by morning.
reactions: I'm running out of dirt and patience in equal measure.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been sitting on my roof every morning this week. Just staring.
details: I'll come outside and there it is, perched right on the edge, looking down at me. My kids think it's funny. I think it's unsettling.
It's always the same spot on the ridge tiles. I moved a scarecrow up there and it just sat next to it.
reactions: It's harmless, I know. But I don't like being watched by anything while I eat breakfast.

entries.intro: Woke up to {mob_type} noises coming from the direction of the {poi_type}. Couldn't get back to sleep after that.
details: It went on for maybe an hour. Growling, scratching, something heavy dragging.
Nobody else seems to have heard it, or they just don't want to talk about it.
reactions: I keep my shutters closed now. Doesn't help with the sound, but it helps with the feeling.

statCheck.pass: {mob_type} are mostly nocturnal hunters. The noises were probably territorial: claiming ground, not looking for trouble with us.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they were doing out there. I just know I didn't like it.

entries.intro: The {wildlife_type} around here has gotten so bold. One walked right up to my table while I was eating outside.
details: Just stood there looking at my plate like it was waiting to be served.
I shooed it off and it went about three steps, then turned around and came back.
reactions: I suppose that's what I get for eating outdoors.

entries.intro: Haven't had a single {mob_type} sighting in weeks. I'm cautiously optimistic.
details: Last season was terrible: tracks everywhere, livestock on edge, people nervous. This stretch of quiet feels earned, honestly.
I've been sleeping better than I have in months. No more waking up at every sound outside.
reactions: I'll enjoy it while it lasts and try not to jinx it.

entries.intro: The smell coming from the woods {direction} has been awful lately. I think something the {mob_type} killed is rotting out there.
details: When the wind shifts you can catch it from across the field. Makes your eyes water.
Nobody wants to go check. Can't blame them, really.
reactions: It'll pass on its own eventually. Just wish the wind would cooperate.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been nesting in my firewood stack. Found a whole family in there yesterday.
details: I went to grab a log and nearly put my hand right on top of one. We both screamed, I think.
They've been building in there for a while, judging by the mess.
reactions: Now I have to relocate the whole pile. That's my afternoon gone.

statCheck.pass: They nest in stacked wood because it mimics hollow trees. Move the pile away from the wall and they'll find somewhere else.
statCheck.fail: I have no idea how to discourage them. They seem pretty committed.

entries.intro: I've been keeping a tally of how many times I see {mob_type} tracks near the settlement. It's getting worse.
details: Started doing it after that bad week last month. Three times in seven days so far.
I showed my count to a few neighbors and they thought I was being dramatic.
reactions: I'm not dramatic: I'm thorough.

entries.intro: Found claw marks on the side of my shed this morning. Has to be {mob_type}.
details: Deep scratches, right at about shoulder height. Whatever did it was big.
The shed was locked, so they didn't get in. Small victories.
reactions: I'm reinforcing the door tonight whether I'm tired or not.

statCheck.pass: Claw marks at that height usually mean scent-marking. It's claiming territory, not trying to break in.
statCheck.fail: I don't want to think about why it was scratching my shed. Nothing good.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got tangled in the laundry line and pulled everything into the dirt.
details: I'd just washed those sheets. Spent the whole morning on them.
The {wildlife_type} seemed more upset about it than I was, honestly. Thrashing around in a bedsheet.
reactions: I untangled it and let it go. Then I washed everything again.

entries.intro: I actually don't mind the {wildlife_type} that come around. They're good company when you're working alone.
details: There's one that shows up most mornings while I'm in the garden. Just does its thing nearby.
My neighbor thinks I'm soft for not chasing them off. Maybe I am.
reactions: Not everything that shows up uninvited is a problem.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} were howling all last night. Just constant noise from {direction}.
details: Couldn't tell how close they were. Sound carries strangely at night.
The dog wouldn't stop barking back at them, so nobody slept.
reactions: I'm running on two hours of sleep and my mood matches.

statCheck.pass: That kind of sustained howling is usually pack communication, not hunting calls. They were probably spread out and regrouping.
statCheck.fail: I don't speak {mob_type}. All I know is it sounded like a lot of them.

entries.intro: My goats have been nervous all week. I think {mob_type} are prowling the edges at night.
details: They cluster in the corner of the pen and won't eat properly. That only happens when something's out there.
I've walked the perimeter twice and found nothing, but the goats know things I don't.
reactions: I trust the goats more than I trust my own eyes at this point.

entries.intro: {wildlife_type} got into the root cellar and made a mess of the turnips. Not a single one left whole.
details: They didn't even eat most of them. Just chewed a bit of each one and moved on, like they were sampling.
I found little teeth marks on every single turnip in the bin. Not one was spared.
reactions: I spent the afternoon salvaging what I could. It wasn't much.

entries.intro: Interesting thing: the {mob_type} haven't come near the south fields since we started burning pine cuttings out there.
details: Could be coincidence, but it's been three weeks and not a single track.
A few of us started doing it after old Marta mentioned the smoke bothers them.
reactions: If it works, it works. I don't need to understand why.

statCheck.pass: Pine smoke carries compounds that irritate their scent glands. They avoid areas that smell strongly of it.
statCheck.fail: Might be the smoke, might be luck. Either way I'll keep burning.

entries.intro: I swear the {wildlife_type} around here is getting fatter every season. Eating better than I am.
details: Saw one yesterday that could barely run. Just waddled off when I came outside.
Between the gardens and the grain stores, they've got an all-you-can-eat situation going.
reactions: Good for them, I guess. Less good for my pantry.

entries.intro: Found a dead {wildlife_type} near the well this morning. Something got to it during the night.
details: Wasn't pretty. Whatever did it didn't even finish eating: just left it there.
Had to clean it up before the kids saw. Not the start to the day I wanted.
reactions: It's nature, I know. Doesn't mean I have to like finding it on my doorstep.

statCheck.pass: That kind of kill pattern: taking a bite and leaving the rest: is typical of something marking territory. The predator's wasting food to send a message.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what got it. Something big enough that I'd rather not meet.

entries.intro: I saw {mob_type} walk right through the middle of the settlement yesterday. Broad daylight.
details: Everyone just froze and watched them go. They didn't seem bothered by us at all.
They walked past three houses, sniffed at a cart, and kept going toward the woods.
reactions: That was too close for comfort. Even the people who say not to worry looked shaken.

statCheck.pass: Daylight boldness usually means they've been pushed out of their territory by a bigger predator. They're desperate, not aggressive.
statCheck.fail: I have no explanation for why they came through here. They shouldn't have.

entries.intro: The chickens won't go outside their coop anymore. I think {mob_type} have been circling at dawn.
details: Normally they're out the second I open the door. Now they just huddle inside and cluck nervously.
I found scuff marks in the dirt near the coop. Something's been pacing.
reactions: Chickens are smarter than people give them credit for. If they're scared, I'm paying attention.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that keeps following me when I walk to the {poi_type}. Same one every time.
details: It keeps its distance but it's definitely following. Stops when I stop, moves when I move.
I tried throwing it a crust of bread once and it just looked at me like I'd insulted it.
reactions: I've started talking to it. We have a routine now.

entries.intro: Something killed a sheep in the outer pasture last night. The marks look like {mob_type}.
details: Dragged it almost to the fence line before abandoning it. Must've gotten spooked.
The rest of the flock is skittish. Won't graze near that corner anymore.
reactions: One sheep I can absorb. I just hope it's not the start of a pattern.

statCheck.pass: A single kill with a drag trail means a lone hunter, not a pack. If it got spooked, it probably won't come back to the same spot.
statCheck.fail: I can't tell if it was one or several. I just know the sheep's dead.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been digging up the garden beds again. Every seedling I planted last week is gone.
details: It doesn't even eat the roots: just digs them up and leaves them on the surface to dry out.
I've tried burying the seeds deeper. It just digs deeper.
reactions: I'm replanting for the third time this season. My enthusiasm is running thin.

entries.intro: Saw {mob_type} tracks on the path between here and the {poi_type} this morning.
details: Fresh ones, too. They crossed the path and kept going toward the ridge.
I turned around and took the long way. Not worth the risk.
reactions: I'll try again tomorrow morning. Hopefully they've moved on.

entries.intro: My cat dragged a live {wildlife_type} into the kitchen this morning.
details: It was more confused than hurt. Just stood there in the middle of the floor looking around.
The cat seemed very proud of itself. I was less impressed.
reactions: I put it back outside and it hopped off like nothing happened. The cat sulked for an hour.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been staying well clear of us this season. Best stretch we've had in years.
details: Nobody's reported tracks, noises, or missing livestock in over a month. People are actually relaxing.
I walked the outer paths yesterday without checking for signs. Didn't even realize I'd stopped doing it.
reactions: I keep waiting for the other boot to drop, but maybe things are just genuinely good right now.

entries.intro: I left the window open last night and a {wildlife_type} got into the house. Found it sleeping on my chair.
details: It didn't even wake up when I came in. Just kept snoring away like it owned the place.
I stood there for a solid minute trying to figure out how this was my life.
reactions: I closed the window, went back to bed, and dealt with it in the morning. I was too tired to care.

entries.intro: I can hear {mob_type} fighting each other most nights now. Awful sounds, coming from the woods {direction}.
details: Snarling and crashing through the brush. Goes on for a while, then sudden silence.
I pulled the blanket over my head like a child. Not proud of it, but there it is.
reactions: As long as they're fighting each other and not coming here, I suppose I can live with the noise.

statCheck.pass: Territorial disputes are a good sign, in a way. It means the area has enough of them that they're competing: and competing {mob_type} don't usually expand outward.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they're fighting about and I don't intend to find out firsthand.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps getting into the grain silo through that crack in the foundation.
details: I've patched it three times now. It chews through whatever I use within a week.
At this point it's probably eaten more of this year's harvest than we have.
reactions: I need to replace the whole foundation stone and that's not a one-person job.

statCheck.pass: Try mixing crusite powder into the mortar. The taste deters it and it hardens like iron.
statCheck.fail: I'm out of ideas. If anyone has a suggestion, I'm listening.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} got into the compost heap and scattered it across the yard. Lovely.
details: Rotting vegetable scraps everywhere. On the walkway, on the steps, in the flower bed.
It took them maybe five minutes to undo a month of composting.
reactions: I'm building a lid for the heap. Should've done it ages ago.

entries.intro: I actually saw a {wildlife_type} chasing a {wildlife_type} across the field yesterday. Pure chaos.
details: They went back and forth three times before disappearing into the hedge.
No idea what the dispute was about, but they were committed to it.
reactions: Best entertainment I've had in weeks. Beat anything the tavern has to offer.

entries.intro: Something has been knocking over the rain barrels at night. I'm blaming {mob_type} until proven otherwise.
details: Three barrels tipped over this week. That's a lot of collected water lost.
I can see where something large leaned against them. The wood is scratched up.
reactions: I've started weighting the barrels with stones. It helps, but it's one more thing to deal with.

entries.intro: Walked out this morning and there were {wildlife_type} tracks all over the fresh snow. Hundreds of them.
details: They must've been through in a big group overnight. The yard looked like a festival ground.
Every single vegetable cover was knocked aside. Just trampled right through.
reactions: At least the snow melts. The garden damage is another story.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} took a swipe at somebody's drying rack last night. Pulled the whole thing down.
details: Jerky, fish, some herbs: all dragged off into the dark. Nothing left but the broken frame.
The owner found it at dawn and just stood there shaking his head.
reactions: You can't leave food out overnight anymore. That's just the reality now.

entries.intro: I've noticed fewer {wildlife_type} around lately. The garden's actually thriving for once.
details: Usually by this point in the season I've lost half my lettuce. This year it's all still there.
I almost feel suspicious about it. Things don't usually go this well.
reactions: I'll take it. First good growing season in three years.

entries.intro: There's a den of {mob_type} somewhere near the {poi_type}. You can smell it when the wind's right.
details: That musky, sour smell that gets in your clothes. Unmistakable once you've smelled it before.
A few of us have been avoiding the area. Takes longer to go around, but at least you can breathe.
reactions: I keep hoping they'll move on by winter. They usually do.

statCheck.pass: That smell means an active, occupied den. They won't abandon it until the young are old enough to travel: probably two more months.
statCheck.fail: I can't tell a den from a burrow. All I know is something lives there and it stinks.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the smokehouse and made off with half a ham. Half a ham.
details: I didn't even know they could push that door open. It's supposed to be latched.
The drag marks went out the back and into the bushes. Gone.
reactions: That ham was supposed to last us through next month. Now I'm figuring out alternatives.

entries.intro: I keep a tally stick by the door for {mob_type} sightings. Haven't added a notch in three weeks.
details: The stick was looking pretty crowded before. Twenty-some notches in one season.
Now it's been blank for a while. Longest gap since I started keeping track.
reactions: That stick sitting there unmarked is the most reassuring thing in my house right now.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} knocked over the outhouse. Just tipped the whole thing right over.
details: I came out at dawn and it was lying on its side in the mud. Door hanging open.
Had to get two neighbors to help me stand it back up.
reactions: I'd be angry if it wasn't so absurd. My neighbor laughed so hard she couldn't help lift.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that keeps drinking from the horse trough. The horses don't seem to mind.
details: I'll come out and it'll be right there alongside the mare, both drinking together.
The mare just looks at me like I'm the odd one out.
reactions: As long as the horses are calm about it, I suppose I am too.

entries.intro: I set snares for {wildlife_type} near the garden and caught my own boot. The boot I was wearing.
details: Tripped the wire walking out to check the traps. Went face-first into the lettuce.
The {wildlife_type} watched the whole thing from the fence.
reactions: I'm done with snares. They win.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} tried to get into the chicken coop last night. The rooster fought them off, somehow.
details: I heard the commotion and by the time I got out there, the {mob_type} were already retreating.
The rooster was standing in the doorway puffed up to twice his size, absolutely furious.
reactions: That rooster gets double rations from now on. He's earned it.

entries.intro: The mud around the {poi_type} is covered in {mob_type} prints. More than I've ever seen in one spot.
details: They're overlapping: different sizes, different depths. Looks like a whole group passed through.
Nobody noticed until the mud dried and the prints set. They'd been and gone by then.
reactions: It's one thing to know they're around. It's another to see proof of that many in one place.

statCheck.pass: Overlapping prints in mud often look like more than they are. Could be the same few individuals passing back and forth over several nights.
statCheck.fail: I can't read tracks well enough to say how many there were. It just looks like a lot.

entries.intro: I've started leaving scraps out for the {wildlife_type} away from the garden. Seems to be working.
details: They go for the easy meal instead of digging up my vegetables. Mostly.
My neighbor says I'm just training them to come around more. She might be right.
reactions: It's either this or lose the garden again. I'll take the tradeoff.

statCheck.pass: Redirecting their attention works short-term, but they'll start expecting the scraps and get aggressive if you stop. Better to use the time to build proper barriers.
statCheck.fail: Seems to work. I'm not going to overthink it.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} left something dead on the path near the {poi_type}. Like a gift nobody asked for.
details: Half-eaten carcass right in the middle of the walkway. Flies everywhere by the time I found it.
Had to move it off the path with a shovel. Unpleasant doesn't begin to cover it.
reactions: They don't do it to bother us. But it bothers us all the same.

entries.intro: Every spring the {wildlife_type} comes back like it never left. Right on schedule, right in my yard.
details: You'd think I'd be used to it by now. Six years of the same thing.
It makes a mess, eats what it can, and by summer it's moved on.
reactions: It's seasonal damage at this point. I plan for it the way I plan for weather.

entries.intro: {mob_type} got into a neighbor's root cellar and didn't leave for two days.
details: Just holed up down there eating whatever it could find. Nobody wanted to go in after it.
Eventually it came out on its own, looking very well fed and completely unbothered.
reactions: My neighbor's still furious. I'd be too, if my entire winter store was gone.

entries.intro: I heard scratching under the floorboards last night. Pretty sure a {wildlife_type} has moved in under the house.
details: Little feet pattering back and forth. Right under where I sleep, of course.
I tapped on the floor and it went quiet for about ten seconds, then started right back up.
reactions: I'll deal with it this weekend. For now we're cohabiting, whether I like it or not.

entries.intro: I used to worry about {mob_type} all the time. Now I just check the fences and get on with my day.
details: You can't spend your whole life afraid of what might come out of the tree line.
I take reasonable precautions. That's all anyone can do.
reactions: The worry was worse than any actual encounter I've had, honestly.

entries.intro: Something big pushed through the hedge behind the {poi_type} last night. You can see where the branches are all bent.
details: Whatever it was didn't go around. Just shouldered right through like the hedge wasn't there.
I found tufts of fur caught on the thorns. Dark, coarse stuff.
reactions: I'm going around the other way until that hedge grows back.

statCheck.pass: Coarse dark fur like that is typical of {mob_type}. A big one, too, given the height of the break.
statCheck.fail: Could've been anything. I'm just going to avoid that spot.

entries.intro: The {wildlife_type} has been unusually loud at dawn lately. I'm awake before I want to be every single morning.
details: It starts before sunrise and goes on for an hour. Right outside the window.
I've tried closing the shutters. It doesn't care.
reactions: I love nature. I just wish nature would start at a more reasonable hour.

entries.intro: {mob_type} scattered the woodpile last night. Logs everywhere, like something had a tantrum.
details: I'd just finished stacking it the day before. Took me all afternoon.
You could see where they'd been rooting around at the base. Looking for grubs, probably.
reactions: I'll stack it again. What else can I do.

entries.intro: Watched a {wildlife_type} steal an entire loaf of bread off the windowsill. Bold as anything.
details: I turned my back for maybe two minutes. Came back and it was gone: bread and all.
I could see it retreating across the yard with the loaf in its mouth, struggling with the weight.
reactions: I should be mad. I'm mostly just impressed by the audacity.

entries.intro: The fence posts near the south field have been chewed to nothing by {mob_type}. I'll need to replace three of them.
details: It's the same ones every time. Something about those particular posts appeals to them.
I replaced them with harder wood last year and they chewed through that too.
reactions: At this rate I should just fence with stone and be done with it.

statCheck.pass: They chew posts to file their teeth down: it's maintenance, not mischief. Posts treated with bitter oil get left alone.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they pick those posts specifically. Some kind of preference I don't understand.

entries.intro: I've been watching a pair of {wildlife_type} raise their young near the garden. It's been surprisingly nice.
details: The little ones stumble around in the grass while the parents keep watch. Entertaining stuff.
They still eat my vegetables. But they're less annoying when you know the names you've given them.
reactions: I've gone soft and I don't care.

entries.intro: {mob_type} dug up the latrine pit behind the old storehouse. The smell reached three houses.
details: Nobody knows why they'd do that. There's nothing of value in a latrine pit.
We had to re-dig and cover the whole thing. A full day's work for something nobody should have to do.
reactions: Of all the things they could destroy, they chose the one that punished everyone equally.

entries.intro: The path to the {poi_type} has been clear of {mob_type} signs all season. First time in memory.
details: I used to have to check for tracks before heading out. Now I just walk, and other people have noticed too.
I've been making the trip at dusk again. Wouldn't have risked that last year.
reactions: Simple things like walking without looking over your shoulder: you forget how good that feels.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} fell into the well. Had to fish it out with a bucket on a rope.
details: It was paddling around down there looking very sorry for itself.
Took three attempts. The first two times it jumped off the bucket before I got it up.
reactions: We built a cover for the well after that. The {wildlife_type} came back the next day and sat by it like it was waiting for another swim.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been coming in from the {direction} more often. That's new.
details: Used to be all the sightings were from the tree line to the north. Now it's shifting.
Could mean their territory's expanding, or they've been pushed out from somewhere else.
reactions: Either way, it means watching a direction we didn't used to have to worry about.

statCheck.pass: When they shift approach direction, something changed in their range: new predator, depleted food, collapsed den. They're adapting, not targeting us specifically.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what drives them to change routes. I just know they have.

entries.intro: I stepped on a {wildlife_type} in the dark last night. Scared both of us half to death.
details: It was sleeping right in front of the door. I didn't see it until I was already on top of it.
The noise it made: and the noise I made: probably woke up the whole street.
reactions: We both survived. My dignity didn't.

entries.intro: The livestock have been restless every night this week. I think {mob_type} are circling closer than usual.
details: The cows bunch up, the goats pace, the chickens won't settle. Animals know things.
I've been sitting up later, keeping a lantern lit near the pen. It seems to help.
reactions: I'm tired, the animals are tired, and we're all just waiting for whatever's out there to move on.

entries.intro: {wildlife_type} dug a burrow right in the middle of the walking path. Someone's going to twist an ankle.
details: It's a proper burrow too, not just a scrape. Deep enough to catch a boot.
I filled it in and by the next morning there was a new one two paces over.
reactions: They're determined. I'll give them that.

entries.intro: I found {mob_type} sleeping behind the hay bales in the barn this morning.
details: Just curled up like they belonged there. Didn't even wake up when I opened the door.
I backed out very slowly and very quietly. My hands were shaking the whole time.
reactions: I left the barn door open and waited. They wandered out an hour later on their own.

statCheck.pass: If they were sleeping that deeply near people, they're probably exhausted or sick. Healthy ones don't let their guard down like that.
statCheck.fail: I don't care why they were sleeping there. I just care that they're gone.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has completely taken over the abandoned shed at the edge of town.
details: Nobody's used that shed in years, but now it's like a whole colony in there.
You can hear them from the path. Scratching, chittering, general chaos.
reactions: As long as they stay in the shed and not in my house, we have an understanding.

entries.intro: I'm convinced the {mob_type} can tell when someone's been walking the patrol route. They avoid the fresh paths.
details: The nights we patrol, nothing. The nights we skip, fresh tracks everywhere.
It's like they're watching us to figure out the schedule.
reactions: We've started varying the times. Seems to confuse them, which is satisfying.

statCheck.pass: {mob_type} track scent trails: fresh human scent on a path tells them it's active, so they avoid the path rather than the people specifically.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if they're smart or just lucky. Either way, they're annoying.

entries.intro: Something's been chewing the leather straps on every tool left outside. {mob_type}, I assume.
details: Axe handles, harnesses, even the cart ties. Anything leather is fair game.
I've started bringing everything inside at night, which is a pain.
reactions: It's the little inconveniences that wear you down more than the big scares.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} built a nest right above the front door. Every time I go in or out, there's a commotion overhead.
details: Twigs falling, things rustling, the occasional aggrieved squawk when I close the door too hard.
My neighbor said to knock it down. I can't bring myself to do it.
reactions: They'll leave when the season turns. I can use the back door until then.

entries.intro: We found {mob_type} droppings inside the {poi_type}. That's a new low.
details: It means they got in through a gap we didn't know about. That's the worrying part.
We've been checking all the walls and windows trying to find where they entered.
reactions: Bad enough when they're outside. Inside is a different conversation entirely.

statCheck.pass: Check the roof line. {mob_type} are better climbers than people realize: they come in from above more often than through walls.
statCheck.fail: We couldn't find the entry point. That means we can't block it.

entries.intro: I've learned to read the {wildlife_type} behavior like a weather forecast. When they disappear, rain's coming.
details: They clear out about half a day before a storm. Every time, without fail.
I've started using them to plan my work. More reliable than watching the clouds.
reactions: For all the grief they cause the garden, at least they're useful for something.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} charged at me while I was gathering firewood near the tree line. Stopped about ten paces short and just stood there.
details: My heart was going so fast I could hear it in my ears. We just stared at each other.
Then they snorted and turned around. Walked away like nothing happened.
reactions: I dropped the firewood and didn't go back for it. Someone else can get it.

statCheck.pass: That's a bluff charge: they do it to establish dominance without committing to a fight. Standing your ground is actually the right response, even though every instinct says run.
statCheck.fail: I thought I was dead. I genuinely thought that was it.

entries.intro: The {wildlife_type} ate through the rope holding up the gate. It swung open overnight and the goats got out.
details: Spent three hours rounding up goats in the dark. Found the last one asleep under a bush.
The rope was chewed clean through. Neat as a knife, almost.
reactions: I'm using chain now. Let them try chewing through that.

entries.intro: Found {mob_type} fur caught on the garden gate this morning. They were in the yard at some point last night.
details: Nothing damaged, nothing missing. They came through and kept going, apparently.
Still, knowing they were that close while I was sleeping doesn't sit right.
reactions: I've started latching the gate even though it's never stopped them before. It makes me feel better.

entries.intro: The new torch posts along the path seem to be keeping {mob_type} at bay. Haven't seen tracks since we put them up.
details: It was someone's idea to keep the outer paths lit through the night. Simple, but it's working.
The firelight doesn't reach far, but it doesn't have to. They just don't like it.
reactions: Best use of lamp oil we've ever managed.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps getting tangled in the fishing nets I leave out to dry. Third time this month.
details: By the time I find it, it's thrashed around enough to ruin a good section of net.
It takes hours to mend. Meanwhile the thing just wriggles free and runs off.
reactions: I'm spending more time fixing nets than actually fishing.

entries.intro: Heard {mob_type} sniffing at the door last night. I could see the shadow under the gap.
details: I held my breath. Just stood there in the dark watching its shadow move back and forth.
After a minute it lost interest and padded off. I didn't open the door until well after sunrise.
reactions: I've never been so grateful for a thick door in my life.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} had babies under the porch. Now there's a whole litter of them living down there.
details: You can hear them squeaking at night. Tiny little voices, actually kind of sweet.
The mother gives me a dirty look every time I use my own front steps.
reactions: I'm leaving them alone until the babies are big enough to move on. It won't be long.

entries.intro: {mob_type} tore the thatching off the corner of the roof. Just ripped it right out.
details: I didn't hear it happen, which is the scary part. I only noticed the daylight coming through.
Rain got in and soaked the bedding before I could patch it.
reactions: Fixing a roof because of weather is one thing. Fixing it because something peeled it open is another.

entries.intro: I think a {wildlife_type} has been using the empty barrel by the back door as a home. It smells like it, anyway.
details: I tipped it and there was nesting material in the bottom. Grass, fur, bits of cloth.
That cloth looked suspiciously like the dishrag I lost last week.
reactions: I want my dishrag back. The barrel they can keep.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have figured out how to knock the lids off the storage bins. Nothing is safe anymore.
details: They wedge their claws under the rim and just pop it open. Seen the marks.
We've tried heavier lids, tied-down lids, weighted lids. They solve each one within a week.
reactions: I'm starting to think they're smarter than half the people I know.

statCheck.pass: Latch the lids with a pin through a hasp. They can push and pull, but they can't work a mechanism that requires lifting and sliding at the same time.
statCheck.fail: I've exhausted my ideas. They out-think everything I try.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that visits the same spot in the yard every evening. Right at sunset, like clockwork.
details: It sits there for about ten minutes, looks around, and leaves. Every single evening.
I've started timing my tea break to watch it. It's become part of my routine.
reactions: There's something calming about a creature that knows exactly where it wants to be.

entries.intro: I slipped in {mob_type} droppings on the path this morning. Right in front of everyone at the {poi_type}.
details: Went down hard. Landed on my back with my legs in the air.
Three people saw. One of them is still laughing about it.
reactions: I'll recover from the bruise. The embarrassment is going to last longer.

entries.intro: {wildlife_type} chewed through the handle of my best broom. The one I've had for years.
details: It was leaning against the wall outside and they gnawed it clean through overnight.
I know it's just a broom, but I liked that broom. The new one doesn't sweep the same.
reactions: Everything goes inside at night now. Everything.

entries.intro: The torches we set out actually seem to bother the {mob_type} less than they used to. They're getting used to the light.
details: Found tracks this morning right under a torch that was burning all night.
Whatever advantage the fire gave us, it's fading.
reactions: We might need to think of something new. They adapt faster than we do.

statCheck.pass: Constant light stops being threatening once they learn nothing happens when they approach it. Intermittent light: something that flickers or moves: keeps them uncertain.
statCheck.fail: I thought fire was supposed to keep them away. Apparently not.

entries.intro: I noticed {wildlife_type} tracks leading right up to the window and then back again. Like it was looking inside.
details: The tracks were in the mud below the kitchen window. Stood there a while, judging by the depth. I can't decide if that's funny or creepy.
My neighbor has a window box with herbs in it. There are {wildlife_type} prints in the soil there too.
reactions: Probably just saw its reflection. At least, that's what I'm telling myself.

entries.intro: The dogs in the settlement have been barking at {mob_type} every night. Nobody's getting any rest.
details: It's a chain reaction: one dog starts, then they all join in. Goes on for an hour sometimes.
The {mob_type} are probably long gone by the time the barking starts, but the dogs don't know that.
reactions: Between the {mob_type} and the dogs, I'm not sure which is the bigger nuisance right now.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} stole a sock right off the drying line. Watched it happen and couldn't do a thing about it.
details: It yanked it free and bolted. Fastest I've ever seen one move.
That was part of my only good pair. I'm walking around mismatched now.
reactions: Somewhere out there, a {wildlife_type} has a very comfortable nest thanks to me.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} knocked over a stack of lumber by the {poi_type}. Took half a day to restack it.
details: You could see where they'd been rubbing against it. Scent marking, or just itchy, who knows.
The boards at the bottom had claw gouges in them. Those aren't usable anymore.
reactions: Every bit of damage adds up. It's never one big thing: it's a hundred small ones.

entries.intro: I think the {wildlife_type} actually helps keep the insect population down around here. The mosquitoes are worse in years when we don't see as many.
details: Last summer we had hardly any {wildlife_type} around and the bugs were unbearable.
This year it's back in force and I haven't been bitten in weeks.
reactions: It eats the garden, sure. But it eats the things that eat me, so it balances out.

entries.intro: I keep finding tufts of {mob_type} fur on the fence posts. They're using our fence as a scratching post.
details: Every morning there's a fresh clump of coarse hair stuck to the wood. Same three posts.
The posts are getting worn down from it. They'll need replacing sooner than they should.
reactions: It's not the worst thing they could do to my fence, but it's still annoying.

statCheck.pass: Scratching posts are territorial markers. The same three posts means one individual claiming this stretch of fence as its boundary line.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what the fur means beyond something big and hairy leaning on my fence.

entries.intro: {mob_type} knocked the beehives over last night. Honey everywhere, and the bees are furious.
details: Three hives tipped. The bees swarmed the yard so badly I couldn't go outside until noon.
I found sticky tracks leading into the brush. They got what they came for.
reactions: The honey I can replace. My patience with those things, less so.

statCheck.pass: They go for the brood comb, not the honey: the larvae are what they're after. Elevating the hives on stone posts makes them give up.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what possessed them to pick a fight with bees, but here we are.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been sleeping in the wheelbarrow every night. I need the wheelbarrow.
details: I go out at dawn and there it is, curled up in the basin like it's a cradle. Won't budge.
I've tried tipping it out. It climbs right back in before I can move the thing.
reactions: I'm hauling dirt by hand now. Because of a {wildlife_type}.

statCheck.pass: They like the curved shape: reminds them of a hollow log. Turn the wheelbarrow upside down at night and they'll find somewhere else.
statCheck.fail: I've tried everything short of sleeping in the wheelbarrow myself.

entries.intro: Found {mob_type} scat on the doorstep this morning. Feels personal at this point.
details: It's not like there aren't a thousand other places to go. They picked my front door.
My neighbor's door is ten paces away and spotless. Just mine.
reactions: I scraped it off and went about my day. What else can you do.

statCheck.pass: That's territorial marking: they're claiming your doorstep as a boundary. Strong-smelling herbs scattered there should overwrite the scent.
statCheck.fail: I don't want to think about why they chose my door specifically.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps stealing the soap from the wash basin outside. Three bars this week.
details: I can't figure out why: it can't taste good. Maybe it likes the smell.
I watched it drag the bar off the ledge and carry it away like a prize.
reactions: Soap isn't cheap. That creature's got better hygiene than half the settlement and it's costing me.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been digging up the potato patch. I've replanted twice already.
details: They don't even eat the potatoes: just dig them up and leave them sitting on top of the soil.
It's like they're offended the potatoes are buried. Every one pulled up and laid out in a row.
reactions: I'm starting to take it personally.

statCheck.pass: They're after grubs and worms in the turned soil, not the potatoes themselves. Compact the earth around the plants and they lose interest.
statCheck.fail: I genuinely don't understand what they get out of it.

entries.intro: I've noticed {wildlife_type} tracks on the roof every morning. It's using my chimney as a shortcut, I think.
details: Little prints going up one side and down the other. Every single night.
Sometimes I hear the scraping while I'm trying to sleep. Little feet on tiles.
reactions: The tiles are fine so far. But I don't love something using my house as a highway.

statCheck.pass: Chimney warmth rises through the tiles at night. That's the highway it's following: warm tiles are easier on small feet.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it picked my roof. Maybe it likes the view.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got into the tool shed and dragged a rake out into the yard. Just the rake.
details: Everything else was untouched: axes, shovels, hammers, all still there. They wanted the rake.
I found it about fifty paces away, leaning against a tree like they'd parked it there.
reactions: I genuinely cannot explain this and I've stopped trying.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has figured out how to get the lid off the butter crock. I'm losing a quarter of every batch.
details: It wedges its head under the edge and flips it. I've seen the marks on the rim.
Started wrapping the crock in cloth and tying it down. It chewed through the cloth.
reactions: At this point I'm just making butter for a {wildlife_type}.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} left a trail of garbage across the whole yard last night. Bones, scraps, things I don't want to identify.
details: Looked like they'd been dragging their dinner through my property on the way somewhere else.
The smell is something else. I've been scrubbing stones all morning.
reactions: My yard isn't a dining table. Apparently nobody told them.

statCheck.pass: They drag food scraps along their trail to mask their own scent from predators. Your yard is just on the route.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what possessed them to drag their mess through my property.

entries.intro: I put up a scarecrow to keep {wildlife_type} out of the beans. It's been three days and the thing is completely ignored.
details: A {wildlife_type} was sitting on its shoulder this morning. Just perched there, eating a bean.
I tried moving it to a different spot. They followed it.
reactions: The scarecrow is now a landmark for finding free food. Opposite of what I wanted.

statCheck.pass: Scarecrows only work if they move. Hang some tin scraps on it that rattle in the wind and they'll give it a wider berth.
statCheck.fail: I'm clearly not outsmarting a {wildlife_type} with a stick in a hat.

entries.intro: {mob_type} dug up the latrine trench behind the neighbor's place last night. You could smell it from the market.
details: The whole area reeked for half the day. People were walking with their shirts pulled over their noses.
My neighbor had to re-dig the trench and fill the old one. A full day lost.
reactions: Some messes you can shrug off. That wasn't one of them.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} ate the candle right out of the lantern on the porch. Wax and all.
details: I heard a crunch, went to look, and there it was: chewing tallow like it was a treat.
It finished the candle, licked its lips, and looked at me like I owed it another one.
reactions: I didn't even know they ate wax. Learn something new every week out here.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} got into the grain again. I swear they can smell it through stone walls.
details: Chewed through the corner of the sack and spread barley across the entire floor.
They took maybe a handful and wasted the rest. That's what gets me.
reactions: It's the waste that hurts more than the theft.

statCheck.pass: They can smell grain through gaps you can't even see. Seal the sacks inside clay jars and the scent won't carry.
statCheck.fail: I've tried everything short of sleeping in the storeroom. Nothing keeps them out.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that sits on the fence and screams at me every time I go to feed the chickens. Every single time.
details: It's not scared of me and it's not warning the others. It just screams directly at me.
The chickens don't even react anymore: they're used to it. I'm the one who flinches.
reactions: I've started talking back to it. We have an arrangement now.

entries.intro: Found a {mob_type} den under the bridge on the way to the {poi_type}. Fresh dug, too.
details: The dirt was still loose and there were claw marks all around the entrance.
I took one look and turned around. That bridge is their problem now.
reactions: I'm taking the long way until further notice.

statCheck.pass: A freshly dug den means they're planning to stay. Under a bridge gives them shelter from above: won't abandon it willingly.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if it's occupied or abandoned. I didn't stick around to find out.

entries.intro: I left a pie cooling on the windowsill and a {wildlife_type} took the entire thing. Plate and all.
details: I stepped away for five minutes and came back to an empty sill and crumbs on the ground.
I found the plate later, licked clean, next to a bush.
reactions: That was dinner. I had bread and cheese instead and tried not to think about it.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been avoiding the west side of the settlement entirely. Nobody knows why.
details: All the sightings, all the tracks: strictly from the east and north. Nothing to the west.
A few of us have been keeping records. It's consistent.
reactions: I'm not questioning it. I just wish I lived on the west side.

statCheck.pass: If there's a certain smell or sound on the west side they dislike: a forge, a tannery, something industrial: that could explain the avoidance. They have sensitive noses.
statCheck.fail: Could be anything. I'm just glad somewhere is safe.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} chewed a hole in the water skin I left hanging outside. Every drop, gone.
details: Neat little puncture right through the leather. Must've smelled the water inside.
By the time I found it, the ground underneath was a puddle and the skin was useless.
reactions: Now I keep the water skins indoors. Another thing I have to bring inside because of one persistent creature.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} are back. Saw fresh tracks this morning after three clean weeks.
details: I knew it was too good to last. The prints were in the mud by the gate, clear as anything.
One set of tracks, which is better than five. But one becomes five quickly.
reactions: Back to checking the fences every morning, I suppose.

statCheck.pass: A single set of fresh tracks after a long gap usually means a scout. If it finds food, more will follow within a week.
statCheck.fail: One set of tracks: could be nothing, could be the start of something.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been pulling the straw out of the chicken coop bedding. The chickens are sleeping on bare ground now.
details: It sneaks in through a gap I can't find and hauls the straw out strand by strand.
I replace the bedding and it's gone again by morning. Every time.
reactions: The chickens are cold, I'm frustrated, and a {wildlife_type} has the best-lined nest in the settlement.

statCheck.pass: They're pulling straw for insulation, which means the temperature's dropping. When the weather warms, they'll stop on their own.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they want the straw so badly. Must be a good nest somewhere.

entries.intro: I stepped outside at night and nearly walked into a pair of {mob_type}. We all froze.
details: They were maybe five paces away, just standing in the yard looking at me. Nobody moved for a long moment.
I backed inside and shut the door. Heard them shuffle off a minute later.
reactions: My hands didn't stop shaking until I'd been sitting by the fire for half an hour.

statCheck.pass: {mob_type} have worse night vision than you'd think: they probably didn't identify you until you moved. Freezing was the smart call.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they didn't react. I'm just glad they didn't.

entries.intro: The {wildlife_type} has been digging up the flower bed I just planted. Not the vegetables: just the flowers.
details: Every bulb pulled out and tossed aside. The carrots right next to them are untouched.
It's like it has a personal grudge against the marigolds.
reactions: I give up on flowers. Vegetables only from now on.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} made a mess of the drying racks near the {poi_type}. Pulled everything down.
details: Herbs, cloth strips, some cured hides: all in the dirt. Trampled beyond saving.
You could see where they'd been tugging at the frame. Bent the poles sideways.
reactions: Nobody's claiming it's their job to fix, so I know it'll be me.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the house through a window I forgot to close. Found it in the pantry, covered in flour.
details: It had knocked the flour bin over and was just standing there, white from head to tail, blinking at me.
The pantry looked like a snowstorm had hit it. Flour on every surface.
reactions: I chased it out, cleaned up, and now I check the windows twice before bed. Every night.

statCheck.pass: They go for flour because of the high fat content in the grain dust. Storing flour in clay crocks with tight lids removes the scent entirely.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how to keep them out short of bricking up the window.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} scratched up the cart something awful. Deep gouges in the sideboards.
details: They must've been leaning on it or rubbing against it. The paint's ruined.
I just had that cart repaired last season. Money well spent, apparently.
reactions: I'll fix it again and they'll scratch it again. That's just the cycle.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that drinks from the dog's bowl every morning. The dog just watches.
details: The dog could easily chase it off, but instead it sits there and waits its turn.
I refill the bowl three times a day now. They've worked out a timeshare.
reactions: I'm feeding two animals now and only one of them is mine.

statCheck.pass: It's learned that the dog won't chase it, so the bowl is a safe water source. If you move the bowl to a new spot, the habit breaks.
statCheck.fail: I've just accepted it. The dog has too, apparently.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} must've crossed the garden last night: muddy tracks straight through the herb beds.
details: They trampled the rosemary flat and walked right over the thyme. Didn't stop to eat anything.
Just passing through, apparently. My herbs were in the way.
reactions: The herbs will bounce back: they always do. I'm just tired of the replanting.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has taken to sitting on the well cover. Just sits there for hours.
details: It won't move when I need water. I have to shoo it off every single time.
The moment my back is turned, it's right back up there.
reactions: It's a well, not a throne. But try explaining that to a {wildlife_type}.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} pulled a shutter clean off its hinges last night. I woke up to cold air and an empty frame.
details: Found the shutter in the yard, splintered down one side. The hinge pins were bent.
I stuffed a blanket in the window and dealt with it at dawn.
reactions: New hinges, new shutter. One more repair I didn't need this month.

statCheck.pass: They test shutters by smell first, then force: the wood probably absorbed cooking odors. Rubbing the outside with strong vinegar masks the food scent.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what attracted them to my shutter specifically.

entries.intro: I caught a {wildlife_type} napping in the freshly turned soil of the garden. Right where I'd just planted seeds.
details: It had dug itself a little body-shaped groove in the bed. Looked very comfortable.
I replanted around it and came back later. The seeds in its spot never came up.
reactions: I can't even be angry. The warm soil on a sunny day: I'd nap there too if I could.

entries.intro: {mob_type} got into the smokehouse again. Third time this season.
details: They know exactly when the latch is weakest: right after a rain when the wood swells and the fit loosens.
Half the venison is gone. What they didn't take, they knocked onto the floor.
reactions: I'm putting a proper iron bolt on the door. Should've done it the first time.

statCheck.pass: They've learned your smokehouse schedule. Changing the latch helps, but varying the smoking times is what really throws them off.
statCheck.fail: They're persistent, I'll give them that. Wish they'd be persistent somewhere else.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got stuck in the fence this morning. Took twenty minutes to untangle it without getting bitten.
details: It was panicking and thrashing, which made everything worse. Patience was not on either side.
I got two scratches for my trouble and it bolted the second it was free. No gratitude.
reactions: You try to help and this is what you get.

statCheck.pass: When they thrash, it tightens the wire. The trick is to cover their eyes first: calms them down instantly so you can work the fence apart.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the right way to free a trapped animal. I just grabbed and hoped.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} toppled the stone cairn I use to mark the boundary line. Pushed every rock aside.
details: I stack those carefully: took me an afternoon to build. They flattened it in one pass.
You can see where they dug at the base. Looking for something underneath, probably.
reactions: I'll rebuild it again. It's becoming a tradition.

entries.intro: I've been watching a {wildlife_type} learn how to open the garden gate. It's almost figured it out.
details: It pushes the latch with its nose and then pulls. Gets it about halfway before it slips.
Give it another week and I'll need a lock.
reactions: Part of me is impressed. The rest of me is dreading the day it succeeds.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been drinking from the livestock trough at night. The water's filthy by morning.
details: Muddy prints all around the base. Whatever they're carrying on their feet, it's not clean.
I have to dump and refill it every day now. That's extra work I don't need.
reactions: The goats won't touch dirty water. Can't blame them.

statCheck.pass: Adding a splash of vinegar to the trough water makes it undrinkable to them but the goats don't mind. They avoid the sour taste.
statCheck.fail: I can't figure out how to keep them away from the water without covering the whole trough.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} stole an egg right out from under a hen this morning. Bold as anything.
details: The hen was still sitting on the nest. Didn't even notice until it was too late.
I watched it happen from the doorway. Too surprised to react.
reactions: One egg isn't a disaster, but it's the principle of the thing.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} scratched a hole right through the wall of the chicken run. Thin boards didn't stand a chance.
details: Splinters everywhere. They punched through like the wood was paper.
The chickens scattered and I spent the evening counting heads. All accounted for, somehow.
reactions: I'm rebuilding with thicker planks this time. Lesson learned.

statCheck.pass: Thin boards flex before they break: that gives them something to grip and pull. Rigid planks without any give are much harder for them to crack.
statCheck.fail: I thought the boards were strong enough. Clearly not.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the wool I had drying outside. Pulled tufts of it everywhere.
details: It was building a nest with it, I think. Dragging bits across the yard toward the hedge.
Three days of carding, wasted. I had to start over.
reactions: At least something's warm and comfortable. It's not me, but still.

entries.intro: I woke up to {mob_type} sounds right outside the wall. Growling, scuffling, something heavy bumping the stones.
details: It lasted maybe ten minutes. I sat up in bed and waited, not moving.
When I checked in the morning, there were scrapes on the wall and a tuft of fur. Nothing else.
reactions: Ten minutes feels a lot longer when you're lying in the dark listening to something you can't see.

statCheck.pass: Ten minutes of investigation and they left: means the wall passed their test. If they'd found a weakness, they'd have worked at it longer.
statCheck.fail: I don't want to guess what they were doing out there.

entries.intro: The {wildlife_type} around here don't even run from the dog anymore. They just look at it.
details: The dog barks, the {wildlife_type} stares, and nobody moves. Some standoff.
I think the dog has actually given up. It just whines now instead of barking.
reactions: When even the dog accepts the situation, maybe it's time I did too.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} knocked over the sign post at the crossroads. Bent the pole completely sideways.
details: Must've been rubbing against it. The pole was leaning at a forty-five degree angle when I found it.
Two people got lost yesterday because the sign was pointing into the ditch.
reactions: I straightened it best I could. Won't last long if they keep at it.

statCheck.pass: They rub against vertical objects to leave scent from glands on their shoulders. Wrapping the post base in rough wire makes the rubbing uncomfortable.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they pick on that particular sign post.

entries.intro: I've started recognizing individual {wildlife_type} by their markings. That's how often they visit.
details: There's one with a notch in its ear that comes every other day. Another with a crooked tail that shows up on weekends.
My neighbor thinks I'm making it up. But I've been keeping track.
reactions: When you know them by name, it's harder to be angry. That's probably their strategy.

entries.intro: {mob_type} tore through the fence and trampled the cold frames. Every pane of glass, shattered.
details: I'd built those frames myself last autumn. Took weeks to get the glass fitted right.
The seedlings inside are done for. Too much cold damage to recover.
reactions: Some things you just have to sit with for a minute before you start cleaning up.

statCheck.pass: They're attracted to the glass because it holds heat from the sun long after dark. Stone cold frames don't have the same draw.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they wanted with the cold frames. Nothing edible in there yet.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been following the mail carrier on the route between here and the {poi_type}. He's not thrilled about it.
details: It trots along behind him like a pet. He's tried shooing it, ignoring it, walking faster.
He says it waits at the same spot every morning. Rain or shine.
reactions: The rest of us think it's charming. The carrier disagrees strongly.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} are scratching at the cellar door again. I can hear them from the bedroom.
details: It's that slow, deliberate scratching: not frantic, but methodical, like they're testing the wood.
I reinforced the door last month and it's holding, but the sound puts my teeth on edge.
reactions: I sleep with a pillow over my head now. It's not dignified but it works.

statCheck.pass: Methodical scratching like that is investigative: they can smell what's behind the door and they're probing for weaknesses. Replace the wooden door with stone or iron and they give up.
statCheck.fail: I don't want to know what they'd do if they got through. I just want them to stop.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} knocked over the wash basin and sent soapy water across the whole yard. My laundry's in the mud.
details: I heard the crash and came running, but it was already gone. Just the evidence left behind.
The white shirt I'd been scrubbing for an hour is now brown.
reactions: Wash day takes long enough without starting over. That's my afternoon, gone.

entries.intro: Interesting thing about the {mob_type}: they avoid that old stone wall at the edge of the settlement. Haven't figured out why.
details: Tracks approach it and turn away, every time. It's like an invisible line they won't cross.
Some folks think the stone has minerals they don't like. I think it's just the angle of the wind there.
reactions: Whatever the reason, I'm not complaining. I planted my garden right next to that wall.

statCheck.pass: Certain types of stone carry sulfur traces. {mob_type} can smell it at concentrations we can't even detect, and they hate it.
statCheck.fail: Could be anything. I'm just glad the wall keeps them off my side.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been leaving little gifts on the doorstep: acorns, mostly, sometimes a pebble.
details: I find them every morning, arranged in a neat little pile by the mat.
My neighbor says it means the {wildlife_type} thinks I'm part of its territory. Not sure how to feel about that.
reactions: I've started leaving the pile there. Feels rude to sweep away a gift.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} were rolling in the mud outside the {poi_type} last night. The whole path is torn up.
details: Deep ruts in the ground, mud splattered up the wall. Looks like a wrestling match happened.
The path was barely dry from the last rain and now it's a swamp again.
reactions: We'll be walking through muck for a week until it dries. Thanks for that.

statCheck.pass: Mud-rolling is how they control parasites: the mud dries and pulls ticks off when it flakes. They pick soft ground near water, which is why paths get hit.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they have to do that right on our path.

entries.intro: I woke up with a {wildlife_type} sleeping on my chest. Must've come in through the window I left cracked.
details: I didn't notice until I tried to roll over and felt the weight. Opened my eyes to a face two inches from mine.
It didn't want to leave. Kept settling back down every time I tried to move it.
reactions: I checked the window latch three times before bed last night. Not making that mistake again.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} chewed through the rope holding the gate open. It swung shut and locked the goats in the field overnight.
details: Nobody noticed until morning. The goats had eaten everything in reach and were standing there looking bored.
The rope was shredded. Not bitten through clean: shredded, like they spent a while on it.
reactions: At least the goats were safe. Annoyed, but safe.

statCheck.pass: {mob_type} chew rope because the fibers clean their teeth. Use wire or chain for anything that needs to hold: they won't bother with metal.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why rope is so appealing to them. It's not food.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} managed to get inside the chicken coop and all three of them just sat there together. Nobody fighting.
details: The hens were on their perch, the {wildlife_type} was on the floor, and everybody seemed fine with the arrangement.
I opened the door expecting chaos and got the most peaceful scene I've ever witnessed.
reactions: I left them to it. Far be it from me to disrupt the peace.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been leaving tracks in circles around the well. Round and round, night after night.
details: Perfect circles in the dirt, like they're pacing a patrol route. Same path every time.
It's worn a little rut now. You can feel it underfoot.
reactions: I don't know what they're doing and I'd rather not find out.

statCheck.pass: Circular tracks usually mean they're following a scent trail that loops back on itself. Something around that well smells interesting to them: probably mineral deposits in the stone.
statCheck.fail: Beats me. Animals do strange things.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been collecting shiny things from people's windowsills. Buttons, pins, coins if you leave them.
details: My neighbor lost a brooch and found it in a nest under the eaves a week later.
It's got quite the collection going. I saw the stash: it's honestly impressive.
reactions: I've started putting a copper coin out on purpose. It's the cheapest entertainment I've ever bought.

statCheck.pass: Shiny object collection is territorial marking behavior. It's claiming this area by filling it with things that carry its scent.
statCheck.fail: Who knows why they like shiny things. Just a creature being a creature.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got into the apple store and ate through maybe a third of the stock.
details: Cores everywhere. They'd take one bite and move on to the next one.
The ones they didn't eat have teeth marks. Can't sell those.
reactions: Between what they ate and what they ruined, I'm down to enough for the household and not much else.

statCheck.pass: The one-bite pattern means they're testing each apple for ripeness. Store the ripe ones indoors and leave the green ones: they'll stop coming.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand why they sample everything instead of finishing one.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps dropping things down the chimney. Sticks, leaves, the occasional nut.
details: I'll be sitting by the fire and hear a clatter, then something rolls out of the grate.
I think it's trying to cache food in there. Doesn't understand the concept of fire.
reactions: I've started finding acorns in the ashes every morning. It's committed, I'll give it that.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} flipped the water trough over and it landed on my best shovel. Snapped the handle clean in two.
details: That trough weighs more than I do. Whatever moved it was not small.
The shovel was leaning against the wall underneath. Wrong place, wrong time.
reactions: New shovel and a water trough to right. Grand total: one wasted morning.

statCheck.pass: They tip water containers to get at the algae that grows on the inside walls. A clean trough doesn't attract them nearly as much.
statCheck.fail: Strong creatures and heavy objects don't mix well for the rest of us.

entries.intro: I found a {wildlife_type} sitting in the middle of the road yesterday, refusing to move. Held up two carts.
details: It just sat there looking at everyone like we were the ones in the wrong.
One of the cart drivers tried clapping at it. It didn't flinch.
reactions: Eventually it got up and wandered off on its own terms. We all just waited.

entries.intro: {mob_type} got into the woodworking shed and chewed the legs off a half-finished chair.
details: Just the legs: left the seat, the back, the arms all fine. Took the legs and mangled them.
Three days of work, and now I'm starting over on the hardest part.
reactions: I keep the shed locked now. The chair will have to wait until I've calmed down.

statCheck.pass: They chew green wood for the moisture content in fresh-cut timber. Once the chair legs season and dry fully, they lose interest.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what was special about the chair legs. Just my luck.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got tangled in my hair while I was napping outside. Woke up screaming.
details: It was thrashing around on top of my head. I was flailing around trying to get it off.
My neighbor came running and found us both in a panic. It took three of us to sort out the situation.
reactions: I nap indoors now. Fresh air isn't worth the risk.

entries.intro: Haven't lost a single chicken to {mob_type} since we started penning them at dusk. Not one.
details: Used to lose two or three a month. Now the pen's secure and the count holds.
The chickens seem calmer too. They actually lay better when they're not stressed.
reactions: Simple solutions. Funny how long it takes to try them.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} pulled the cork out of my cider jug on the porch. Drank about a third of it.
details: I found the cork on the ground and the jug tipped sideways with sticky residue everywhere.
The {wildlife_type} was lying in the shade nearby, looking very relaxed.
reactions: I hope it enjoyed the cider. That was the good batch.

entries.intro: Found {mob_type} digging at the base of the fence again. This time they almost got through.
details: The hole was big enough to fit my arm through. Another night and they'd have been in.
I packed it with stones and tamped it down. Took an hour.
reactions: It's a fence, not a suggestion. I wish they understood the difference.

statCheck.pass: When they dig at fence bases, packing the fill with sharp gravel discourages them. Soft soil is easy: gravel hurts their pads.
statCheck.fail: I'm running out of ideas for keeping that hole filled.

entries.intro: The {wildlife_type} around here has gotten comfortable enough to eat right out of a dropped basket. Doesn't even wait for you to walk away.
details: I set the basket down for one second: one: and it was nose-deep in my berries.
Didn't run when I yelled. Just looked up with a berry in its mouth and went right back to eating.
reactions: I carry everything in closed bags now. Baskets are an open invitation.

entries.intro: {mob_type} left a dead rodent on the path near my door. Seems like a message, but I can't read it.
details: Just placed there, not eaten, not torn apart. Whole and dead, right where I'd step.
I've asked around and nobody else got one. Lucky me.
reactions: I kicked it into the bushes and tried not to think about it.

statCheck.pass: Some {mob_type} cache food near familiar scent markers. Your door probably smells consistent to them: they're storing a snack, not sending a threat.
statCheck.fail: I don't speak {mob_type}. All I know is there was a dead thing on my doorstep.

entries.intro: I've noticed a {wildlife_type} that comes to the garden only when I'm there. Ignores it when I'm gone.
details: My neighbor says the garden is untouched when I'm away for the day. The moment I'm back, so is it.
I think it likes the company more than the food.
reactions: I've got a garden companion whether I wanted one or not.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} are getting bold enough to come out before dark now. I saw a pair of them at dusk near the {poi_type}.
details: Broad twilight, people still on the paths, and there they were. Didn't seem bothered at all.
A year ago you'd only see them at full dark. The window's shifting.
reactions: I don't like the trend. But it's a trend, not a crisis.

statCheck.pass: Dusk activity means they're feeding earlier, which usually means the nighttime competition is getting fiercer. They're adjusting schedules, not becoming more aggressive.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they're showing up earlier. I just wish they wouldn't.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} dug up every single onion in the row and didn't eat one. Just dug them up.
details: They were lined up on the soil surface like an inventory. Each one removed and placed aside.
The garlic right next to the onions was untouched.
reactions: I replanted them all. I'm sure they'll be dug up again by Thursday.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} were screeching at each other behind the barn last night. Went on for an hour.
details: Couldn't tell what they were fighting about. Territory, food, each other: who knows.
The barn animals were restless. Took them another hour to settle after the noise stopped.
reactions: A bad night's sleep for the animals means a worse day for me. That's just how it works.

statCheck.pass: Screeching like that is usually a mating dispute: two rivals in the same territory. It'll stop once one of them backs down or moves on.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they were fighting about and I'd rather not find out.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that grooms itself on my porch rail every afternoon. Same time, same spot.
details: It spends about twenty minutes there, very thorough, then hops down and leaves.
The railing is covered in fur. I brush it off and it's back the next day.
reactions: I've accepted the railing belongs to the {wildlife_type} between two and three in the afternoon.

entries.intro: {mob_type} overturned every planter box in the yard. Six of them, all face-down in the mud.
details: The soil spilled out, the plants are half-crushed, and the boxes are cracked on two sides.
I spent the spring building those boxes. They were the nicest thing in the yard.
reactions: I'm not rebuilding them until the {mob_type} move on. No point doing it twice.

statCheck.pass: They flip planter boxes to get at the grubs and worms in the bottom drainage layer. Lining the bottom with fine mesh takes away the incentive.
statCheck.fail: I can't figure out why they'd bother with the planter boxes specifically.

entries.intro: I think a {wildlife_type} is living in the attic. I can hear it every night above my head.
details: Scratching, thumping, the occasional running patter. Right above the bedroom.
I went up to check and found droppings and nesting material but no creature. It heard me coming.
reactions: We share a roof now. Rent-free, of course.
