statCheck.pass: Seal the entry point with steel wool: they won't chew through it. Usually it's where a rafter meets the wall: look for rubbed wood and droppings.
statCheck.fail: I couldn't find how it gets in. It's up there every night though.

entries.intro: The good news is the {mob_type} haven't touched the south pasture all month. The bad news is they've been hammering the north side.
details: Every bit of trouble that used to spread around has concentrated in one area.
The folks on the north side are not happy. I can't blame them.
reactions: It's not a solution when the problem just moves to your neighbor's yard.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps meeting me at the door every morning like it expects breakfast. I have never fed it.
details: It sits there staring at me with absolute confidence that today's the day.
I've tried going out the back door. It figured that out within two days.
reactions: I admire the persistence, but I'm not running a charity kitchen.

entries.intro: {mob_type} tracks all over the garden this morning, but nothing damaged. They walked through without touching a thing.
details: Prints between the rows, around the bed edges, even around the compost. But no digging, no eating.
It's the strangest thing. Like they were just out for a stroll.
reactions: I should be relieved and I am. But I'm also confused.

statCheck.pass: Some {mob_type} use gardens as transit routes when the soil is soft: it's easier on their feet than packed paths. They'll keep passing through as long as the soil stays turned.
statCheck.fail: I have no idea why they came through and didn't eat anything. Not complaining.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been screaming from the same tree every night for a week. Bloodcurdling sounds.
details: The first night I nearly fell out of bed. Now I just lie there and wait for it to finish.
The neighbors all hear it too. We've bonded over our shared lack of sleep.
reactions: It's mating season, apparently. It can't end soon enough.

statCheck.pass: That's a territorial mating call: it'll last about two weeks at peak season, then taper off. The tree is its broadcast spot.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how long it plans to keep this up. Feels like forever.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have stopped coming near the chicken coop since I started hanging old pots on the fence. The noise scares them off.
details: Every gust of wind sets the pots clanging. The {mob_type} hate it.
The chickens don't love it either, but they've learned to ignore it. The {mob_type} haven't.
reactions: It's not pretty and the noise drives me a little mad, but I've still got all my chickens.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been caching food inside my boots when I leave them on the porch. Keeps finding nuts in there.
details: I put my foot in this morning and stepped on an acorn. Not the wake-up I wanted.
It's filling every shoe and boot it can find. My neighbor's gotten it too.
reactions: Boots go inside now. Another thing the wildlife has claimed.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pulled all the thatch off the lean-to by the garden. Left it completely bare.
details: I used that lean-to for shade while working the beds. Now it's a skeleton of poles.
The thatch was scattered in a wide arc. They must've been pulling it and flinging it.
reactions: I'll re-thatch it when I find the energy. Right now the sun can have me.

statCheck.pass: Thatch is prime nesting material for them. Treating the straw with a bitter herb wash before thatching makes it unappealing to pull.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how to make them leave the thatch alone.

entries.intro: I saw a {wildlife_type} carrying one of my socks across the roof this morning. Full speed, no hesitation.
details: It must've pulled it off the line in the night. By the time I saw it, the sock was already gone.
That's the second sock in a month. Different feet, too.
reactions: I'm buying socks in threes now. One for me, one for the {wildlife_type}, one for the inevitable.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} knocked the shed door completely off its frame last night. Just battered it open.
details: I heard the crash but by the time I got there, they were gone. Door was flat on the ground.
They didn't even take anything. Looked inside, decided there was nothing worth eating, and left.
reactions: They broke my door to browse and left. I almost respect the nerve.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has taken up residence in the rain barrel. I went to fill a bucket and found it swimming in circles.
details: It looked right at me and kept swimming. Not alarmed in the slightest.
I can't use the water now: it's been bathing in there for who knows how long.
reactions: I dumped the barrel, refilled it, and put a board on top. Problem solved, for now.

entries.intro: I noticed the {mob_type} have cleared out since the new neighbors moved in with their dogs. Four dogs.
details: The dogs bark at everything, all night, constantly: it's maddening. But the {mob_type} have vanished.
Haven't seen a single track in two weeks. Turns out four dogs is the magic number.
reactions: Traded one nuisance for another, really. But at least the dogs don't eat the livestock.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been chewing the rungs of the ladder I keep leaning against the barn. Two rungs are gone.
details: I went to climb up and my foot went through where a rung should've been.
The chew marks are clean. It's working on the third rung now.
reactions: One more rung and that ladder's firewood. Which is probably what I should've made it into.

entries.intro: {mob_type} found the salt lick I put out for the cattle and demolished it. One night, gone.
details: Those licks aren't cheap and they're supposed to last a whole season.
The cattle stood at the empty spot the next morning looking confused.
reactions: Now I bring the salt lick inside at night and put it out at dawn. One more chore.

statCheck.pass: {mob_type} crave salt the same way livestock do. Bury the lick in a shallow pit with a heavy stone over it: cattle can nose the stone aside, but {mob_type} can't get the leverage.
statCheck.fail: Salt is salt, I suppose. Everything wants it.

entries.intro: I actually enjoy hearing the {wildlife_type} in the evenings. It's the one nice sound at the end of the day.
details: Everything else at dusk is chores and tired feet. But that sound out in the dusk: that I like.
My spouse says I'm romanticizing pests. Maybe so, but it's still nice.
reactions: Not every creature around here is a problem. Some of them are just neighbors.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} got to the berry bushes before I could harvest them. Every ripe berry, gone.
details: I was planning to pick them this morning. Went out and found bare branches and purple stains on the ground.
They even broke some of the canes getting at the higher berries. The bush is half-ruined.
reactions: Next year I'm putting netting over the bushes. Should've done it this year.

statCheck.pass: They hit berry bushes right at peak ripeness because the sugar scent peaks then. Harvest a day earlier than you think you should and you'll beat them to it.
statCheck.fail: Every year I think I'll get there first and every year they win.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} fell asleep in the sun on the path to the {poi_type}. Nobody wanted to step over it.
details: Three people were just standing there waiting for it to move. It didn't.
Eventually someone went around through the grass. We all followed.
reactions: We took a ten-minute detour because nobody wanted to wake up a sleeping creature. That says something about us.

entries.intro: {mob_type} tore the cover off the well and dragged it about twenty paces into the field.
details: The cover was heavy: took two people to set it right. They moved it like it was nothing.
Debris fell into the well. Had to fish it all out before anyone could draw water.
reactions: We're building a stone lid that bolts down. They won't move that.

statCheck.pass: They were after standing water: at night, a covered well sounds like a stream to them. A stone lid eliminates the echo that attracts them.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what drew them to the well cover. Nothing edible there.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been eating the mortar out from between the stones on the garden wall. Picking at it like candy.
details: There are little chunks missing all along the base. You can see the tooth marks in the lime.
At the rate it's going, the bottom row will be loose by autumn.
reactions: I never thought I'd have to defend a wall from being eaten.

statCheck.pass: It's after the calcium in the lime mortar: probably preparing for laying season. Mix ash into the mortar and the taste deters them.
statCheck.fail: Why anything would eat a wall is beyond me.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} ignored the garden entirely this week. Walked right past it.
details: I can see the tracks going along the fence line and continuing on. Not even a pause.
I don't know what changed, but the vegetables are all still there.
reactions: I'm going to enjoy this for as long as it lasts and not question it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has claimed the scarecrow as a lookout perch. Sits on its head all day.
details: The other animals come and go while it watches from up there. Like a little guard.
Ironically the garden's been less raided since it took up the post.
reactions: The scarecrow failed at its job but the {wildlife_type} is doing fine. Funny how things work out.

entries.intro: I keep finding {mob_type} bedding sites in the tall grass beyond the fence. Flattened circles where they've been sleeping.
details: Three or four of them, close together. They're nesting out there regularly.
The grass is so beaten down in those spots that nothing grows back.
reactions: Knowing they sleep that close isn't comfortable. But they've been there a while and haven't caused trouble.

statCheck.pass: Bedding sites that close to a settlement mean they're using your presence as a buffer against larger predators. You're protecting them, in a way.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they sleep so close. I'd rather they didn't.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} tried to take a nap in the bread basket at the market. Right there, in front of everyone.
details: It climbed in, circled twice, and lay down on top of three loaves. Like it was a bed.
The baker didn't know whether to laugh or cry. She chose both.
reactions: Those loaves had to be thrown out. But the story's been worth telling all week.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} haven't touched the orchard since we planted lavender around the edges. Not once.
details: It was an experiment, honestly. Someone said they don't like the smell.
Two months now and every fruit is accounted for. First clean season in years.
reactions: I'm planting lavender around everything. If it works, it works.

statCheck.pass: Lavender oil contains compounds that overwhelm their scent receptors. It doesn't hurt them: they just can't smell anything else while they're near it, and that makes them nervous.
statCheck.fail: Could be the lavender, could be coincidence. I'm not taking it out to test the theory.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} stared me down for a full minute outside the barn this morning. Didn't blink.
details: I was holding a pitchfork and it still didn't move. Just staring.
I'm the one who looked away first. I'm not proud of that.
reactions: I've been outmatched in a staring contest by something that weighs less than my boot.

entries.intro: {mob_type} dragged the haystack apart and spread it across the entire paddock.
details: I'd just built that stack yesterday: baled, stacked, covered. All of it undone in one night.
The hay is too trampled and wet to re-bale. It's mulch now.
reactions: I stood there looking at it for a while. Then I went inside and had a drink.

statCheck.pass: Hay stacks attract them because the fermentation warmth draws insects, and they're after the insects. A properly ventilated stack runs cooler and draws less attention.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they wanted with the hay. They didn't eat it, just tore it apart.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has figured out how to tip the compost bin. Spreads the contents across the yard every night.
details: It pushes against the side until it topples, then picks through the scraps at leisure.
I weighted the bin with rocks. It pushed harder.
reactions: I'm in an arms race with something that doesn't know it's in an arms race.

statCheck.pass: It tips the bin by pushing at the center of gravity. A wider base or a wall-mounted bin can't be tipped no matter how hard they push.
statCheck.fail: I've tried everything to keep the bin upright. Nothing lasts.

entries.intro: I watched some {mob_type} try to climb the stone wall by the {poi_type} last week. They couldn't manage it.
details: Kept scrabbling at the stones and sliding back down. Tried maybe six times.
Eventually they gave up and went around the long way. Took them about an hour.
reactions: Nice to know there's something they can't get past.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps pulling the thatch out of the eaves bit by bit. There's a bald spot forming above the door.
details: Every morning a few more strands on the ground. It's nesting season, I assume.
The leak hasn't started yet. But it will, and it won't be cheap.
reactions: I'll patch it when the season turns. There's no point fixing it while they're still pulling.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got into the pickling crock on the back step. Ate the pickles, drank the brine.
details: The crock was tipped over and empty when I found it. Not a single pickle left.
There were wet tracks leading away and a strong vinegar smell in the air.
reactions: I'm more offended than I should be. Those pickles took three weeks to set.

statCheck.pass: They'll chew through ceramic but not fired stoneware: the texture is too hard on their teeth. Use a stoneware crock with a weighted lid.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how to store anything outside without them getting into it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} made a nest inside the forge bellows. The smith found out when he tried to pump them.
details: He says the bellows wheezed, then something shot out across the workshop at high speed.
He didn't get any work done for the rest of the day. Spent it cleaning bedding material out of the mechanism.
reactions: I shouldn't laugh but I was crying by the time he finished telling the story.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been getting into fights with the settlement cats. The cats are winning.
details: I've seen it twice now: a cat puffed up to three times its size, hissing, and some {mob_type} backing away.
The cats around here have no fear. Grew up rough, I think.
reactions: I never thought I'd be grateful for mean cats, but here we are.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} dug a hole in the path so deep that a cart wheel dropped into it. Took four of us to lift the cart out.
details: The driver didn't see it until the wheel was already in. Axle nearly cracked.
We filled the hole with gravel. There was a new hole next to it by morning.
reactions: I'm convinced they're doing it on purpose. No proof, just a feeling.

entries.intro: I've started leaving the porch light burning all night. The {mob_type} come less often when it's lit.
details: Before the light, I'd hear them most nights. Now it's maybe once a week.
The oil cost adds up, but it's cheaper than replacing what they'd destroy.
reactions: Small price for a good night's sleep.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into a fight with its own reflection in the water trough. Lost badly.
details: It was pecking at the water, splashing, circling around for another go. Went on for twenty minutes.
By the end it was soaked and furious. The reflection remained undefeated.
reactions: Best thing I've seen all month.

entries.intro: {mob_type} shredded the canvas cover on the firewood stack. Just tore it to ribbons.
details: The firewood's fine: they didn't touch it. They just destroyed the cover for no apparent reason.
That canvas was nearly new. Cost me a whole afternoon's wages.
reactions: I'll get a new one. And nail it down this time.

statCheck.pass: Canvas holds scent from whatever it's been covering: they shred it to investigate the smell, not to eat it. Airing the canvas before storing removes the trigger.
statCheck.fail: No idea why they shredded it. Pure spite, as far as I can tell.

entries.intro: The {wildlife_type} has been bathing in the puddles outside the door. Splashing everywhere.
details: Every rain leaves a puddle, and every puddle gets claimed. Within minutes there's one in there, going wild.
The mud gets flung onto the door, the walls, the steps. Everything.
reactions: I could fill the puddle with gravel. But honestly, it's a little entertaining.

entries.intro: Someone left the gate to the {poi_type} open and {mob_type} got in. Tracked mud through the whole place.
details: Muddy prints across the floor, knocked over a few things, and left through the same gate.
Nobody wants to admit they left the gate open. Cleaning up took most of the morning.
reactions: The gate has a latch now. First useful thing we've agreed on in weeks.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps scratching at the back door at the same time every evening. Like it has an appointment.
details: Right at sundown, every day: three little scratches, then it waits.
I opened the door once by accident and it walked in like it lived here.
reactions: The door stays closed now. I don't need a roommate.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} brought something rotten into the yard and buried it near the vegetable patch. The smell is incredible.
details: I dug it up and: well, I'd rather not describe it. I moved it far away.
By morning they'd dug it back up and reburied it in the same spot.
reactions: We're in a disagreement about where this thing belongs. I'm losing.

statCheck.pass: That's food caching behavior: they bury surplus near familiar scent markers. Your yard smells consistent to them, so they treat it as their pantry.
statCheck.fail: Whatever game they're playing with that buried thing, I want no part of it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has started bringing its young to the garden. Now I'm feeding a whole family instead of just one.
details: The little ones are clumsy and knock over twice as much as they eat.
There used to be one set of tracks. Now there are five.
reactions: I should be angrier. But the little ones wobbling around the lettuce makes it hard.

entries.intro: {mob_type} chased the goats so badly last night that one jumped the fence. Found it two fields over.
details: The goat was fine but panicked. Took me and a neighbor an hour to catch it.
The fence has a gap now where the goat went over. One more thing to fix.
reactions: If a goat can jump the fence, I need a taller fence. Or calmer goats.

statCheck.pass: Goats panic in straight lines: they always bolt for the highest point they can reach. If the fence has a lower section, that's where they'll go over.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how the goat got over a fence that tall. Desperation, I guess.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that waits by the fisherman's dock every afternoon. Knows exactly when they come in with the catch.
details: It sits perfectly still until the boat's in, then edges closer and closer to the baskets.
The fishermen have stopped fighting it. They just toss it a scrap and it goes away satisfied.
reactions: It's got a better routine than most people I know.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pushed over the rain collection barrel. A week's worth of water, soaked into the ground.
details: It was full, too. That barrel was heavy enough that I can barely move it empty.
The barrel itself cracked when it fell. So now I need water and a barrel.
reactions: Every problem they cause comes with a bonus problem attached.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been sitting in the same patch of sun on the porch every afternoon. Won't share.
details: I tried sitting in that spot first. It climbed onto my lap and I had to leave.
It's the best sun spot on the property and it knows it.
reactions: I've found a second-best spot around the corner. Smaller, but mine.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} seem to be avoiding the area where we dump the ash from the fires. Interesting.
details: I started spreading ash along the fence line as an experiment. No tracks since.
My neighbor's side, no ash: tracks every morning.
reactions: Could be coincidence. But the ash is free and the fence repairs aren't, so I'll keep at it.

statCheck.pass: Wood ash irritates their paw pads. They can smell it from a good distance and won't cross a fresh line of it.
statCheck.fail: Might be the ash, might be luck. Either way I'm keeping the line fresh.

entries.intro: Found a {wildlife_type} inside the outhouse this morning. Nearly had a heart attack.
details: I opened the door and it was just sitting there on the seat. Looking at me.
I closed the door, waited five minutes, opened it again. Still there.
reactions: I used the bushes. Some battles aren't worth fighting.

entries.intro: {mob_type} broke the axle on the cart that was parked by the fence. Just crashed into it.
details: Must've been running through in the dark and hit it at full speed. The impact snapped the axle clean.
The wheel was twenty paces away in the grass.
reactions: A new axle isn't cheap and it isn't quick. The cart's out of service for a week at least.

statCheck.pass: {mob_type} bolt in straight lines when spooked: they probably didn't even see the cart. Parking vehicles away from their transit routes prevents collisions.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they ran into it. Maybe they're just clumsy.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been gnawing on the leather hinges of the garden gate. They're about to give out.
details: I replaced them twice and it chewed through the replacements even faster.
It's not eating the leather: just chewing it. Maybe it likes the texture.
reactions: Iron hinges it is. I should've skipped the leather from the start.

entries.intro: I've been tracking the {mob_type} patterns for months now. They come in waves: a bad week, then two good ones, then bad again.
details: It's predictable enough that I can plan around it. Bad weeks, everything goes indoors.
Good weeks, I relax a bit: let the chickens range, leave the tools out.
reactions: Living with them is all about patterns. Once you know the rhythm, it's manageable.

statCheck.pass: Those cycles usually track with the moon phases: they forage more actively around the new moon when it's darkest. Plan your defenses around the lunar calendar.
statCheck.fail: I can't explain the cycle. I just know it exists.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} tipped over the milk pail this morning. The fresh milk I spent half an hour collecting.
details: It stuck its head in while I had my back turned. Pulled back, knocked the pail, milk everywhere.
The cat was the only one happy about it. Lapped up what it could before I chased everyone off.
reactions: I'll milk the goat again this evening. But I'll be standing guard this time.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} are using the same path through the settlement every time. Worn a trail right through the middle.
details: You can see the track: a thin dirt line where the grass won't grow back.
It cuts between two houses and goes straight to the fields. Very direct.
reactions: They've essentially built themselves a road. I'd admire the efficiency if it wasn't through my yard.

statCheck.pass: {mob_type} are creatures of habit: once they establish a route, they use it until something blocks it completely. Put any obstacle on that path and they'll find a new one.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they always go the same way. They just do.

entries.intro: I accidentally startled a {wildlife_type} this morning and it knocked over everything on the porch. Everything.
details: I opened the door too fast. It panicked, hit the shelf, and there went the pottery, the tools, and a jar of preserves.
The jar smashed. Sticky fruit everywhere, mixed with broken pottery.
reactions: I scared it: it destroyed my porch. Feels like an unfair exchange.

entries.intro: The neighbor's kid has been feeding the {mob_type}. Now they come to that house every night expecting a meal.
details: What started as one scrap tossed into the dark has turned into a nightly congregation.
The neighbor had a talk with the kid. But the {mob_type} haven't gotten the message.
reactions: They'll keep coming until the habit breaks. Could be weeks.

statCheck.pass: Once {mob_type} associate a location with food, it takes about a month of nothing before they give up. Consistent denial is the only way.
statCheck.fail: Kids and wildlife: a combination that always ends in more work for the adults.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been using the clothesline as a perch. The weight of it is stretching the line.
details: I've tightened it three times. It sags back down within a day.
My shirts are almost touching the ground now when I hang them.
reactions: I need a sturdier line or a lighter creature. I can only control one of those.

entries.intro: Found {mob_type} prints inside the open stable this morning. They walked right in while the horses slept.
details: The horses were calm, which surprised me. Whatever came through didn't bother them.
There were prints in the hay and around the feed trough. Nothing taken, nothing disturbed.
reactions: The stable door gets latched from now on. Calm horses or not.

statCheck.pass: If the horses weren't alarmed, whatever came through was downwind and moving slowly. Calm horses mean the visitor wasn't hunting.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why the horses didn't react. That worries me more than the tracks.

entries.intro: I watched a {wildlife_type} try to steal a turnip from the garden and drop it three times on the way to the fence.
details: It kept picking it up, waddling two steps, dropping it, and starting over. The turnip was too big.
It finally gave up and went back for a smaller one. Succeeded on the first try.
reactions: I lost a turnip but gained a story. Fair trade.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} knocked over the stone memorial marker at the crossroads. It's been standing there for years.
details: It's too heavy for one person to right. I'll need help and a lever.
The base is cracked where it hit the ground. Might not stand properly again.
reactions: That marker was there before I was. Now it's on its side because something leaned on it too hard.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the sewing basket and ran off with a whole spool of thread. Left a trail across the yard.
details: Thread was wound around the chair legs, the fence post, and into the hedge. Like a spider web.
I spent an hour following the thread trying to recover it. Got about half.
reactions: Thread costs money. The {wildlife_type} has expensive taste for something that doesn't wear clothes.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been quiet for so long that people are starting to relax. That makes me nervous.
details: Last time everyone relaxed, they came back worse than before.
I keep my fences up and my stores locked. Quiet doesn't mean gone.
reactions: I'd rather be prepared and wrong than relaxed and surprised.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} fell asleep inside the empty rain barrel again. I could hear it snoring.
details: Tiny little snores echoing around inside the barrel. Almost musical.
I left the barrel alone and filled a bucket from the well instead.
reactions: Some things you just don't have the heart to disturb.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} tore up the vegetable netting I'd just finished installing. Took me all weekend.
details: Ripped clean through and trampled what was underneath. Didn't even eat most of it.
I found a piece of netting snagged on a bush ten paces away.
reactions: I'm going to sit down, think hard, and come up with something they can't tear through.

statCheck.pass: Standard netting is too thin: they hook their claws in it and pull. Mesh with openings smaller than a finger width resists tearing because they can't grip it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what kind of barrier would actually stop them at this point.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps tapping on the window at night. Just this persistent tap, tap, tap.
details: It does it around midnight. I get up, look out, and there it is on the sill.
It sees me, stops, and starts again the moment I go back to bed.
reactions: I've hung a cloth over the window and the tapping stopped. Apparently it was the reflection.

entries.intro: I found {mob_type} scratches on the tree where I hang the game. They know that's where the meat goes.
details: Deep gouges in the bark, about chest height. They've been reaching for it.
I've started hanging it higher. My arms are sore but the meat's safe.
reactions: It's an arms race: I hang higher, they reach higher. Eventually one of us runs out of tree.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the spice jars and sneezed so hard it knocked itself off the shelf.
details: I heard a crash, then a series of tiny sneezes. Found it on the floor surrounded by pepper.
It sneezed its way out of the house and across the yard. Left a pepper trail.
reactions: The spices are ruined but I can't stop laughing about it. Fair trade.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} seem to avoid the area since we started keeping a fire going all night at the edge of the field.
details: Takes turns tending it: two hours each among four of us. Worth the lost sleep.
The tracks stop about fifty paces from the fire line and turn back every time.
reactions: As long as we keep the fire going, we keep the peace. That's the deal.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} dragged a dead fish onto the porch and left it there. No idea where it got a fish.
details: There's no stream near my house. The nearest water is a good walk away.
It must've carried that fish the whole distance. Dedicated creature.
reactions: I threw the fish out and mopped the porch. Smells like the docks out there now.

entries.intro: {mob_type} chewed through the clothesline rope overnight. Everything that was hanging is in the mud.
details: Two shirts, a pair of trousers, and the good tablecloth. All face-down in the dirt.
The rope was bitten clean through in two places. Not frayed: bitten.
reactions: Wash day, again. My hands are raw and my mood is worse.

statCheck.pass: They chew through rope the same way they chew anything fibrous: for tooth maintenance. A length of chain costs more but lasts forever.
statCheck.fail: Rope, leather, twine: they'll chew through anything. I don't know what to use instead.

entries.intro: I think a {wildlife_type} is teaching its young that my garden is a food source. I've seen lessons in progress.
details: The adult leads the young ones to the lettuce, demonstrates a nibble, and steps back.
They're literally being educated on how to steal from me. Generational theft.
reactions: I can't compete with a curriculum. I need a fence.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} ripped the fish trap out of the stream and left it on the bank. Empty, of course.
details: The trap was shredded. Wicker basket, completely pulled apart.
I had three decent fish in there when I set it. All gone.
reactions: I'll build another trap, a stronger one. It's the only productive response I've got.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has started greeting me when I come home. Sits by the door and chirps.
details: It's there every evening without fail. Like a little welcoming committee of one.
My kids named it. Now I can't chase it off because they'll be upset.
reactions: It eats the garden, but it makes the kids happy. I suppose that's a trade I can live with.

entries.intro: Found where {mob_type} have been rubbing against the corner of the house. The plaster is worn down to the stone.
details: There's a smooth patch about knee-height where they've been leaning. Fur stuck in the rougher spots.
That plaster was new last spring. It won't be cheap to redo.
reactions: I'm half tempted to put out a scratching post and spare the house.

statCheck.pass: They rub to shed their undercoat in warm weather. Offering a rough-barked log nearby gives them a preferred scratching spot away from the house.
statCheck.fail: I can't stop them from rubbing the house without standing guard all night.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} ate the seeds out of every sunflower head in the garden. Surgical precision.
details: The stems are still standing, the petals are still there. Just the centers: picked clean.
It must've taken hours. Every single head, emptied.
reactions: I grew those for seed stock. Now I'm buying seeds instead of harvesting them.

statCheck.pass: They time the harvest by watching the seed heads droop: drooping means ripe. Cut the heads a day before they droop fully and you'll beat them to it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how they knew exactly when to hit. Perfect timing, every time.

entries.intro: I've noticed {mob_type} won't go near the area where we dry hides. Something about the smell.
details: The tanning smell clears out about everything with a nose, to be fair.
But the result is a {mob_type}-free zone around the drying racks. Works for me.
reactions: Smells terrible for us too, but at least something good comes of it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} dropped a nut on my head from the roof while I was walking underneath. Right on the crown.
details: I looked up and it was staring down at me. I swear it was deliberate.
It had a whole row of nuts lined up on the gutter. An arsenal.
reactions: I'm wearing a hat outside now. Not because of the weather.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got tangled in the washing that was hanging out and dragged a whole sheet across the yard.
details: By the time I got outside, there was a bedsheet trailing into the bushes and no {mob_type} in sight.
The sheet had claw holes and mud stains. Beyond saving.
reactions: That was the good sheet. We're down to the threadbare one now.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has moved into the old boot I left by the back step. The boot is now a home.
details: I went to put it on and felt resistance, then warmth, then a face looking back at me.
The boot's been there long enough that I think the {wildlife_type} has a stronger claim.
reactions: I conceded the boot. I have other shoes.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} scratched the paint off the front door. Three deep grooves right through to the wood.
details: I'd just painted it last month. First coat of color that door's had in years.
The scratches are at the exact height where the handle is. They were trying to get in.
reactions: I'm not painting the door again until they lose interest. Could be a while.

statCheck.pass: The scratching at handle height means they're attracted to the hand oils on the wood. Wiping the door down with strong soap removes the scent that draws them.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they want with my door. It's not food.

entries.intro: I've grown fond of the {wildlife_type} that visits the garden. I'd miss it if it stopped coming.
details: It eats a bit, I grumble a bit, and we both go about our days. It's become a rhythm.
It showed up limping once and I actually worried about it. That's when I knew I was in trouble.
reactions: It's not a pet. But it's not exactly a stranger anymore either.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pulled up every single stake in the bean row. Yanked them right out of the ground.
details: The beans were just starting to climb, too. A week of growth, gone overnight.
I found the stakes scattered across the yard like someone had a tantrum.
reactions: I'll replant. But I'll be grumbling about it the whole time.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been rolling in the herb bed every morning. The lavender is flat.
details: It lies there on its back, kicking its legs, grinding the plants into the dirt.
The sage survived, but the lavender is done for. Crushed into nothing.
reactions: I'd move the herb bed, but I planted it there for the sun. The creature has good taste in napping spots.

entries.intro: Found {mob_type} nesting material stuffed into the gutter. The whole drainage is blocked.
details: Every time it rained this week the water poured off the edge instead of through the downspout.
I pulled out enough fur and leaves to fill a bucket.
reactions: Cleaning gutters wasn't on my list this week, but here we are.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been lining up pebbles on the windowsill. Neat little rows.
details: I cleared them off yesterday. This morning: new pebbles, same arrangement.
My neighbor thinks it's building something. I think it just likes the sill.
reactions: It's not doing any harm. I've stopped clearing them off.

entries.intro: {mob_type} chewed clean through the handle of the well crank. Snapped it in two.
details: Nobody could draw water until we lashed a branch on as a replacement.
The replacement works, but it wobbles. Proper fix will take a new handle.
reactions: Of all the things to chew, they picked the one thing everyone uses every day.

statCheck.pass: They target well cranks because the wood absorbs moisture and salt from hands. Oiling the handle with something bitter keeps them off it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what drew them to the crank. Bad luck, probably.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has taken to following the cat around the yard. The cat pretends not to notice.
details: It walks about two paces behind, matching the cat's speed exactly.
The cat sits, it sits. The cat moves, it moves: they do this for an hour every morning.
reactions: I'm not sure which of them is more stubborn.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} pried the lid off the salting barrel and ruined a week's worth of cured meat.
details: The lid was weighted with a stone. They knocked the stone off first, then worked the lid.
The brine was all over the floor and the meat was dragged halfway across the room.
reactions: A week of curing, gone in one night. That stings.

statCheck.pass: They can smell brine through wood. A barrel with a locking clasp instead of a weighted lid is the only thing that stops them consistently.
statCheck.fail: I thought the weighted lid would hold. I was wrong.

entries.intro: I watched a {wildlife_type} spend ten minutes trying to eat a pinecone. Just gnawing at it with no progress.
details: It turned it over, tried different angles, dropped it, picked it back up. Fully committed.
Eventually it gave up and kicked the pinecone into the bushes. Very deliberate kick.
reactions: I respected the effort, if not the outcome.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} dug under the smokehouse wall and tunneled straight to the hanging rack.
details: The tunnel was narrow but deep: they knew exactly what they were heading for.
I found the hole in the morning. The meat was gone, and the tunnel was already being backfilled with loose dirt.
reactions: I'm putting flat stones under the smokehouse walls. Should've done it when we built the thing.

statCheck.pass: They dig in soft soil next to foundation edges where the ground is loosest. Packing gravel along the base makes digging too difficult to be worth the effort.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how to stop something that digs. Short of a stone floor, I'm out of ideas.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} figured out how to unlatch the garden gate. I watched it do it.
details: It pushed up with its nose, the latch lifted, and it nudged the gate open. Walked right in.
I've been blaming the wind for the open gate for weeks. It was never the wind.
reactions: I need a latch it can't reach, or a smarter fence.

entries.intro: {mob_type} knocked over the entire stack of drying firewood. It was leaning on the house wall.
details: The crash woke up the whole house. Logs rolling everywhere in the dark.
It took most of the morning to restack. Some of the logs rolled into the mud and have to dry again.
reactions: I'm building a proper woodshed. Should've done it years ago.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the bread dough I left rising on the counter. There were little footprints all through it.
details: It must've walked across it, realized it was sticky, and panicked. Dough tracks everywhere.
The dough had fur in it by the time I found it. Couldn't salvage any of it.
reactions: I'm covering the dough from now on. Lesson learned the hard way.

entries.intro: Found where some {mob_type} have been sharpening their claws on the fence corner post. It's half the thickness it was.
details: Deep vertical grooves all the way down. The post is barely holding together.
That's the corner post: the one that keeps the whole fence line taut. If it goes, the fence goes.
reactions: I'm replacing it with stone. They can sharpen their claws on someone else's property.

statCheck.pass: They prefer soft, weathered wood for claw maintenance. Treating the post with pine tar makes the surface too sticky and they'll find something else.
statCheck.fail: I can't guard a fence post all night. I just have to replace it and hope.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been bringing its food to my porch to eat. Drops whatever it caught and has a meal right there.
details: There are little bones and scraps left on the boards every morning.
It's picked the same spot each time: the corner where the porch gets afternoon sun.
reactions: I sweep it off and it brings more. We have a routine now, whether I like it or not.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} tipped over the outhouse door while someone was inside. I won't say who.
details: The screaming was heard from three houses away. Nobody helped because everyone was laughing.
The door went face-down in the mud. The person inside had to climb out the top.
reactions: We fixed the door and we don't talk about it. That's the agreement.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been stealing kindling from the pile by the back door. One stick at a time.
details: I've watched it make six trips in a single afternoon. Picks the driest stick each time.
My kindling pile is half what it was a week ago.
reactions: It's building something. I'm funding the materials.

entries.intro: {mob_type} got into the grain store and left tracks through every bin. Ate some, spoiled the rest.
details: They knocked bins over, scattered grain across the floor, and walked through it.
The grain they didn't eat is full of droppings now. Can't use it.
reactions: That was a month's supply. I'm rationing until the next delivery.

statCheck.pass: They only revisit a grain store once they know it's accessible. If you secure it completely after the first break-in, they move on within a few days.
statCheck.fail: Once they find a food source, getting them to forget about it is the hard part.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} fell off the roof and landed in the water bucket. Splashed water everywhere and ran off soaked.
details: I heard the thud, then a splash, then scrambling claws on cobblestone. By the time I looked, it was gone.
The bucket was knocked over and there was a wet trail leading into the hedge.
reactions: I hope it's okay. It looked ridiculous, but that was a long fall.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} ripped out the scarecrow and dragged it into the field. Found it face-down in the ditch.
details: The clothes were torn off and scattered. Just the stick frame left.
I spent an afternoon building that scarecrow. It lasted two days.
reactions: If the scarecrow can't scare them, I'm not sure what will.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that drinks from the same puddle outside my door every morning. Particular about its puddle.
details: I've seen it walk past three other puddles to reach that one.
When the puddle dries up after a few days, it stands at the spot looking confused.
reactions: I caught myself filling the puddle with water from a bucket. I don't know what's happening to me.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got into the coal store and tracked black prints through the house. Every room.
details: The prints go from the back door, through the kitchen, across the bedroom floor, and back out again.
I've been scrubbing for two days and the stains are still there.
reactions: Black footprints on white plaster. It's almost artistic, if you squint.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} tried to carry off a whole cabbage head this morning. It was bigger than the creature.
details: It got about five paces before tipping over sideways. The cabbage rolled back toward the garden.
It tried again, tipped again, and sat there staring at the cabbage like it was the cabbage's fault.
reactions: I lost a cabbage leaf in the struggle. Could've been worse.

entries.intro: {mob_type} bent the iron hinges on the cellar door: not broken, bent, and that takes force.
details: The door still closes, but it doesn't sit flush anymore. Cold air gets in.
I need a blacksmith to straighten the hinges. Can't do it with a hammer at home.
reactions: Iron hinges, bent: I don't even want to think about what they were after down there.

statCheck.pass: Cellars trap cool air that carries food scents upward. The gap under the door is like a chimney of smells to them. Sealing the gap kills the scent trail.
statCheck.fail: Something in the cellar is drawing them. I just don't know what.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been pecking at its reflection in the copper pot I leave outside. All day long.
details: Peck, peck, peck: from dawn until I bring the pot in. The surface is covered in little dents now.
I turned the pot over. It found the bottom, which is also reflective.
reactions: The pot's ruined for cooking. It's a creature mirror now.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} pulled the rope out of the pulley on the well. The whole length, coiled up in the grass.
details: Must've tugged on the loose end and kept pulling until the whole thing came free.
The bucket was at the bottom of the well with no way to retrieve it.
reactions: Took an hour to fish the bucket out with a hook. A terrible way to start a morning.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been stashing berries in my boots. I found out the hard way.
details: Crushed berries between my toes first thing in the morning. Purple stains on my only good socks.
It's not just one boot: it fills both. Equal portions in each.
reactions: I keep my boots inside now. The creature can find its own storage.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} knocked down the clothesline poles. Both of them, snapped at the base.
details: The poles were sunk two feet deep and braced with stones. Still went down.
All the laundry ended up in the garden dirt.
reactions: I'll sink them deeper this time. Maybe three feet. Maybe four.

entries.intro: I think the {wildlife_type} that visits the yard has learned my schedule. It shows up exactly when I eat breakfast.
details: I ate early one day to test it. The creature was there early too.
It sits just close enough to be noticed. Far enough to run if I stand up.
reactions: It doesn't beg. It just watches. Somehow that's worse.

entries.intro: {mob_type} shredded the tarp covering the hay bales. Not a scrap of it left in one piece.
details: The hay underneath was soaked from rain since the tarp was destroyed. Ruined.
That tarp was oiled canvas: not cheap and not easy to replace.
reactions: Wet hay, no tarp, and a week of rain ahead. Perfect timing.

statCheck.pass: Oiled canvas has a scent that attracts them: the oil mixture smells like something edible. Untreated canvas doesn't draw the same attention.
statCheck.fail: I'll get another tarp. Not much else I can do.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps sitting on the garden fence and screaming at the cat. Every single afternoon.
details: The cat sits on one side, the creature on the other. They just yell at each other.
It goes on for about twenty minutes, then they both leave. Like they had an appointment.
reactions: I've gotten used to the noise. Three o'clock: screaming time.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} got into the herb drying rack and pulled everything down. My whole harvest for the season.
details: Bundles of dried herbs scattered across the floor. Half of them too trampled to use.
The rack itself is fine: they just wanted what was on it.
reactions: Months of growing, weeks of drying, and one night of {mob_type}. That's the math.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} dug up the onion sets I planted yesterday. Every single one, lined up neatly on the soil surface.
details: It didn't eat them. Just unearthed each one and left it sitting on top of the dirt.
Like it was doing quality control on my planting depth.
reactions: I replanted them. We'll see if they get inspected again tomorrow.

entries.intro: Found {mob_type} have been using the water trough as a bathtub. The livestock won't drink from it now.
details: The water was murky, with fur floating on the surface. Took a full scrub to clean it.
Refilled it with fresh water. By morning it was murky again.
reactions: I'm hauling buckets to the livestock until I figure out how to keep them out of the trough.

statCheck.pass: They bathe to cool down and to shed parasites. A shallow pan of water away from the livestock gives them an alternative and they'll usually prefer it.
statCheck.fail: I can't fence the trough without making it hard for the livestock too.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} chewed through the leather strap on the gate and it swung into the road.
details: A cart had to swerve around it. The driver wasn't pleased.
That strap was new. Lasted exactly four days.
reactions: I'm using rope now. If it chews through rope too, I'm using chain.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} seem to have moved their regular path closer to the settlement. Tracks are appearing where I've never seen them.
details: Used to be the tracks stopped well before the outer gardens. Now they're right up against the fences.
It's not one set of tracks either: it's many, same route, night after night.
reactions: We're keeping closer watch. Not panicking: just paying attention.

statCheck.pass: When the ground outside dries up in warm months, they shift toward settlements because of the water sources. It's seasonal, not a change in behavior.
statCheck.fail: Could be anything making them come closer. I try not to overthink it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been peeling bark off the fruit trees. Long strips, all around the trunk.
details: Three trees so far. The bark comes off in curls and the exposed wood dries out in the sun.
If it gets all the way around, the tree will die. Two of them are close.
reactions: I'm wrapping the trunks with burlap. It's not elegant but it might save them.

statCheck.pass: They eat the soft inner bark for the sugars in it: it peaks in spring when the sap runs. Wrapping trunks from late winter through spring covers the vulnerable period.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it targets the fruit trees and leaves everything else alone.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pushed their way into the chicken run through the feeding hatch. The one that's barely big enough for my hand.
details: The hatch is bent outward now: they squeezed through something half their size.
The chickens are fine, just shaken. The hatch needs complete replacement.
reactions: I underestimated how much they can compress themselves. Noted.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} stole the rag I use to clean the windows. Pulled it right off the sill.
details: I saw it disappear into the hedge with the rag trailing behind it like a flag.
Found the rag two days later, shredded and woven into a nest. Good use of materials, I suppose.
reactions: I'd be angry if it weren't so resourceful.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} knocked over the beehive frames I'd set out to air. Honey and wax everywhere.
details: The bees were furious. I got stung four times just trying to recover the frames.
The {mob_type} tracks lead away at a run. Even they don't stick around for angry bees.
reactions: The bees are the real victims here. Their whole home was upended.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been sleeping in the vegetable crate I use to carry produce. Every time I need it, occupied.
details: I tried putting it on its side so it's less comfortable. It sleeps on its side too.
It's the perfect size for it: just the right fit to curl up.
reactions: I bought a second crate. One for produce, one for the creature.

entries.intro: {mob_type} got into the root cellar through the ventilation gap and ate every last carrot.
details: The gap was barely a hand's width. I never thought anything could fit through it.
Carrot tops scattered across the floor. They ate the roots and left the greens.
reactions: I've screened the vent with wire mesh now. Should've been there from the start.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been sitting on top of the scarecrow every afternoon. Using it as a lookout.
details: It surveys the garden from up there, spots what it wants, and hops down to eat it.
The scarecrow has become a dining platform. The opposite of its purpose.
reactions: I built the scarecrow to keep creatures out. Instead I built them a watchtower.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} overturned the ash bin and scattered gray dust across the entire porch.
details: Ash gets into everything: the door frame, the boots, the cracks in the boards.
I swept for an hour and the porch is still gray.
reactions: It'll take a good rain to truly clean it. Until then, gray porch.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been marking territory on the corner of the house. The wall smells awful.
details: I've scrubbed it three times. The smell fades for a day, then comes back.
I think it re-marks as soon as I clean it. We're in a cycle.
reactions: I'm losing this war of persistence. The creature has more patience than I do.

statCheck.pass: Vinegar neutralizes the scent compounds they use for marking. A thorough vinegar wash breaks the cycle because they can't detect the old mark.
statCheck.fail: I've tried everything I can think of. The smell persists.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} turned over the seed trays I had germinating in the cold frame. Soil everywhere, seedlings ruined.
details: The glass pane on the cold frame was pushed aside: they got in from the top.
Those seedlings were three weeks along. I'm starting over from scratch.
reactions: Three weeks of careful watering, undone overnight. Gardening here takes patience.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps digging up the stepping stones in the garden path. Flips them over to get at the bugs underneath.
details: Every stone, flipped neatly onto its face. Like it's doing an inspection.
I put the stones back every morning. They're flipped again by evening.
reactions: At least the bug population under my path is well managed.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} found the compost pile and have been sleeping in it. The warmth draws them.
details: Every morning there are body-shaped impressions in the compost. Sometimes two or three.
The pile is getting flattened instead of breaking down properly.
reactions: My compost heap has become a bed. I just want it to rot in peace.

statCheck.pass: Decomposing compost generates heat from the inside out: they bed down because it's warm and the smell masks their own scent from predators. Fencing the pile solves it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they love the compost pile so much. It smells terrible to me.

entries.intro: I left the shutters open overnight and a {wildlife_type} left a pile of acorns on the table. Right in the middle.
details: A neat little pile. Almost arranged. Like a payment for something.
I don't know what it thinks I owe it, or what it thinks it's owed.
reactions: I left the acorns on the windowsill outside. They were gone by morning.

entries.intro: {mob_type} tore the lattice off the side of the porch. Ripped it clean away from the nails.
details: I'd grown climbing beans up that lattice. The plants went down with it.
The nails are still in the wall. The lattice is in pieces in the yard.
reactions: I'll rebuild it. But I'll use heavier wood this time.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the flour sack and left white footprints all over the house. Little ghostly tracks.
details: The tracks went from the pantry to the bedroom, across the blankets, and out the window.
It ate maybe a handful of flour and wasted the rest. The sack was half empty.
reactions: The footprints would be funny if flour wasn't expensive.

entries.intro: We strung bells on the fence line and the {mob_type} haven't crossed it since. Three weeks clean.
details: Every gust of wind rings them. The sound carries well in the quiet.
The neighbors complained about the bells at first, but the results speak for themselves.
reactions: I'll take annoying bells over missing produce any night.

statCheck.pass: It's not the sound: it's the unpredictability. They can't tell wind-bells from alarm-bells, so they avoid the risk entirely.
statCheck.fail: Could be the bells, could be coincidence. Either way, I'm leaving them up.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been hoarding chestnuts inside the empty barrel. I found over a hundred in there.
details: Layer after layer, packed tight. Like it's been at it for weeks.
I need the barrel, but I feel bad emptying someone's pantry.
reactions: I moved the chestnuts into a box nearby. I hope it finds them.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been gnawing the foundation timbers under the house. I can hear them at night.
details: The sound carries through the floor: a slow, steady scraping.
I checked under the crawl space and there are tooth marks on three beams.
reactions: Foundation repairs are expensive and I'm pretending I don't know about this yet.

statCheck.pass: They gnaw foundation timbers to wear down their teeth: the old, damp wood is soft enough to be comfortable. Coating the beams with a clay-and-ash paste hardens the surface enough to deter them.
statCheck.fail: I have no idea how to stop something from chewing the underside of my house.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} managed to unravel the entire ball of twine I left on the workbench. Wrapped it around everything.
details: The bench legs, the tools, the chair: all connected by a web of twine.
It must've batted the ball around for an hour to create that much chaos.
reactions: I spent longer untangling twine than I would've spent on the project I needed it for.

entries.intro: {mob_type} trampled the rhubarb patch flat overnight. Not a single stalk left standing.
details: There were tracks going through it in every direction: they walked back and forth for no clear reason.
The rhubarb was almost ready to harvest. Another week and I'd have had it.
reactions: The roots might survive. I'll know in a few weeks.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been digging up the gravel walkway. Little holes every few feet.
details: It digs, sniffs, fills it back in, and moves to the next spot. Very systematic.
The walkway looks like it has pockmarks. My neat gravel path is a mess.
reactions: Whatever it's looking for under the gravel, I hope it finds it soon.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} discovered the salt block I set out for the goats and licked it down to nothing in one night.
details: A salt block that was supposed to last two months: gone.
The goats stood at the empty spot this morning looking betrayed.
reactions: I'm putting the next one inside the pen where the gate latches. A dedicated salt fortress.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} built a dam in the drainage ditch and flooded the lower garden beds.
details: Sticks, mud, leaves: packed tight enough to hold back a surprising amount of water.
The lettuce was underwater by morning. Most of it will rot.
reactions: I cleared the dam. The creature was back at it before sunset. We're at an impasse.

statCheck.pass: It's damming for the pool habitat, not to annoy you. If there's a low spot nearby you can dig out, it'll dam that instead and leave the ditch alone.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand the motivation. There's a whole stream nearby, but it wants my ditch.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have started digging at the corners of the foundation. I keep filling the holes, they keep digging.
details: Four holes, one at each corner. Filled them yesterday: they were dug out again by dawn.
The holes are getting deeper each time. They're committed.
reactions: I'm packing the holes with stone rubble now. Good luck digging through that.

statCheck.pass: They dig at corners because that's where the soil is loosest: rain runoff from the roof softens the ground there. Grading the soil so water flows away from the corners makes it too packed to dig.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they always pick the corners. Some kind of instinct.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been eating the wax seal off every jar on the pantry shelf. Just the wax.
details: The jars are unsealed now: the preserves inside will spoil if I don't re-seal them.
It chewed through eight seals. Methodical: went jar by jar.
reactions: I'm keeping the pantry door shut and the window latched. Simple solution I should've started with.

entries.intro: We haven't had a single {mob_type} incident since the cooper's dog had puppies. Six of them, all barkers.
details: Six puppies making noise at all hours. Annoying for us: apparently terrifying for them.
The tracks stop well clear of the settlement now. Puppy perimeter.
reactions: The cooper wants to sell the puppies. I'm hoping he keeps at least four.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} chewed the bristles off the broom I leave by the door. Down to the stub.
details: I didn't notice until I tried to sweep and just scraped wood across the floor.
The bristles are scattered across the yard. Must've pulled them out one by one.
reactions: A broom without bristles is a stick. I need a new broom.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} used the garden as a thoroughfare last night and flattened everything in a straight line across it.
details: A perfect path of destruction: trampled plants from one side of the garden to the other.
They didn't stop to eat anything. Just walked through like the fence wasn't there.
reactions: At least they were efficient about it.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that waits on the fence until I turn my back, then drops into the garden. Every time.
details: If I look at it, it freezes. The second I turn around: thump, it's in the beans.
I tried watching it through a gap in the shutters. It waited me out. More patient than me.
reactions: It knows the game better than I do.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pushed a boulder into the irrigation channel and blocked the flow. The lower fields dried up.
details: It took three of us and a lever to move the stone back. The channel had already backed up and overflowed upstream.
The lower crops missed two days of water. Some of them won't recover.
reactions: One stone, one night, and a week's worth of crop damage. That's the kind of math I hate.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has started bathing in the horse trough at dawn. Splashes water everywhere and leaves feathers floating.
details: The horses just stand there watching it. I think they're fascinated.
The trough water is half gone by the time the creature finishes. Big bather.
reactions: I refill the trough and it happens again the next morning. Part of the routine now.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} ate through the door of the cold store. The actual door: chewed right through the planks.
details: There's a hole big enough to put my head through. They ate around the latch.
Everything inside was ransacked. Cheese, butter, dried fruit: all gone.
reactions: I need an iron-braced door for a cold store now. That's where I am in life.

statCheck.pass: They chew around latches because the latch metal stops them: they just go around the obstacle. An iron plate behind the latch covers the weak spot.
statCheck.fail: How do you defend a door against something that eats wood? I'm asking seriously.

entries.intro: I watched a {wildlife_type} try to bury a walnut in the flower pot on my windowsill. The pot is full of flowers.
details: It dug up a pansy, dropped the walnut in, and patted the soil back down.
The pansy is now lying on the sill. The walnut has replaced it.
reactions: I put the pansy back and moved the walnut. I imagine this will happen again.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} dug a pit right in the middle of the path to the well. Deep enough to turn an ankle.
details: I found it the hard way: stepped in it while carrying a bucket in the dark.
Filled it with packed earth and tamped it down. There's a new pit next to it this morning.
reactions: I walk to the well with a lantern now. Trust nothing in the dark.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has made itself comfortable in the abandoned wasp nest under the eaves. Moved right in.
details: The wasps are gone: the creature is there now, poking its head out where they used to fly.
It's actually kind of cozy-looking in there. Dry, sheltered, good view of the garden.
reactions: Better a {wildlife_type} than wasps. Significant upgrade, honestly.

entries.intro: {mob_type} have been rolling the loose stones off the garden wall. One by one, down the slope.
details: I stacked them back up three times this week. The wall is shorter every time I look.
They don't seem to eat the stones: they just push them off and move on.
reactions: I'm going to mortar the wall. End the conversation permanently.

statCheck.pass: Loose stones trap warmth from the sun: they roll them off to lie on the warm ground underneath. Mortar solves the problem because there's nothing left to move.
statCheck.fail: I've given up trying to understand them. Mortar it is.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} ate every single strawberry the morning they ripened. It was watching them closer than I was.
details: I checked the evening before: green. Went out at dawn to pick and: nothing. Just stems.
There were red stains on the leaves. Fresh. It couldn't have been more than an hour ahead of me.
reactions: I've been out-timed by a creature with a better eye for ripeness than mine.

entries.intro: Found a family of {mob_type} had bedded down inside the empty cart. Straw everywhere, fur everywhere.
details: They'd packed the bed of the cart with leaves and grass. Made it homey.
The cart smells now. That deep, musky smell that doesn't come out easily.
reactions: I chased them off and aired the cart for three days. Still smells a bit.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the chicken coop and fell asleep between two hens. All three were napping together.
details: I opened the coop and there it was, nestled in the straw with a hen on each side.
The hens didn't seem bothered. Just shuffled over to make room.
reactions: I closed the door and let them have their nap. Some things are too peaceful to disturb.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been leaving scratch marks on the shutters. Deep ones, right where the latch is.
details: Every morning: fresh marks. They work at it while we sleep.
The wood around the latch is getting thin. A few more nights and they'll have it.
reactions: I've bolted a metal plate over the latch. That should end it.

statCheck.pass: They scratch at latches because they've learned latches are weak points in barriers. They're not mindless: they're testing the hardware.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if they understand what a latch is or if they just scratch at edges.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been gathering dandelion fluff and stuffing it into the crack in the wall.
details: It pulls the fluff off the stems, flies over to the crack, pushes it in, and goes back for more.
The crack is now completely packed with white fluff. Better insulated than it was.
reactions: It's not the repair method I would've chosen, but it's sealed the draft.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got into the charcoal supply and spread it across the yard. Everything is black.
details: Black paw prints on the steps, the wall, the water barrel. A charcoal crime scene.
The charcoal itself is ruined: broken into dust and mixed with dirt.
reactions: I'll be making more charcoal this week. Wasn't in the plan, but here I am.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been leaving half-eaten fruit on the doorstep every morning. Always exactly one piece.
details: Always taken a few bites and then left there. Like it's paying rent.
The fruit changes: sometimes berries, sometimes something I can't identify.
reactions: I don't know what the arrangement is, but I haven't broken it.

entries.intro: {mob_type} scraped the whitewash off the entire lower half of the back wall. Down to bare stone.
details: I can see the rubbing marks: they lean against it and shuffle sideways.
The whitewash took a full day to apply. They removed it in one night.
reactions: I'm not repainting until they stop using my house as a scratching post.

entries.intro: I left a bucket of nails outside overnight and a {wildlife_type} replaced them all with acorns.
details: The nails were gone and there was a bucket full of acorns in their place. Exact same bucket.
I found the nails later, scattered in the garden. But the acorns were neatly stacked.
reactions: I'm not even angry. I'm impressed by the organizational skills.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} dug up the drainage ditch I'd just finished and caved the sides in. Two days of work.
details: The ditch was supposed to keep the lower garden from flooding. Now it's a mud trench.
They dug across it in several spots: crosswise, like they were making their own paths through it.
reactions: I'll dig it again. And line it with stones this time so the sides hold.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps trying to nest in the chimney. I find fresh twigs on the hearth every morning.
details: It drops sticks down the flue all night. I hear them clattering as they fall.
I've pulled out enough nesting material to fill a bushel basket.
reactions: I need a chimney cap. The creature needs a different chimney.

entries.intro: The goats are calmer since the {mob_type} stopped coming around at night. First peaceful week in a while.
details: No pacing, no bleating at three in the morning. They're eating better too.
Whatever scared the {mob_type} off, I hope it stays.
reactions: Peaceful goats make for a peaceful household. I'll take it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the pantry and nested inside the large mixing bowl. Wouldn't leave.
details: I needed the bowl for dinner. It needed the bowl for a nap. Standoff.
It hissed when I tried to pick up the bowl. Tiny hiss, but it meant business.
reactions: I used a different bowl. The creature won the negotiation.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} tore the weathervane off the roof. Found it bent in the garden.
details: The mounting bracket is still up there, twisted sideways. They must've climbed.
The vane itself is mangled. The arrow points in every direction now.
reactions: I haven't needed the weathervane in years, but I liked having it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} discovered the bell on the garden gate and won't stop ringing it. Pushes the gate, hears the bell, pushes again.
details: It's been at it since sunrise. Push, ding, pause, push, ding.
I thought someone was at the gate the first four times. Ran out each time.
reactions: I took the bell off. The creature looked disappointed.

entries.intro: {mob_type} upended the composter and spread rotting scraps across the walkway. The smell is something else.
details: Potato peels, eggshells, and things I'd rather not identify: all baking in the sun on the path.
They picked through what they wanted and left everything else for me to deal with.
reactions: Cleaning up decomposing food in the afternoon heat. The glamorous life.

entries.intro: I think the {wildlife_type} that visits my yard has started recognizing me. It doesn't run when I come out anymore.
details: It used to bolt at the door opening. Now it just looks up and keeps eating.
We've developed a working distance: about four paces. Closer than that and it shuffles away.
reactions: I'm not sure if being recognized by garden pests is an achievement or a problem.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} broke the handle off the water pump outside. Snapped it like a twig.
details: The pump is useless without the handle. We're hauling from the well until it's fixed.
The handle was iron-banded oak. Whatever hit it was serious.
reactions: Another thing on the repair list. The list is getting long.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} built a nest inside the empty watering can. I went to use it and got a face full of angry creature.
details: It shot out of the spout and went straight for my hat. Knocked it clean off.
The inside of the watering can is now lined with feathers and grass. Professional work.
reactions: I'll use a different watering can. That one belongs to someone else now.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have figured out that the back gate has a weak spot at the bottom. They've widened it.
details: What was a small gap is now big enough for them to squeeze under comfortably.
I wedged a board in the gap. They pulled the board out.
reactions: I'm replacing the bottom rail with something they can't pry loose. This ends here.

statCheck.pass: They work at weak spots because they can smell what's on the other side. If the gap transmits food scent, they'll keep trying. Blocking the airflow stops the incentive.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how they find the weak spots every time. Trial and error, I guess.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been using the flat stone by the door to crack snail shells. All day long.
details: Crack, eat, crack, eat. The stone is covered in shell fragments.
There's a little pile of empty shells next to the stone. Like a midden heap.
reactions: It's keeping the snails away from the garden, so I can't really complain.

entries.intro: {mob_type} knocked over the lamp post at the path junction. The lamp smashed on the cobbles.
details: Oil from the lamp soaked into the stones. It's a slick patch now on top of being dark.
That lamp was the only light on that stretch. Now it's black after sundown.
reactions: Walking that path at night now means going slow and hoping for the best.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been pulling moss off the garden wall and carrying it away. The wall looks patchy.
details: It takes a beak full of moss, flies off, comes back for more. Constant trips.
The wall used to be uniformly green. Now it's green and gray in patches.
reactions: The wall looks worse, but the creature's nest must be wonderfully soft.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have started coming earlier in the evening. I can see them before the light's even fully gone.
details: Used to be they waited for full dark. Now they're out at dusk, bold as anything.
I've moved my evening chores earlier to avoid them. They're setting my schedule.
reactions: I refuse to let them dictate my routine. But I am adjusting it slightly.

statCheck.pass: Earlier emergence usually means the population is growing: more mouths, more pressure to find food sooner. The bolder they get, the more there are.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they're showing up earlier. Maybe they're getting braver.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} ate the cork out of the rain gauge and the water all drained out overnight.
details: I've been tracking rainfall for two months. That night's measurement is just gone.
The cork had teeth marks all around it. Must've seemed interesting.
reactions: I'll get a new cork. And keep the gauge where small creatures can't reach it.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} found the loose board on the back of the shed and have been coming and going through it.
details: I can see the traffic pattern in the dust inside: in through the gap, circle the room, out again.
They haven't taken anything. Just walking through like it's a shortcut.
reactions: I nailed the board down. They'll have to find a different route.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} knocked the lid off the rain barrel and a whole family of frogs moved in.
details: The creature was probably after a drink, but the frogs got a home out of it.
Now I've got a barrel full of frogs and no rain water.
reactions: I moved the frogs to the pond. They were back in the barrel by evening.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} left claw marks on the oak door to the {poi_type}. People are rattled.
details: Deep gouges, head-height. They were reaching for the handle or just scratching.
Somebody carved them deeper to make the story better. I know because the original marks were lower.
reactions: The actual marks weren't that bad. The gossip made them worse.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps hiding behind the woodpile and jumping out when I walk past. Gets me every single time.
details: I know it's there. I tell myself it's there. I still jump when it bursts out.
It runs five paces, stops, and looks back at me. I think it enjoys this.
reactions: My heart can't take much more of this. But I'm not rearranging the woodpile.

entries.intro: {mob_type} destroyed the cold frame by stepping right through the glass. Just walked across it.
details: Both panes, shattered. The seedlings underneath are full of broken glass now.
I'll have to start the seedlings over. The glass itself isn't cheap either.
reactions: I can't protect glass from something that doesn't know glass exists.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} stole the handle off the hand crank for the butter churn. Ran off with it in its mouth.
details: The handle was wood with a brass fitting. I think it liked the shine.
I found the brass fitting two days later by the hedge. No sign of the wood part.
reactions: Churning butter with a replacement handle that doesn't quite fit. Every rotation wobbles.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} tracked something sticky through the house. I still don't know what it was.
details: The prints are tacky and yellowish. Every step left a mark on the floorboards.
I've scrubbed the floor twice and the spots still feel gummy.
reactions: I'm choosing not to investigate what the substance was. Some questions are better left unanswered.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been visiting the garden so regularly that the cat has stopped chasing it. They just coexist.
details: The creature eats on one side, the cat naps on the other. No conflict.
They've apparently negotiated a peace I wasn't part of.
reactions: If the cat's given up, what chance do I have?

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} toppled the stone wall along the east garden. Just pushed the top course of stones right off.
details: Stones rolled downhill into the garden beds. Some of the squash is crushed.
Restacking a dry stone wall is slow, careful work. A full afternoon, at least.
reactions: I built that wall stone by stone. They un-built it in one pass.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} ate the string off the seed packets hanging in the shed. All of them, just the string.
details: The packets fell on the floor and the seeds mixed together. I don't know what's what anymore.
I'll be planting mystery gardens this season.
reactions: Could be interesting. Could also be a row of weeds next to the wrong companions. We'll see.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} pushed through the hedge so many times there's a permanent gap now. Might as well be a gate.
details: The branches are broken and bent aside. Anything can walk through now, not just them.
I've been stuffing thorny cuttings into the gap. Slows them down but doesn't stop them.
reactions: I'll plant something thornier. Long-term solution for a long-term problem.

statCheck.pass: Once they establish a gap, they'll maintain it: pushing through any repair that isn't solid. The only permanent fix is a post and rail insert in the gap with the hedge growing around it.
statCheck.fail: I've tried blocking the gap every way I know. They find a way through every time.
