entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that has been eating mud from the same spot by the foundation wall. Just licking the dirt.
details: It's dug a shallow depression from all the licking. Comes back to the same spot daily.
I've watched it for a week now. Same time, same patch of dirt.
reactions: Whatever minerals are in that dirt, the creature is committed to the diet.

statCheck.pass: Mineral licks form where water seeps through certain soil and deposits salts near the surface. That spot probably has trace minerals it can't get elsewhere.
statCheck.fail: I've got no idea why it eats the dirt. Tastes can't be that different from creature to creature, but apparently they can.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pulled the rope bridge handrail loose where it crosses the ditch. Nobody noticed until someone grabbed it.
details: The rope held just enough to look fine, then gave way when weight hit it.
The person caught themselves. Could've been a bad fall into the ditch.
reactions: We re-tied the ropes properly. Double-knotted this time.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has started leaving earthworms on the doorstep. Just a pile of worms, neatly arranged.
details: Three or four worms each morning, placed right in front of the door.
They're still alive and wriggling. Freshly collected.
reactions: I've started putting them in the garden. The creature provides: the garden benefits.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been chewing the edges of the wooden water channel. The whole thing is going to leak soon.
details: The chew marks are concentrated where the wood stays damp. It's softening and crumbling.
If the channel fails, the garden loses its only reliable water source.
reactions: I'm lining the channel with clay. Should protect the wood and keep the water clean.

statCheck.pass: Damp, weathered wood is easier to chew and provides moisture at the same time: they're drinking and gnawing in one motion. A clay lining removes both incentives.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they target the water channel specifically. Maybe the wet wood tastes different.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been collecting feathers and arranging them on the fence post. A little fan shape.
details: Different colors, different sizes: all stuck into the cracks in the post.
It adds a new one every few days. The collection is growing.
reactions: I've started looking forward to seeing what new feather appears. Terrible hobby on my part.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} knocked over the stone trough and cracked it. Water's been pooling in the yard since.
details: The crack runs the whole length. Patching it might hold, but the trough is weakened.
The pooling water is turning the yard to mud.
reactions: I need a new trough. The cracked one is a puddle maker now.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the seed store and cached half of it underground somewhere. I'll never find it.
details: It must've made fifty trips, cheeks full each time.
That was next season's planting stock. I'll have to buy more.
reactions: Somewhere out there is a fortune in seeds buried by a creature with no farming plans.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} got into the beeswax supply and ate every block. Pure beeswax: who eats that?
details: I use the wax for sealing, waterproofing, and candle-making. All of it, gone.
Tooth marks in the crate where they pried it open.
reactions: Beeswax takes months to accumulate. I'm back to nothing.

statCheck.pass: They eat wax for the trace proteins and stored honey scent trapped in it. Pure wax from hive frames attracts them: rendered wax with the scent boiled out does not.
statCheck.fail: I didn't think anything would eat plain wax. Learning something new every week.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been picking the ripe peas off the vine and eating them one by one. Delicate about it.
details: It selects only the fattest pods. Leaves the skinny ones alone.
I'm getting the runts of the harvest because the creature has better taste than me.
reactions: It's hard to resent something with such refined preferences.

entries.intro: {mob_type} dragged the drying rack into the field and left it on its side. Everything that was on it: scattered.
details: Fish, herbs, strips of leather: all in the grass. Half of it muddy.
The rack itself has a broken leg now. Won't stand level anymore.
reactions: I'll prop it with a stone and be more careful about where I set it up.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been sleeping on the warm chimney stones every evening. Right on top.
details: I can see the silhouette from the yard: curled up on the chimney cap.
When we light the fire, it moves to the edge and stays for the warmth.
reactions: As long as it doesn't fall down the chimney, we're fine.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} chewed through the fence rails at the exact spot where I spliced two boards together. They found the weak point.
details: The splice was invisible to me. They went straight for it.
Two bites and the rail came apart. The whole section sagged.
reactions: I'm using single-length boards from now on. No more splices for them to find.

statCheck.pass: Spliced joints absorb moisture at the seam: the wood there is softer and easier to chew. Sealing the splice with pitch makes it just as hard as the rest of the board.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how they find the weak spots. Maybe the wood smells different where it's joined.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} ate the tallow candle I'd set out on the windowsill to cool. The whole thing, wick and all.
details: I looked out ten minutes later and there was nothing but a grease smear on the sill.
I made that candle yesterday. It lasted less than a day.
reactions: Candles go inside from now on. No more windowsill cooling.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pushed through the garden and trampled every row except the garlic. Stepped around it perfectly.
details: The beans, the lettuce, the squash: flattened. The garlic: not a single leaf bent.
A perfect ring of destruction with a garlic island in the middle.
reactions: Next year I'm planting garlic around everything. Apparently it's the one thing they respect.

statCheck.pass: The sulfur compounds in garlic irritate their paw pads and they can smell it from a distance. Interplanting garlic between rows acts as a natural barrier.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they left the garlic alone. Maybe they just don't like the taste.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps pulling the wash off the lower rungs of the drying frame. Only the lower rungs.
details: Anything within reach gets pulled down and dragged a few paces. Then abandoned.
It doesn't take the clothes. Just removes them from the frame and leaves.
reactions: I'm hanging everything on the top rung now. Inconvenient, but effective.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} left prints on the roof. The actual roof: they climbed up there somehow.
details: Muddy prints going up one side and down the other. Over the ridge beam.
The thatch is pushed down where they walked. A clear trail across the top of the house.
reactions: I don't know how to feel about something walking over my head while I slept.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been scratching at the same spot on the tree stump for days. There's a groove worn into it.
details: Scratch, scratch, scratch: all morning, every morning.
I think there's something under the bark it wants. Or it just likes the sound.
reactions: Either it's got a purpose I can't see, or it's as bored as I am.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} tore the wind chimes down and scattered the pieces across the path.
details: The wooden tubes are cracked, the string is gone, and the striker is nowhere to be found.
My spouse made those wind chimes. They weren't fancy, but they meant something.
reactions: I'll gather the pieces and see if they can be reassembled. Not the same, but close enough.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the knitting basket and tangled itself in the yarn. I found it wrapped up like a present.
details: It was turning in circles trying to free itself, which only wound the yarn tighter.
I had to hold it still and unwind it strand by strand. It bit me twice.
reactions: The yarn is a knotted mess and I've got two bite marks. Nobody won.

entries.intro: Haven't heard {mob_type} around the settlement in weeks. The dogs are relaxed and the chickens are calm.
details: The tracks that used to appear every morning have stopped entirely.
Some folks say they've moved on. I say give it time.
reactions: I'm enjoying the quiet. But the fences stay up.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps sleeping in the exact center of the doormat. Right where I step.
details: I've moved the mat three times. It follows the mat.
It must like the texture, or the spot, or both.
reactions: I check before I step now. It's become a habit: look down, then walk.

entries.intro: {mob_type} got into the workshop and knocked every tool off the wall pegs. The noise woke the whole street.
details: Hammers, saws, chisels: all on the floor in a heap. Some of the edges are nicked from the fall.
The door latch was still closed: they came in through the window I thought was too small.
reactions: Every window gets a screen now. Every single one.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has learned to drink from the spigot on the rain barrel. Turns it with its paws.
details: The first time I thought I'd left it open. The second time I watched it happen.
It doesn't close the spigot when it's done. I keep finding an empty barrel.
reactions: Clever enough to open it, not thoughtful enough to close it. Typical.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} flipped the bench outside the front door. It was bolted down.
details: The bolts pulled right out of the wood. The bench is on its back in the flower bed.
That bench has been there for years without moving. Now it's upside down.
reactions: I'm bolting it into stone this time. They can try.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been stacking small stones on the fence post. One on top of another, like a little cairn.
details: There are five stones now, balanced neatly. I've watched it add the last two.
It's very precise about placement. Adjusts each stone until it's satisfied.
reactions: I've started wondering if it's trying to build something or if it's just passing time.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} dug up the fence post and left it lying flat. The hole they dug is knee-deep.
details: The post was set in packed earth and gravel. They excavated the whole thing.
Without that post, the whole fence section leans. The gate doesn't close right.
reactions: I'm resetting the post deeper. And packing it with stones instead of gravel.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} dragged a whole branch onto the porch and started stripping the bark off it. Made a mess.
details: Bark shavings everywhere: on the steps, in the boots, caught in the doorframe.
It worked at that branch for hours. Thorough creature.
reactions: I swept the bark, moved the branch, and came back to a new branch. We're in a loop.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have learned to avoid the snares. Walk right past them like they're marked on a map.
details: I set six snares last week. Not a single one tripped. But tracks all around them.
They go around, under, or over: never through.
reactions: I'm not outsmarting them with snares. Time for a different approach.

statCheck.pass: They smell the human scent on the rope and the disturbed soil around the trigger. Setting snares with gloves and covering the disturbance with leaf litter removes the warning signs.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how they spot the snares. Maybe they can see the line better than I think.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps hiding seeds in the cracks of the garden wall. Now plants are growing out of the wall.
details: Little green shoots poking out between the stones. The roots will split the mortar eventually.
It's been doing this all season: every crack has something sprouting.
reactions: The wall is slowly becoming a planter. Nature reclaims everything, one seed at a time.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} chewed through the leather lashing on the gate hinge. The gate swung open and the sheep got out.
details: Found three sheep in the neighbor's garden and two more in the road.
Rounding them up took most of the morning. The neighbor wasn't thrilled about the garden.
reactions: Iron lashing this time. Leather is a snack, apparently.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been catching frogs in the garden pond and leaving them on the back step. Alive.
details: The frogs hop away eventually, but they're confused. Sitting on the step looking dazed.
Three frogs this week. The creature seems to think I want them.
reactions: I put them back in the pond. The creature catches them again. It's a frog cycle.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} tore a hole in the canvas tent I use for the market stall. Right through the middle.
details: I patched it, but it looks terrible. The patch shows from thirty paces.
The tent was already old. This might be the excuse I need to get a new one.
reactions: A torn tent at the market doesn't exactly inspire confidence in the merchant.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} managed to knock the lid off the honey pot and fell in. Had to pull it out covered in honey.
details: It was sticky, panicked, and angry. Getting honey off a struggling creature is harder than you'd think.
The honey is contaminated now. Half a pot, wasted.
reactions: The creature ran off sticky. I hope it learned something. I doubt it.

entries.intro: {mob_type} have been gnawing the sign that marks the path to the {poi_type}. The lettering is unreadable now.
details: Half the letters are chewed away. You'd never know where it pointed.
It's the third sign we've put up on that post. They go through them.
reactions: We'll try a stone marker next. Let them chew on that.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got tangled in the fishing line I'd left to dry and dragged the whole reel across the yard.
details: Line was wrapped around its legs, the fence, the water barrel, and the garden stake.
I spent twenty minutes untangling everything. The creature did not appreciate the help.
reactions: I'll hang the line higher. Anything at ground level is apparently a hazard.

entries.intro: The neighbor figured out that hanging old boots on the fence keeps {mob_type} away. Something about the smell.
details: His fence line has a boot every ten paces. Looks ridiculous but the tracks stop cold at the property line.
My fence, no boots: tracks everywhere. The evidence is hard to argue with.
reactions: I asked him for spare boots. My pride lost to his results.

statCheck.pass: Worn leather holds human foot scent longer than any other material: it's concentrated and persistent. The boots smell like a person standing there to them.
statCheck.fail: I don't fully understand why it works. I just know his side is clean and mine isn't.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} ate the mortar between the stones on the well cap. The cap is loose now.
details: It's been picking at it for weeks: little by little, crumble by crumble.
The stones shift when I push on them. Not safe.
reactions: Re-mortaring the well cap goes on the list. Right near the top.

entries.intro: {mob_type} knocked the plow blade out of alignment while it was stored in the barn. Must've bumped it hard.
details: The blade is torqued sideways. It'll cut a crooked furrow if I don't fix it first.
Getting a plow blade realigned takes a blacksmith. That's a trip and a cost.
reactions: Of all the things in the barn, they hit the one I can't fix myself.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has made a den inside the haystack. I discovered it when I grabbed an armful and it bit me.
details: There's a hollow carved out in the center. Warm, dry, surrounded by insulation: smart creature.
The hay around the den is soiled and packed down. That section is unusable.
reactions: I'm leaving that part of the haystack alone. It's earned the space.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} found the whetstone and dragged it off the workbench. Found it buried in the garden.
details: Why they wanted a whetstone is beyond me. It's not food, it's not nesting material.
The stone is chipped from being dropped. Still usable, but not as good.
reactions: I keep the workshop door shut now. Tools are not toys.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that waits under the kitchen window when I cook. It can smell everything.
details: I can see its shadow through the curtain, sitting perfectly still.
The moment I set something on the windowsill to cool: it's standing up, alert.
reactions: I cool everything on the table now. The window is compromised.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} pulled all the straw out of the scarecrow's body and used it for bedding. Left the clothes on the frame.
details: An empty shirt and trousers hanging on a stick: that's what's guarding the crops now.
The straw is in a neat pile about ten paces away. Fluffed up and slept in.
reactions: The scarecrow's been robbed by the very things it was meant to scare. I can't make this up.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} brought its young to the yard for the first time today. They stumbled around the garden like little drunks.
details: Falling over, bumping into things, eating dirt instead of plants.
The adult watched them with what I can only describe as tired patience.
reactions: I know that look. I've made that face. We have more in common than I'd like to admit.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got into the soap-making shed and ate every bar that was curing. Two weeks of work.
details: The drying rack is knocked over and the molds are cracked. Tooth marks in the wooden frames.
They left greasy prints all over the floor. At least it smells clean in there now.
reactions: I'll make more. Not like I have a choice.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been gnawing on the leather straps of the sandals I leave by the door. Both pairs.
details: The buckle tongues are chewed off and the soles are separating where the stitching was eaten.
I've started bringing them inside. The creature gnaws the doorframe instead now.
reactions: Apparently everything I own is edible to something.

statCheck.pass: It's after the salt from foot sweat soaked into the leather. Rinsing the straps in fresh water before leaving them out removes the attractant.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what makes leather so appealing. Maybe it just likes chewing.

entries.intro: {mob_type} knocked the clay chimney cap off the roof. It shattered on the path below.
details: Rain's been getting into the flue since. The whole fireplace smells damp and ashy.
A replacement cap means hiring someone comfortable on a roof. That's not me.
reactions: I've been cooking outside until it's fixed. Not ideal in this weather.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been sitting on the same fence post every morning, singing. Loud and early.
details: It starts before the sun is fully up. The same five notes, over and over.
My neighbor swears it's pleasant. I think my neighbor sleeps through it.
reactions: I'm awake before dawn every day now. Could be worse reasons.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} ripped the handle clean off the garden pump. Bent it sideways first, then snapped it.
details: The pump still works if I turn the stub with both hands, but it's slow going.
I need to find a smith willing to forge a replacement handle. The old one is beyond salvage.
reactions: Watering the garden takes three times as long now. Every morning.

statCheck.pass: The metal handle conducts cold from the groundwater and they lick it for moisture. The repeated stress of being pushed and pulled loosens the fitting until it snaps.
statCheck.fail: I have no idea why they'd bother with a pump handle. It's just iron.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps dragging snail shells onto the porch and cracking them open on the stones. Dozens of shells.
details: The pile of broken shells is growing. It uses the same flat stone every time.
The porch looks like a shell midden now. Crunchy underfoot.
reactions: At least something is keeping the snail population down. Even if my porch pays the price.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} tore the shutters off the shed and used them as a ramp to get onto the roof.
details: The shutters are leaning against the wall with claw marks dug into the wood. They used them like a ladder.
They left droppings all over the roof. Right on top of the spot I just re-thatched.
reactions: I'm impressed by the ingenuity. I'm furious about the shutters.

statCheck.pass: They're problem-solvers when food is involved. If there's nothing worth reaching on the roof, remove whatever attracted them and they won't bother building ramps.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they wanted up there. Maybe they just like being tall.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} pulled the caulking out of the window frame. Picked at it until it was all gone.
details: There's a draft through the gap now. I can feel it from across the room.
They took the caulking in strips and left it in a pile under the window. Didn't eat it: just removed it.
reactions: Re-caulking a window is not how I planned to spend the afternoon.

entries.intro: {mob_type} got into the pantry while we were out and licked every jar clean. Didn't break anything: just licked.
details: Every jar lid had been pried open. The contents are intact but contaminated.
The preserves, the pickles, the honey: all visited. Nothing is sealed anymore.
reactions: I can't eat any of it now. A pantry full of food and none of it's mine anymore.

statCheck.pass: They test everything by taste before committing. The licking means they sampled and moved on: nothing was sweet enough or fatty enough to eat in bulk.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they'd lick and not eat. Maybe they weren't hungry enough.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has taken to sunbathing on the flat stone I use for kneading bread. Every afternoon.
details: It stretches out full length and doesn't move for hours. Completely at ease.
I've tried shooing it off. It comes back within minutes.
reactions: I knead bread in the morning now. The afternoons belong to the creature.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pulled the rope out of the bucket and chewed it into three pieces. I can't draw water without it.
details: The rope was new. Replaced it just last week after the old one frayed.
I've been carrying water from the neighbor's well. It's twice the walk.
reactions: I'm keeping the rope inside from now on. More hassle, but at least it'll last.

statCheck.pass: Hemp rope absorbs well water minerals that they crave. A chain with a rope core would give the flexibility without the chewable surface.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why rope is so irresistible to them. It's just hemp.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has hollowed out a space inside the compost heap and moved in. I can see the entry tunnel.
details: The compost is too wet to turn now: the whole structure is compromised by tunnels.
It's warm in there and sheltered. Can't blame the creature for the choice.
reactions: I'll build a new compost heap and leave that one to its tenant.

entries.intro: {mob_type} have been piling debris against the back door at night. Sticks, stones, clumps of turf.
details: Every morning there's a small barricade I have to clear before I can open the door.
I don't know if they're doing it on purpose or it's some nesting behavior that happens to be against my house.
reactions: I clear it, they rebuild it. We've found a rhythm, if not an understanding.

statCheck.pass: They cache materials near structures they consider part of their territory boundary. The back of the house feels like the edge of their range, and they're marking it.
statCheck.fail: Beats me why they chose my back door. Maybe they're decorating.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the dye pot and walked across the clean laundry. Blue footprints on every white shirt.
details: The prints won't come out no matter how hard I scrub. The dye is good: I'll give it that.
The creature itself ran off trailing blue across the yard. Quite a sight.
reactions: I've got decorated shirts now. Not the decoration I'd have chosen, but permanent.

statCheck.pass: Indigo dye attracts them: the fermentation process creates a scent similar to overripe fruit. Covering the dye pot breaks the scent trail.
statCheck.fail: I think it just walked through the pot by accident. Wrong place, wrong time.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} dug up the stepping stones on the path and flipped every one of them over. All twelve.
details: The flat sides are face down now. Walking on the rough sides is like walking on teeth.
Flipping them back is heavy work. Each stone is as big as my torso.
reactions: I've done seven so far. My back has opinions about the remaining five.

statCheck.pass: They flip stones to eat the slugs and beetles living underneath. It's a foraging technique: flip, eat, move on.
statCheck.fail: I can't figure out why they'd flip stones. Maybe they just enjoy the work.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that watches me from the garden wall every time I eat outside. Never approaches: just watches.
details: It tilts its head when I lift the spoon. Follows every bite with its eyes.
I've tried ignoring it but it waits, and I've tried staring back but it wins.
reactions: I eat inside now. The judgment was too much.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got into the stable and chewed through every halter hanging on the wall. All four.
details: The buckles are intact but the leather is in pieces. Can't lead a horse with a buckle alone.
Replacing four halters is expensive. I'm stitching the least-damaged one back together for now.
reactions: Leather and {mob_type}: the most expensive combination in my life.

statCheck.pass: Leather oiled with neat's-foot oil smells like rendered fat to them. Switching to a plant-based conditioner removes the food scent entirely.
statCheck.fail: Something about stable leather gets them excited. Maybe the horse smell.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has claimed the warm spot near the chimney base. Curled up against the stones every evening.
details: It doesn't move when I walk past. Just opens one eye, then closes it again.
The stones hold heat well after the fire goes out. Smart spot, honestly.
reactions: We've reached an arrangement. I don't bother it: it doesn't bother me.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pulled the drain grate out of the ground and dragged it into the field. The drain flooded the walkway.
details: Standing water all the way from the gutter to the road. Ankle-deep in the low spot.
The grate was iron: heavy. They moved it twenty paces into the tall grass.
reactions: Found the grate, put it back, wedged stones around it. We'll see how long it lasts.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps leaving berries on the windowsill. Only one kind, always ripe.
details: Three or four berries each morning, placed in a neat cluster. Never crushed.
I've started eating them, and they're good. The creature has taste.
reactions: It's the only gift-giving relationship in my life that asks nothing in return.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} gnawed a hole in the water barrel large enough to put a fist through. Drained overnight.
details: I found the barrel empty and the ground around it a swamp. The garden is a puddle.
Patching a barrel with a hole that size means replacing the whole stave.
reactions: That was drinking water for the week. Now it's mud.

statCheck.pass: They gnaw barrels where the staves are dampest on the outside. Keeping the barrel on a stone platform so it dries evenly makes it less appealing to chew.
statCheck.fail: Apparently water barrels are just targets. I don't know why.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been dropping acorns down the chimney one at a time. I hear them bouncing off the walls on the way down.
details: There's a pile of acorns in the fireplace now. Must be forty or fifty.
Every few minutes: another one. Plink, plink, clatter.
reactions: I've given up sweeping them. I'll clean the lot once the creature moves on.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} found the cider press and knocked it off the platform. The pressing plate cracked.
details: The plate is stone and it split right down the center. Can't press with a split plate.
The apples were ready this week. Now they'll go soft before I can find a replacement.
reactions: I'll juice by hand. Slower, messier, but the apples won't wait.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been leaving neat little holes all over the lawn. Perfectly round, about a thumb's width.
details: Dozens of them, spaced evenly. Like it's following a grid only it can see.
I think it's hunting grubs. The lawn looks like a pincushion.
reactions: The grubs are probably a bigger problem than the holes. I should be grateful, I suppose.

statCheck.pass: It's aerating the soil while hunting: those holes improve drainage and root growth. The lawn will be healthier next season because of them.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what it's after down there. Something worth all that digging, apparently.

entries.intro: {mob_type} chewed through the rope holding the gate closed and the whole pen emptied into the road.
details: Took an hour to round everything up. Two chickens are still unaccounted for.
The rope was thick hemp. They went through it like it was string.
reactions: I'm using chain now. Let them try chewing through that.

statCheck.pass: They chew in shifts: one gnaws while the others wait. A rope that would take one creature all night goes down in an hour with three taking turns.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how they get through that thickness of rope so fast.

entries.intro: I found a {wildlife_type} asleep inside the empty flower pot on the back step. Tucked in tight like it was made for it.
details: It filled the pot exactly. Round creature, round pot: perfect fit.
I needed that pot for planting. Instead it's a bed now.
reactions: I'll use a different pot. That one's spoken for.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} tore the oilcloth off the woodpile and shredded it. Now the wood is getting rained on.
details: Wet firewood is useless firewood. It'll take days to dry out once I get a new cover on it.
The oilcloth was in strips across the yard. They didn't eat it: just destroyed it.
reactions: Replacing oilcloth isn't cheap. I'm considering a proper shed instead.

statCheck.pass: The linseed oil used to waterproof the cloth has a nutty smell that attracts them. Wax-treated canvas doesn't carry the same scent.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they have against oilcloth. Maybe the texture.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} figured out that the garden bell is connected to a string and keeps pulling the string. Ding, ding, ding.
details: It pulls, listens, pulls again. Fascinated by the cause and effect.
The neighbors have asked me to remove the bell. The creature won't stop.
reactions: I disconnected the string. The creature sits by the limp string looking confused.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} burrowed under the flagstone path and collapsed it. Three stones sank into the tunnel.
details: The path has a dip in it now that catches rainwater. Turns into a puddle every time.
Resetting flagstones means digging out the tunnel and packing the base again. A full day's work.
reactions: I walk around the dip. Fixing it is on the list, somewhere.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} knocked the vinegar jug off the shelf and it shattered on the floor. Everything reeks now.
details: The bread, the cheese, the dried fruit on the lower shelf: all soaked in vinegar.
The smell has seeped into the floorboards. Mopping makes it worse.
reactions: Everything tastes of vinegar this week. Even things that weren't in the room.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been quiet this season. First time in three years the garden's made it to harvest without a single trampling.
details: The fences are intact, the crops are standing, the rows are straight. Feels unnatural.
My neighbor says they've moved to better forage elsewhere. I'll take it.
reactions: I'm enjoying it, but the fences stay up. Quiet doesn't mean gone.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that has learned to open the latch on the cold frame. Lifts it with its nose.
details: I've watched it from inside: nose under the latch, flick upward, lid swings open.
Once it's open, it eats whatever's sprouting and leaves the lid up. Everything inside freezes.
reactions: I tied the latch with twine. It's working on the twine.

statCheck.pass: It learned the motion by watching me open the latch: they mimic physical actions they've seen repeated. A different latch style would reset the learning.
statCheck.fail: I have no idea how it figured out the latch. I can barely work it with my own fingers.

entries.intro: {mob_type} got into the seed-drying trays on the porch and scattered everything. Weeks of collecting, gone in one night.
details: Seeds mixed with dirt, fur, and droppings. None of it is usable now.
The trays are fine, at least. Wooden frames with mesh: too boring to chew.
reactions: Seed collecting starts over. The season waits for nobody.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps bringing moss inside through the open window and piling it in the corner of the room.
details: There's a damp green mound growing in the corner. It adds to it every day.
I remove the moss and it brings more. We're in a decorating disagreement.
reactions: The window stays closed now. Whatever aesthetic the creature was going for, I'm not on board.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been rubbing against the gatepost so often the wood is polished smooth. Almost pretty, actually.
details: The post was rough-hewn oak. Now it's worn down and glossy from the rubbing.
They come at dusk, rub their sides on it, and move on. Regular as clockwork.
reactions: The gate's weaker from the wear, but I have the smoothest post in the settlement.

statCheck.pass: They're scent-marking: the rubbing deposits oils from their hide that signal territorial boundaries to others. It's a signpost for them, same as it is for us.
statCheck.fail: I think it just feels good to them. Like a scratch they can't reach otherwise.

entries.intro: {mob_type} have been peeling the bark off the fence posts. Not eating it: just peeling and dropping it.
details: The exposed wood dries out and cracks. Each post looks worse than the last.
They work methodically from top to bottom. Very thorough.
reactions: I've started using posts with the bark already stripped. Nothing left to peel.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got tangled in the fishing nets drying by the fence and tore them to shreds trying to get free.
details: The nets are in pieces. Three full-size nets, reduced to string scraps.
Mending nets is tedious work. Re-weaving from scratch is worse.
reactions: I'll dry the nets on the roof from now on. Nothing's reaching them up there.

entries.intro: I watched a {wildlife_type} carry a stone up to the roof, drop it, and watch it bounce. Then it went to get another stone.
details: It did this six times. Same spot on the roof, same watching as the stone fell.
The stones are scattered around the base of the house now.
reactions: I don't know what it's studying, but it's committed to the research.

entries.intro: {mob_type} knocked the rain gauge off its post and stomped it flat. The glass tube is powder.
details: I've been tracking rainfall all season. That data is gone with the gauge.
Replacing the glass tube means a trip to the glassblower. Not a short walk.
reactions: I'll estimate the rain by how deep the puddles get. Less precise, but harder to stomp.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been rolling in the herb garden every morning. The mint is flat, the thyme is flat, everything is flat.
details: It lies on its back and wriggles, seemingly blissful. The herbs disagree.
The scent must feel good on its fur. The whole garden smells crushed.
reactions: The herbs will grow back: they always do, and the creature has found its spa.

statCheck.pass: Aromatic herbs repel parasites, so it's rolling in them to coat its fur with the oils. Mint and thyme are natural flea deterrents.
statCheck.fail: I think it just likes the smell. Can't say I blame it: fresh mint is nice.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} pried the iron ring off the hitching post outside the door. Just wrenched it out of the wood.
details: The hole where the ring was set is splintered open. The post needs replacing.
I found the ring in the grass ten paces away. Bent into a figure eight.
reactions: Iron rings aren't free, and posts aren't either. Another expense I didn't plan for.

statCheck.pass: The ring clinks in the wind, and metal sounds attract their attention at night. A rubber or leather washer behind the ring silences the movement.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what drew them to the hitching ring. Maybe they were testing their strength.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has decided the water basin outside is a bath, not a drinking trough. Splashes half the water out every visit.
details: I fill the basin: it empties the basin. We do this twice a day.
The ground around the basin is permanent mud now.
reactions: I could move the basin, but I know the creature would find it.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} found the loose board under the back wall of the barn and widened it into a proper entrance.
details: The board was slightly rotted at one end. They exploited that spot and tore it open.
Now there's a gap wide enough for anything to walk through. The barn is basically open-air.
reactions: I'll nail a plank over it tonight. They'll probably find the next weakness by tomorrow.

statCheck.pass: They follow moisture damage: rotted wood and loosened nails are their entry points. Sealing the base with pitch and checking for soft spots each season prevents this.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how they always find the one weak board. Bad luck on my part, I guess.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} keep pulling the twine off the bean poles at night. Every morning, less twine.
details: The beans are falling over without support. They can't climb what isn't there.
I find bits of twine in the hedge, in the yard, draped over the fence. A trail of string.
reactions: I've switched to wire. Less comfortable to steal, I'd imagine.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been coming less often since I started spreading wood ash along the fence line. Something about it puts them off.
details: The tracks stop right at the ash line. They walk up to it, then turn around.
I'm going through ash faster than I'm burning wood. Burning extra just to make more ash.
reactions: Whatever works. I'll burn every scrap of kindling if it keeps them out.

statCheck.pass: The lye in wood ash irritates their pads and they can taste it in the air. A fresh line needs to be laid after every rain, though: water dissolves the lye.
statCheck.fail: Something about the ash bothers them. I don't know what, but I'm not stopping.

entries.intro: {mob_type} have been chewing the rungs of the stile over the garden fence. Three rungs are half their original thickness.
details: The stile wobbles now when I climb it. One more gnaw session and a rung breaks.
I painted the rungs with vinegar and they licked it off and kept chewing.
reactions: I'll replace the wooden rungs with stone steps. Harder to chew, harder to climb, but permanent.

entries.intro: {mob_type} scattered the charcoal kiln I'd spent two days building. Just kicked it apart.
details: The earth mound collapsed and the half-charred wood inside is ruined. Too much air got in.
Two days of stacking, sealing, and slow-burning: undone in one night.
reactions: Building kilns is patience work. I have less patience now than when I started.

statCheck.pass: The warmth radiating from the kiln draws them in, and the smoldering wood scent masks other predator smells. They bed down against warm earth mounds if they can.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if they destroyed it on purpose or just leaned against it and it gave way.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has taken to grooming itself on the windowsill while I eat breakfast. We make eye contact daily.
details: It licks its paws and watches me chew. I chew and watch it lick its paws.
We've been doing this for two weeks. Neither of us has made the first move to stop.
reactions: It's the most consistent relationship in my life right now.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pushed a dead log into the irrigation channel and dammed the whole thing. The upper fields flooded.
details: The log was wedged tight. Took two of us with a pry bar to shift it.
Half the seedlings in the upper beds drowned before we cleared the blockage.
reactions: One log, one night, half a planting. That's the math.

statCheck.pass: They dam channels to create shallow pools for drinking. The flooding is a side effect they don't understand or care about.
statCheck.fail: I can't tell if it was deliberate or they just knocked the log sideways.

entries.intro: I watched a {wildlife_type} spend twenty minutes trying to get a nut out of a crack in the stone wall. Finally got it.
details: It tried teeth, paws, a stick: went through every tool available. Patient creature.
When the nut finally popped out, the creature sat still for a moment, then ate it in one bite.
reactions: Honestly, I was rooting for it by the end.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} broke into the beehive and the bees have been aggressive all week. Can't walk near the garden without getting stung.
details: The hive's been repaired but the bees are still agitated. They remember the intrusion.
I've been stung four times going to the well. The well is twenty paces from the hive.
reactions: The {mob_type} caused the problem and moved on. I'm the one dealing with the aftermath.

statCheck.pass: Disturbed hives stay aggressive for about two weeks while they rebuild and re-establish the colony scent. Smoking them with dry grass calms them, but only temporarily.
statCheck.fail: I've never seen the bees stay this angry for this long. I give them a wide berth.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been carrying pebbles onto the roof and dropping them into the gutter. The gutter is full of stones now.
details: The water can't drain. Every rain overspills the gutter and runs down the wall.
I cleaned it out and by the next day: more pebbles.
reactions: I'm not climbing a ladder every week to clean stones out of the gutter. But here I am.

entries.intro: {mob_type} chewed through the leather bellows in the workshop. Both sides, completely perforated.
details: Can't work the forge without bellows. The fire just smolders without forced air.
Leather bellows take time to make. I'm fanning with a board in the meantime.
reactions: A board doesn't move enough air. Everything takes twice as long.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that appears every time I split firewood. Sits on the chopping block and won't move until I shoo it.
details: I set a log down, it hops up. I shoo it off, I set the log, it hops up again.
I think it likes the vibration in the wood when the axe lands. Moves to the log pile to watch.
reactions: Splitting wood now takes twice as long because of the negotiations.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} dug under the hen house and collapsed the floor. The hens fell through into the hole.
details: Three hens stuck in a pit, squawking. Getting them out without widening the hole was tricky.
The floor needs to be rebuilt from underneath. I'll have to jack the whole structure up.
reactions: The hens are fine but the hen house is not, and my schedule for the week is gone.

statCheck.pass: They dig under structures to reach the warmth and the food scraps that accumulate beneath. Laying a stone foundation two hands deep prevents tunneling entirely.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they targeted the hen house. Maybe the noise attracted them.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has started following me to the well every morning. Walks three paces behind, stops when I stop.
details: It waits while I draw water, then follows me back. Same distance, same pace.
I've tried talking to it. It tilts its head and keeps following.
reactions: I have an escort now. Didn't ask for one, can't dismiss it.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} broke into the root cellar and ate every turnip but left the parsnips untouched. Sat right next to them and chose not to.
details: The turnip bin is empty and the parsnip bin is full. They had opinions about root vegetables.
There are tooth marks on one parsnip where they tested it and apparently decided against it.
reactions: I'm eating parsnips all winter. At least one of us is happy about it.

statCheck.pass: Parsnips contain a compound that's mildly toxic to most creatures when raw, and the bitter taste is a warning they can detect. Storing turnips with parsnip shavings might deter them.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they left the parsnips. Maybe they just don't like the taste.

entries.intro: {mob_type} chewed through the handle of the garden fork. Clean through, right where I grip it.
details: The fork's head is fine: iron doesn't attract teeth. But the ash handle is ruined.
I'll whittle a new handle, but the wood has to be seasoned or it'll crack.
reactions: I'm building a tool chest with a lid. Everything goes inside from now on.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} kicked over the smoker I was using to calm the bees. Burning coals scattered across the yard.
details: I was mid-harvest. The bees went from calm to furious in seconds.
Put out the coals before anything caught, but I've got stings on every finger.
reactions: Bee keeping and {mob_type}: two hobbies that don't mix.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been pressing its face against the kitchen window every evening. Just staring in.
details: There's a nose print on the glass that I clean off every morning. It comes back every night.
It watches the whole dinner from start to finish, then leaves.
reactions: Having a silent audience makes every meal feel like a performance.

entries.intro: {mob_type} overturned the manure pile and spread it across the walkway. Knee-deep in some spots.
details: The smell is something else. Every door on the street is closed and every window too.
Shoveling it back into a pile is the worst chore I've ever done.
reactions: The garden will benefit eventually. The walkway will not recover for weeks.

statCheck.pass: They roll in manure to mask their own scent. It's territorial behavior: the stronger the smell, the more dominant the signal.
statCheck.fail: I can't imagine why they'd dig through manure on purpose. Maybe they smelled something buried in it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been leaving shed fur in a neat pile outside the back door. Like it's making a delivery.
details: The pile grows each day. A soft, fluffy mound of fur right on the step.
I've been using it to pad the bottoms of storage crates. Surprisingly useful.
reactions: Free insulation. Not the source I'd have chosen, but I'll take it.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} ripped the pulley off the grain hoist and the whole counterweight dropped. Dented the platform.
details: The pulley wheel cracked when it hit the ground. The rope is frayed where it tore loose.
Hoisting grain by hand until it's fixed. My shoulders are already protesting.
reactions: One broken pulley and everything becomes harder. Amazing how much one piece of rope and iron matters.

statCheck.pass: The rope runs through grain dust that collects on the pulley wheel. They gnaw the rope for the dust, and the pulley fails when the fibers are too thin to hold the weight.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if they chewed the rope or just pulled hard enough to tear it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} ate every ripe cherry off the tree the night before I planned to pick them. Every single one.
details: I'd been watching them ripen for weeks, telling myself one more day.
The creature had the same idea, apparently, and better timing.
reactions: Next year I pick early. Slightly unripe cherries beat no cherries.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} dragged the garden bench into the flower bed and used it as a scratching post. Every surface is clawed.
details: The seat, the legs, the armrests: all gouged. Deep scratches in the grain.
The flowers under the bench are crushed flat. Both the bench and the bed need work.
reactions: I sanded the scratches down and the bench is usable, but the flowers are less forgiving.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps pushing its way into the shed and rearranging the tools. Never breaks anything: just moves things.
details: The hammer ends up where the saw was, and the saw ends up on the floor. Nothing is where I left it.
I've started keeping a mental map of where the creature thinks things should go.
reactions: It has a system. I don't understand the system, but it's consistent.

entries.intro: {mob_type} have been marking the fence posts with scratches. Deep ones: three parallel lines on every post.
details: The marks are all at the same height. Consistent, deliberate.
Every post in the row has them now. Like a boundary they're documenting.
reactions: The fence is holding, but looking rougher by the week.

statCheck.pass: Those are territorial marks: the height and spacing tell other groups the size and number that claimed this range. Fresh marks mean the claim is active.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what the scratches mean to them. Probably nothing good for me.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} discovered the bell pepper plants and has been taking one pepper a day. Exactly one, always the reddest.
details: It selects carefully. Walks past the green ones, past the orange ones, takes the red one.
By the time a pepper is fully ripe, the creature gets it first.
reactions: I'm harvesting everything at orange now. Red peppers are a luxury I can no longer afford.

statCheck.pass: Red peppers have the highest sugar content: triple what green ones have. The creature can smell the difference and waits for peak sweetness.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how it knows which ones are ripest. Better eyes than mine, maybe.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} ripped the clothesline anchor out of the wall. Pulled the whole iron hook right out of the mortar.
details: The hook took a chunk of mortar with it. There's a fist-sized hole in the wall.
The washing that was on the line is in the dirt. Again.
reactions: I'm setting the new anchor in stone, not mortar. Overkill for laundry, but here we are.

statCheck.pass: Clothing on a line holds human scent. They pull the line because the smell agitates them: it's a territory response, not mischief.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why the clothesline offends them. Maybe they don't like clean laundry.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the open cupboard and arranged every cup in a neat row on the counter. Didn't break one.
details: Six cups, all right-side up, evenly spaced. Better organized than I usually keep them.
I found the creature sitting on the counter looking satisfied.
reactions: I put them back in the cupboard: it's my kitchen, I think.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} have been sleeping in the hay loft and their weight is cracking the floorboards.
details: I can see the bowing from below. The boards flex when I poke them with a broomstick.
If the floor gives way, there's livestock underneath. Can't have that.
reactions: I'm reinforcing the floor joists this week. Should've done it a year ago.

statCheck.pass: They bed down in the warmest spot, which is directly over the livestock: the animals' body heat rises through the floorboards. Blocking the loft hatch with a heavy door would keep them out entirely.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they pick the loft over any other sheltered spot. Must be comfortable.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been storing walnuts inside my boots every night. Fills both boots to the brim.
details: I shake them out every morning. Walnuts pour across the floor like marbles.
I tried stuffing the boots with cloth. The creature pulled the cloth out and replaced it with walnuts.
reactions: I wear the boots to bed now. Only way to keep them empty.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} scratched a groove into the bottom of the door deep enough to see daylight through it.
details: The draft coming through the groove is constant. Feels like the door is cracked open.
I stuffed the groove with rags. They pulled the rags out from the other side.
reactions: I'm nailing a board across the bottom of the door. Let them scratch at that.

statCheck.pass: They can smell food through the gap under the door and scratch to widen it. An iron kick plate across the bottom stops them cold: they won't scratch metal.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they smell something inside. I don't know what drives them to scratch one particular door.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} brought a live frog into the house and released it in the kitchen. The frog is still at large.
details: I've been finding it in different rooms all day. Behind the flour bin, under the chair, inside a pot.
The creature watched me chase the frog for five minutes and then lost interest and left.
reactions: The frog will find its way out eventually. I hope.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pulled the trellising off the garden wall. The climbing plants are hanging in midair now.
details: The trellis was wired to iron hooks. They bent the hooks and pulled the whole thing loose.
The vines have nothing to climb. They'll die without support if I don't get the trellis back up.
reactions: I spent the morning re-wiring while the vines spent the morning dying. We'll see who wins.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that has figured out when I take bread out of the oven. Shows up within minutes, every time.
details: I don't know if it smells it or hears the oven door. But it's there, waiting, before the loaf has cooled.
It sits at a polite distance and stares: very patient, very obvious.
reactions: I've started tearing off a corner for it. We have a routine now.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got into the linseed oil I use for treating wood. Knocked the pot over and it soaked into the floor.
details: Linseed oil doesn't come out of anything. The floor is permanently stained and slippery.
The pot was full: a whole season's pressing. Gone into the boards.
reactions: The floor is waterproof now, at least. Silver lining.

statCheck.pass: Raw linseed oil has a nutty taste they find appealing. Boiled linseed oil smells sharper and they avoid it: switching to boiled would protect the supply.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what attracted them to linseed oil. It doesn't smell like food to me.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} have been sleeping on the stack of clean towels I leave on the porch to dry. Every towel: covered in fur.
details: I wash the towels, hang them, fold them, set them down. They arrive within the hour.
Shaking the fur out doesn't work. It's woven into the fabric.
reactions: I carry the towels inside the moment they're dry now. No resting on the porch.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have stopped crossing the stream since the water level rose. First dry stretch in months.
details: The higher water makes the crossing too deep for them. Our side has been peaceful.
The garden is untouched, the fences are intact. Almost too good.
reactions: I'm hoping for a long rainy season. Selfish, maybe, but effective.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been digging up the garlic bulbs I planted last month. Pops them out, sniffs them, and drops them.
details: Every bulb is out of the ground by morning. I replant them, it digs them up again.
It clearly doesn't want to eat them. Something about the smell offends it.
reactions: We disagree about garlic. I'm planting deeper.

entries.intro: {mob_type} tore the slats off the bottom of the garden gate. Crawled through the gap and ate the lettuce.
details: Just the lettuce: the carrots, the onions, the radishes were all untouched. They came specifically for lettuce.
The gate looks like it lost a fight. Three slats missing, two more hanging by one nail.
reactions: I'm not replanting lettuce until that gate is solid. No point feeding them on purpose.

statCheck.pass: Lettuce has high water content and a mild scent they can detect easily, while root vegetables don't broadcast their presence the same way. Covering the rows with cloth at night hides the scent.
statCheck.fail: I have no idea why lettuce and nothing else. Picky eaters.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been leaving chewed-up flower heads on the front step. Like little bouquets, but crushed.
details: Daisies mostly, with a few dandelions mixed in. All slightly mangled and very damp.
It seems deliberate. The placement is too consistent to be random.
reactions: I've started thinking of them as gifts. Badly wrapped, partially eaten gifts.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} broke the crossbeam on the clothesline by swinging on it. Snapped clean in the middle.
details: All the washing dropped into the mud. A full day's laundry, filthy again.
The beam is split. I can't just nail it: the break goes right through the grain.
reactions: I need a new beam. And a new place to put it where nothing can swing from it.

statCheck.pass: They hang from horizontal beams to stretch their spines: it's a comfort behavior, not mischief. A rope clothesline doesn't support their weight, so replacing the beam with rope between two posts solves it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they decided to swing on the clothesline beam. Entertainment, maybe.

entries.intro: I've noticed a {wildlife_type} comes to the garden only after rain. Sits in the wet grass and does nothing.
details: It doesn't eat, doesn't dig: just sits. Fur damp, eyes half-closed.
Every rain, same creature, same patch of grass.
reactions: I think it just likes the feeling. Can't say I don't understand.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} knocked the stone off the top of the gatepost. It was the capstone: held the mortar in place.
details: Without the cap, rainwater gets into the mortar joints and the whole post is softening.
The capstone is heavy. I need help lifting it back into position.
reactions: I'll get it fixed before the next rain. Or the post will dissolve before my eyes.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has figured out that the garden hose drips and comes to drink from the drip every afternoon.
details: It sits under the drip and catches the drops one at a time. Patient beyond reason.
I fixed the drip once. It sat by the dry hose looking betrayed.
reactions: I let the hose drip now: the water waste is tiny, and the creature's happiness is not.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} pulled the weather vane off the barn roof and left it in the pig trough.
details: The vane is bent and the directional arrow is snapped off. Just a metal stick now.
I didn't know the weather vane was loose. Apparently they did.
reactions: I'll check my wind direction the old way: look at the trees.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the mending basket and pulled out every needle. Found them scattered across the floor.
details: I've retrieved most of the needles, but I'm sure there's one hiding in the rug. I'll find it with my foot.
The thread was unwound and dragged across two rooms.
reactions: The basket goes on a high shelf from now on. Above creature height.

entries.intro: {mob_type} have been digging up the fence line and burying things in the holes. Bones, scraps, bits of hide.
details: Every morning there's a new hole under the fence with something foul buried in it.
The fence is undermined now. The posts lean because the soil around them is soft from all the digging.
reactions: I'm filling the holes with gravel. Harder to dig through, and they can bury their treasures somewhere else.

statCheck.pass: They bury food near structures they consider landmarks. The fence line is consistent and visible: a reliable reference point for cache retrieval.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand why they always bury things near my fence specifically.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that has started grooming the cat. The cat tolerates it, barely.
details: The creature licks the cat's head while the cat sits rigid, eyes wide, perfectly still.
This happens on the porch every afternoon. Neither of them looks happy about it.
reactions: Forced friendship: the cat endures it, the creature persists, and I watch.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} pushed the garden cart down the hill. It rolled into the pond and sank.
details: Half the cart is sticking out of the water. The rest is in the mud at the bottom.
Getting it out means wading in waist-deep. I'm not looking forward to it.
reactions: I always parked that cart at the top of the hill. Never again.

statCheck.pass: They lean on wheeled objects and when the wheel gives, the push becomes a roll. Chocking the wheels with stones prevents the initial movement.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if they pushed it deliberately or just leaned on it and it went.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has worn a trail through the yard from visiting the same path every day. Grass is gone, dirt is packed.
details: The trail runs from the hedge to the compost pile. Straight as a ruler.
I'd be annoyed about the bare patch, but the creature is more consistent than my garden paths.
reactions: I'm thinking of laying stones along it. If there's going to be a path, it might as well be a good one.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} tore the lid off the salt box and licked the whole contents down to the wood.
details: That was my cooking salt for the month. The box is clean: they didn't leave a grain.
Salt is heavy to carry and expensive to replace. One night, one problem.
reactions: The salt box goes inside now. Anything left outdoors is apparently a buffet.

statCheck.pass: They crave mineral salts the same way livestock do. A cheap salt block set away from the house satisfies the craving without them ransacking the supply.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they'd eat plain salt. Seems unpleasant, but what do I know.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been carrying small sticks onto the porch and building a pile in the exact center. Right in the walkway.
details: The pile is knee-high now. Every stick laid carefully, one on top of the last.
I moved the pile once. The creature rebuilt it in the same spot within a day.
reactions: I walk around it now. The creature clearly has plans I don't understand.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} dug a trench right across the garden path. Knee-deep, perfectly straight.
details: The displaced dirt is piled neatly along one side. Methodical diggers.
I filled the trench and they re-dug it the next night. Same exact line.
reactions: I'm considering just stepping over it and calling it a drainage feature.

statCheck.pass: They dig along underground root lines. There's a tree root running under that path and they're exposing it to get at the grubs living in the bark.
statCheck.fail: I can't figure out why they insist on digging in that exact spot.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the open barrel of flour and rolled in it. Came out looking like a ghost.
details: White footprints everywhere: across the floor, up the wall, along the windowsill.
The flour that isn't on the floor or the creature is full of fur. Not fit for bread.
reactions: I'll sift what I can save. The rest is creature flour now.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pushed the pig trough against the fence and used it as a step to climb over.
details: The trough is too heavy for me to move alone. They shoved it three paces.
Once they were over the fence, they went straight for the vegetable garden. Targeted.
reactions: I chained the trough to the post. They'll have to find another ladder.

statCheck.pass: They're tool users in a crude sense: they'll push, stack, or drag objects to gain height. Keeping movable objects away from fence lines eliminates the opportunity.
statCheck.fail: I didn't think they were clever enough to use things as steps. I was wrong.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been leaving polished stones on the garden wall. Smooth, round, all roughly the same size.
details: There are a dozen now, lined up along the top of the wall. Each one placed flat side down.
They're river stones, and the nearest river is a fair walk: that's commitment.
reactions: I leave them where they are. The creature has a project and I don't want to interfere.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} chewed the lid of the well until it's paper-thin in one spot. One more rain and it'll cave in.
details: The wood at the chew point is soft and splintered. You can push a finger through it.
A well without a lid is a hazard for every creature and person.
reactions: I'm replacing the lid with a heavier board and a stone on top. They can chew stone if they're that determined.

statCheck.pass: They can smell the moisture rising from the well and chew toward the source. A stone slab or metal plate over the access point eliminates the scent trail.
statCheck.fail: The well lid seems like the last thing worth chewing. Apparently not.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been eating the wax seal off every honey jar on the shelf. Peels it off cleanly and eats just the wax.
details: The honey is fine underneath, but exposed. It'll crystallize without the seal.
I've re-sealed the jars three times this week. It peels them open again overnight.
reactions: I switched to cork stoppers. Less satisfying to peel, apparently: it's left them alone.

statCheck.pass: Beeswax retains trace honey scent that the creature can smell even after rendering. Cork carries no food scent, which is why the switch worked.
statCheck.fail: I'm just glad the corks stopped it. I don't know why wax was so appealing.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} found the tin of rendered tallow on the workbench and ate the whole thing. Tin included: they chewed the tin.
details: The tin is crumpled and tooth-marked. They bit right through the metal.
Tallow is hard enough to render. Losing it to something that eats containers is a new low.
reactions: Iron containers from now on. I refuse to lose another tin.

statCheck.pass: Thin tin is softer than their teeth, and they chew through it the same way they'd gnaw bark. Anything thicker than a coin's width stops them.
statCheck.fail: I didn't think anything could chew through metal. I was clearly wrong.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps burying things in the freshly turned soil of the garden beds. I dig up surprises every time I plant.
details: Acorns, pebbles, a piece of bone, half a mushroom. An archaeological dig in my own garden.
The creature watches me unearth its treasures with what I can only describe as concern.
reactions: I put the items in a pile by the fence. The creature takes them back and reburies them.

entries.intro: {mob_type} have been bumping against the walls of the house at night. Not breaking in: just bumping.
details: The whole house shakes slightly each time. Things rattle on the shelves.
I can hear them walking along the foundation and then: thud. Move on, thud.
reactions: Sleep is harder when the walls vibrate. But at least they're not coming through.

statCheck.pass: They bump structures to test the walls for give: if a wall flexes they try harder, and if it's solid they move on. Reinforcing the weakest sections discourages them.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if they're trying to get in or if they're just clumsy.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been collecting pine cones and arranging them in a circle in the yard. Perfectly round.
details: The circle is about an arm's length across. Each cone placed with the stem pointing outward.
It adds a new cone every day or two. The circle is almost closed.
reactions: I want to see it finished. I don't know why.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} ate through the canvas sack of barley and it poured out through the hole. A trail of grain from the shed to the fence.
details: The sack was suspended from the ceiling: they climbed the shelves to reach it.
Most of the barley is on the ground now, mixed with straw and dirt.
reactions: Wooden bins with lids. That's the only storage that survives them.

statCheck.pass: They follow the scent trail of grain dust in the air, and canvas breathes enough to let that scent through. Sealed bins eliminate the airborne scent entirely.
statCheck.fail: I thought suspending it from the ceiling would be enough. Nothing is enough.

entries.intro: There's a {wildlife_type} that chirps at me from the fence every time I hang laundry. Disapproves of something.
details: It gets louder the longer I take, chirping at each item as I hang it.
I finished hanging everything today and it went quiet. Satisfied, I suppose.
reactions: I'm being supervised by a creature that doesn't understand cloth.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got under the porch and have been denning there. I can hear them snoring through the floorboards.
details: The porch boards creak more now from the burrowing underneath.
They've made it cozy: dug out a proper hollow and lined it with something soft.
reactions: As long as they stay under the porch and out of the house, I can live with the snoring.

statCheck.pass: Once they've established a den, they defend it. Blocking the entrance while they're out foraging at dusk is the cleanest way to relocate them.
statCheck.fail: I'm not sure how to get them out without a confrontation nobody wants.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} have been pulling the stuffing out of the old chair I keep on the porch. Handful by handful.
details: There's a trail of horsehair from the chair to the hedge. The seat cushion is half-empty.
They're building a nest somewhere with my chair's insides.
reactions: The chair needed re-stuffing anyway. They're just accelerating the timeline.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} haven't touched the crops since the dog arrived. Two weeks of perfect silence.
details: The dog patrols the fence line at dusk and dawn. The tracks stop at the property edge.
I was skeptical about getting a dog just for pest control. I'm a believer now.
reactions: Best investment I've made in years. The dog gets extra supper.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been leaving perfectly round holes in the garden soil. Like someone pressed a cork into the dirt.
details: Dozens of them, neat and uniform. I thought someone was using a tool.
Turns out the creature presses its nose into the soil to smell for grubs. Each hole is a sniff.
reactions: The holes don't hurt anything: the creature gets grubs and the soil gets aerated.

entries.intro: {mob_type} shoved the compost bin against the back door and I couldn't get out this morning. Had to go through the window.
details: The bin is heavy when full. I couldn't push the door open more than a hand's width.
Climbing out the kitchen window in my morning clothes was not dignified.
reactions: The neighbor saw me climbing out the window. That's a conversation I'll have eventually.

statCheck.pass: They push objects against doors they've seen people exit from. It's a territorial display: blocking a route they associate with human activity.
statCheck.fail: Whether it was accidental or deliberate, the result was the same.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been eating the glue off the spine of the one book I own. Nibbled it right down to bare thread.
details: The pages are loose now. They slide out when I pick it up.
I don't know why it targeted the glue. Animal hide glue, maybe: smells like food to the creature.
reactions: I'll have the book rebound. The content is fine: just unattached.

statCheck.pass: Hide glue is rendered from the same proteins as jerky, so to anything with a keen nose, a book spine smells like dried meat. Fish glue for the rebinding would attract less interest.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it ate the binding. Maybe it was bored.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} toppled the stone stack I use as a property marker. Knocked it flat and scattered the stones.
details: I built that stack five high with the heaviest stones I could carry. They went through it like a wall of straw.
The stones are spread across ten paces. Collecting them alone will take most of the morning.
reactions: I'll stack them again. It's my land, and the stones say so.

statCheck.pass: They topple vertical stacks to test for nesting creatures hidden between the stones. It's instinctive foraging: they knock it down and check the gaps.
statCheck.fail: Maybe it was in their way. Maybe they just like knocking things over.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got into the laundry tub and fell asleep in the soapy water. I found it floating contentedly.
details: It was on its back with its eyes closed, completely relaxed in my wash water.
The laundry needs redoing. The water is full of fur and whatever the creature brought with it.
reactions: At least one of us enjoyed the bath.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} gnawed the support beam under the lean-to until it snapped. The whole roof sagged overnight.
details: The beam is chewed to half its thickness at the break point. Slow, persistent work.
Everything stored under the lean-to is exposed now. The tools, the firewood, the grain sacks.
reactions: A temporary prop holds the roof. The real fix is a new beam, and that's a full day.

statCheck.pass: They chew structural wood to file their teeth. They prefer load-bearing timber because the compression makes it denser and more effective as a tooth file.
statCheck.fail: Of all the wood in the yard, they picked the one piece holding up the roof.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps meeting me at the garden gate every morning like a gatekeeper. I have to wait for it to move.
details: It sits right in the gate opening, blocking the path. Stares at me, then eventually waddles aside.
The delay is about thirty seconds. It makes me earn entry every day.
reactions: I've started saying good morning to it. The creature doesn't respond, but the habit's formed.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} tore the planking off the bridge over the drainage ditch. Three boards, ripped loose and tossed aside.
details: The remaining boards are spaced too far apart to walk on safely. Especially at night.
I've been jumping the ditch instead. Fine in dry weather: risky when it's wet.
reactions: New boards are cut and ready. I'm nailing them down with twice the nails this time.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pulled the wick out of the lantern on the porch. Just the wick: yanked it clean out.
details: The lantern is useless without a wick. Oil and glass and no light.
I found the wick in the yard, chewed to fuzz.
reactions: I made a new wick from cotton scrap. They'll probably take that one too.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pried the nails out of the fence board. Not pulled: pried, one by one, with their teeth.
details: Eight nails, each one bent and lying in a row on the ground. The board fell off.
The patience it took to extract eight nails is impressive. And deeply frustrating.
reactions: I'm using bolts. Let them pry those out.

statCheck.pass: The nails work loose slightly from the wood expanding and contracting with moisture, and they can feel that looseness. Pre-drilling and countersinking makes the nails flush and undetectable.
statCheck.fail: I didn't know they could pull nails. That feels like it shouldn't be possible.

entries.intro: I watched a {wildlife_type} catch a grasshopper, examine it carefully, and then let it go. Caught the same one again and let it go again.
details: It did this four times with the same grasshopper. Each time: catch, inspect, release.
By the fourth time the grasshopper seemed less alarmed. Resigned, maybe.
reactions: I don't know what the creature was looking for. Apparently the grasshopper didn't have it.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} rolled the grinding stone out of the workshop and into the yard. It weighs more than I do.
details: The stone is lying on its side in the middle of the path. I can't lift it alone.
Moving it back means at least two people and a lever. I've been asking around.
reactions: The workshop is useless without the stone. And the stone is in the mud.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been leaving feathers inside the house through a gap in the eaves. The bedroom is full of down.
details: Every morning there's a new layer of feathers on the bed. Like a thin dusting of snow.
The gap in the eaves is tiny. The creature must push them through one at a time.
reactions: My pillow has never been softer. Though I'd prefer the delivery method changed.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} chewed through the leather strapping that holds the cart bed to the frame. The bed shifted sideways on the road.
details: I was hauling a load and the whole bed slid off the frame. Everything spilled.
Re-strapping takes leather I don't have in stock. I'm borrowing until I can tan more.
reactions: I check the strapping every morning now. A habit I shouldn't need but do.

statCheck.pass: Cart strapping soaks up road salt and rain minerals that accumulate in the leather. They chew it for the mineral content, not the leather itself.
statCheck.fail: I wish I knew why leather strapping is so irresistible to them.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been sitting on the scarecrow's shoulder like a companion. The scarecrow is more popular than ever.
details: It perches there all morning and watches the garden. The actual garden it was meant to protect.
Other creatures see something sitting on the scarecrow and give it wider berth.
reactions: The scarecrow finally works. It just needed a live creature on it to be convincing.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} trampled the potato mounds flat. Every single hill, stomped level with the ground.
details: The potatoes are exposed to sunlight now. They'll green up and go bitter if I don't re-hill them today.
Re-hilling twenty mounds by hand is four hours of shoveling. My arms are already sore.
reactions: The potatoes were two weeks from harvest. Two weeks was too much to ask.

statCheck.pass: They flatten mounds because the loose soil is easy to dig and sometimes harbors grubs. Tamping the soil firmly after hilling makes the mounds less inviting.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if they stepped on the mounds deliberately or just didn't care where they walked.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} built a nest in the crook of the drainpipe and the rain can't drain properly. The pipe overflows every storm.
details: The nest is woven tight: sticks, grass, bits of string. Solid construction.
Removing it means breaking something the creature worked hard on. I keep hesitating.
reactions: I'll wait until the young leave. Then the nest comes down and a mesh cap goes on.

entries.intro: {mob_type} somehow got the padlock off the shed. It was still locked: they pulled the hasp out of the wood.
details: The lock is fine, but the hasp is ripped free with the screws still in it. They went around the lock entirely.
The shed door was standing open when I found it. Nothing inside was touched: they just opened it.
reactions: A stronger hasp, longer screws, and a prayer. That's my security plan.

statCheck.pass: The screws were in softwood, and they can feel the flex in a hasp and work it back and forth until the screws pull free. Hardwood behind the hasp or through-bolts solve it permanently.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how they got the hasp out. Locks only work if the thing the lock is attached to holds.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been lining up pebbles in a row along the top of the garden wall. Twenty-three so far.
details: Each pebble is roughly the same size and color. It's selective about which ones qualify.
I counted them yesterday and again today: two new ones added.
reactions: I've started counting them every morning. We're both invested now.
