entries.intro: The {mob_type} chewed through the hitching rope while the horse was tied up. The horse wandered off down the road.
details: Found the horse grazing in a field half a mile away. Calm as anything: no idea it was lost.
The rope is in two pieces. Clean bite, right where it was tied.
reactions: I'm using a chain lead now. Heavier, but I know where the horse will be.

statCheck.pass: They chew hitching ropes because the tension makes the fibers separate easily. A slack loop on a chain post removes the tension they exploit.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if they wanted the rope or wanted the horse free. Either way: same result.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps catching moths under the porch lantern and eating them. Provides its own entertainment.
details: It sits directly under the light and snaps them out of the air. Rarely misses.
The moth population near the house has dropped noticeably.
reactions: Fewer moths means fewer chewed-up shirts. The creature is earning its keep.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} knocked the milestone over on the path to the {poi_type}. The thing weighed half a ton.
details: It's lying face-down in the ditch. The carved face with the distance is pressed into the mud.
Nobody can lift it back. We'll need a team and a lever.
reactions: Until it's upright again, everyone's guessing how far to the {poi_type}.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} stole the cork float off the fishing line and dropped it down the well. I heard the plop.
details: The cork is bobbing in the well water now. Too far down to reach without a hook.
Fishing without a float means guessing at bites. My catch rate has halved.
reactions: I'll carve a new float. The old one can live in the well.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been leaving claw marks on the trees around the settlement. Fresh ones every morning.
details: The marks are high up: above head height. They're stretching when they make them.
Every marked tree is along the same path. Like a trail of warnings.
reactions: I don't like walking past scratched trees. Feels like walking under someone's notice.

statCheck.pass: Height marks show dominance: they reach as high as they can to make the mark look like it came from something bigger. It's for other creatures, not for us.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what the scratches mean. Nothing good, probably.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been hiding in the woodpile and jumping out when the cat walks past. Gets the cat every time.
details: The cat puffs up, hisses, then walks on. The creature retreats into the woodpile.
Five minutes later: the same ambush. The cat never learns and the creature never tires.
reactions: It's the most reliable entertainment in the household. Better than anything I could arrange.

entries.intro: {mob_type} tore the canvas awning off the back of the house. The frame is bent and the fabric is in shreds.
details: The awning kept rain off the firewood. Without it, the wood gets soaked.
I can't straighten the frame without a forge. The bends are too sharp for cold working.
reactions: Wet firewood and a bent frame: the cost of one visit.

statCheck.pass: Canvas flapping in the wind sounds like wings to them and triggers a hunting response. Securing the edges taut so the canvas doesn't billow prevents the attack.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they had against the awning. Maybe they just like tearing things.

entries.intro: {mob_type} chewed the bristles off the boot brush by the door. Flat as a board now.
details: I've been scraping mud off my boots with a stick instead.
They left the handle intact, just the bristles: every single one.
reactions: A new brush is on the list. Low priority, high annoyance.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} found the clay pots stacked behind the shed and smashed every one. Eight pots: all fragments.
details: The shards are mixed with the garden soil now. I'll be pulling pottery out of the beds for years.
Those pots took an afternoon to throw and a day to fire. Gone in minutes.
reactions: I'll make more and store them inside this time. Clay is cheap: my time is not.

statCheck.pass: Stacked pots trap moisture and insects between them. They knock the stacks over to get at the beetles and slugs hiding in the gaps.
statCheck.fail: I don't think they have anything against pottery. Probably just bumped into them.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps perching on the chimney and dropping things down it. Sticks, mostly, but also a snail once.
details: I've installed a wire mesh cap twice. It pulls the mesh off both times.
The snail landed in the soup pot. Discovered that at dinner.
reactions: I check the pot before eating now. A precaution I never thought I'd need.

entries.intro: Haven't seen {mob_type} tracks near the {poi_type} in over a month. The path is clear for the first time I can remember.
details: People are walking the path again without looking over their shoulders.
The ground has smoothed over where their tracks used to be. No fresh prints at all.
reactions: I'll walk that path tonight. It's been too long since I did it without watching the shadows.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} fell into the pickle barrel through a gap in the lid. Spent the night marinating.
details: I pulled it out in the morning: soaked in brine, bewildered, and very salty.
The pickles are fine. The creature ran off smelling like vinegar.
reactions: The lid fits tight now. One pickling incident is enough.

entries.intro: {mob_type} have been pushing loose stones off the garden wall onto the path. One at a time, through the night.
details: Every morning there's a stone or two on the walkway. I stub my toe in the dark.
The wall is shrinking slowly. A stone a night adds up.
reactions: I'm mortaring the stones in place. They'll have to push harder than gravity allows.

statCheck.pass: They push the top stones off to lower the wall height until they can step over it. Once the wall drops below their shoulder height, they'll cross freely.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if they're doing it deliberately or just bumping the wall as they pass.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has learned that the kitchen door sticks in humidity. Pushes it open on damp days and walks right in.
details: On dry days the latch catches and it can't get in. On damp days: the creature knows.
I've found it inside three times this week. Always near the bread shelf.
reactions: I planed the door so it closes flush. The creature pushed on it yesterday and looked surprised.

statCheck.pass: It remembers which conditions let the door open and tests it each time conditions change. Consistent latching is the only fix: they don't forget a weakness once they've found it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how it knows when the door will give. Maybe it tries every day and I only notice the successes.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} broke the garden sundial. Knocked the gnomon right off the base.
details: The gnomon was bronze: now it's a bent rod in the grass. The base is cracked.
It wasn't useful most days anyway. But it was mine and now it's broken.
reactions: I'll glue the gnomon back. It'll tell wrong time, but it'll look like a sundial again.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} drank from the paint jar I left out and walked away with blue paw prints. A trail of blue across the yard.
details: The paint is on the fence, the stones, the side of the house. Everywhere it stepped.
I followed the trail to the hedge, where it ends. The creature is in there somewhere, probably blue.
reactions: I'll cover paint from now on. The yard does not need decorating.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} knocked over the barrel I use to collect rainwater and it rolled downhill into the neighbor's fence.
details: The barrel dented the fence and broke two pickets. Now two yards need repair.
The neighbor was understanding about it. The fence was old anyway.
reactions: I owe a fence repair and a barrel retrieval. Both uphill.

statCheck.pass: They tip barrels to drink from the spill, and on a slope, tipping becomes rolling. A flat stone platform under the barrel prevents both.
statCheck.fail: Could've been accidental or deliberate: same result either way.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been pressing itself flat against the warm hearthstones on the outside wall. It goes completely still.
details: When the fire is going, the stones warm through. The creature spreads out like melted butter.
It's so flat against the wall it's almost invisible. Nearly sat on it once.
reactions: I check the wall before leaning on it now. Warm stones attract creatures and I attract surprises.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pulled the measuring stakes out of the field I'd just surveyed. Every single stake, pulled and dropped.
details: The string lines are tangled in the grass. The whole layout has to be re-measured.
Two hours of careful measuring: undone by creatures that don't care about property lines.
reactions: I'll drive the stakes deeper next time. And measure when they're less likely to visit.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got trapped in the hallway and panicked. Scratched every surface trying to get out.
details: The walls, the floor, the doorframe: all clawed. Plaster dust everywhere.
They were in and out in maybe a minute. The damage looks like an hour's work.
reactions: The hallway needs replastering. One visit, one complete renovation.

entries.intro: The neighbor's trick of planting marigolds along the fence seems to actually deter {mob_type}. I've started doing the same.
details: The scent must bother them. Tracks approach the marigold line and turn away.
My garden is bordered in yellow now. Not the look I planned, but effective.
reactions: I'll take ugly and effective over pretty and trampled.

statCheck.pass: Marigold roots release a compound into the soil that repels burrowing pests, and the above-ground scent deters browsers. It works on two levels at once.
statCheck.fail: Something about the flowers puts them off. I just plant them and don't ask why.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been using the flat stone by the well as a place to dry mushrooms. Lays them out in rows.
details: Little mushroom caps, evenly spaced, drying in the sun. Very organized.
If I move them, they're replaced by the next day. Same stone, same layout.
reactions: The creature is more disciplined about food preservation than most people I know.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pulled the thatch right off the corner of the roof. I woke up to rain on my face.
details: The thatch is in the yard, shredded. A hole the size of a barrel lid in the roof.
I patched it with oilcloth for now. It flaps in the wind and doesn't inspire confidence.
reactions: Re-thatching a corner takes a full day. And that's if the weather holds.

statCheck.pass: They pull thatch because insects nest in the straw layers and the creatures can smell them. A layer of clay daub under the thatch blocks the scent.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they wanted with the thatch. Maybe nothing: maybe they just like pulling things.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has taken to sleeping on the garden gate. The hinge is bowing from the weight.
details: Every morning the creature is draped over the top rail. Peaceful as anything.
The gate doesn't close properly anymore. The hinge pin is bent.
reactions: I could fix the hinge, but the creature would just bend the new one too.

statCheck.pass: It sleeps on the gate because the wood absorbs the day's heat and stays warm through the night. A flat stone shelf nearby, placed in the sun, would be warmer and spare the hinge.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it picked the gate. Plenty of flat surfaces around.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} dug a trench alongside the path. Perfectly straight, knee-deep, and completely unnecessary.
details: The dirt is piled neatly on one side. They're tidy diggers, I'll give them that.
I stepped into it in the dark and twisted my ankle. Healed now, but I'm still bitter.
reactions: I filled it in. I expect they'll dig it again.

statCheck.pass: They dig along hard-packed paths because the edge between compacted soil and loose soil is where grubs concentrate. The straighter the path, the straighter the trench.
statCheck.fail: I can't imagine why they'd dig a perfect line. But here it is.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has figured out how to open the butter crock. Lifts the lid, helps itself, replaces the lid.
details: I only noticed because the butter level was dropping faster than I was using it.
Tiny claw marks in the butter surface. Very polite theft, but theft all the same.
reactions: I've started weighing the crock. The numbers don't lie even if the lid looks undisturbed.

statCheck.pass: It learned by watching: if it sees you open a container the same way three or four times, it copies the motion. Changing the lid mechanism resets the learning.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how it figured out the lid. Clever little creature.

entries.intro: {mob_type} bent the weather vane on the roof into a right angle. It points at the ground now.
details: The arrow is folded in half: straight down. Useless for wind direction.
Getting up there to fix it means borrowing a tall ladder. I hate tall ladders.
reactions: The wind blows the same whether the vane works or not. But it bothers me.

statCheck.pass: The spinning vane catches moonlight and the reflections attract moths, which draw the creatures to the roof. A dull-painted vane doesn't reflect and gets ignored.
statCheck.fail: No idea why they bent it. Maybe the squeaking annoyed them as much as it annoyed me.

entries.intro: {mob_type} have been dropping stones into the boots I leave on the porch overnight. Three or four per boot.
details: I've learned to shake the boots before putting them on. Stones and toes don't mix.
The tracks around the porch tell the story: they visit, deposit, and leave.
reactions: I bring the boots inside now. The porch belongs to them.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} knocked the beehive off its stand. Bees everywhere, honey on the ground, and chaos for two hours.
details: The bees were furious. I couldn't go near the yard until they settled.
Most of the honeycomb is ruined: crushed and mixed with dirt.
reactions: The bees rebuilt. The hive is back on the stand with heavier bracing.

statCheck.pass: They knock hives to scatter the bees and eat the comb while the swarm is disoriented. A weighted lid and staked base makes the hive too stable to topple.
statCheck.fail: Whether they wanted the honey or it was an accident: either way, same mess.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been stacking twigs on the windowsill in a very specific pattern. Not a nest: something else.
details: The twigs are alternating sizes, laid like a tiny fence. Deliberate and careful.
When I move them, the pattern is rebuilt within a day. Same arrangement.
reactions: I've stopped moving them. The creature clearly has a plan, and I'm just in the way.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} ripped the handle off the well crank. Just the handle: twisted it right off the shaft.
details: Drawing water now means gripping a bare metal rod. It hurts the hands.
The handle was iron. They bent it like a green branch.
reactions: A new handle is forged and waiting. I'm bolting this one on, not pinning it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been following the cat around the yard like a student. Copies everything the cat does.
details: The cat sits: the creature sits. The cat grooms: the creature tries to groom.
The cat has noticed and is deeply unimpressed. But it tolerates the company.
reactions: Two creatures loafing in the sun, one copying the other. My yard has a mentorship program.

entries.intro: {mob_type} ate the soap off the washstand. The whole cake: gone.
details: There's tooth marks in the dish where the soap used to be. They bit the dish too.
Soap is made with tallow, so I suppose it smells like food to them.
reactions: I keep the soap inside now. Losing soap to creatures is a problem I didn't anticipate.

statCheck.pass: The tallow in hard soap is what draws them. Switching to ash-lye soap without animal fat eliminates the attraction entirely.
statCheck.fail: Something about the soap appealed to them. I don't want to know what.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps sitting in the empty bucket by the door. Fits perfectly and doesn't want to leave.
details: I need the bucket, but the creature is in the bucket. We're at an impasse.
Tilting the bucket doesn't help: it braces itself against the sides.
reactions: I've started using a different bucket. This one belongs to the creature now.

statCheck.pass: The bucket walls block wind on all sides and the metal holds ground warmth. It's the most sheltered spot at ground level: a purpose-built den from its perspective.
statCheck.fail: I can't tell what it likes about the bucket. Comfort, maybe.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} chewed through the rope clothesline and everything fell in the mud. Clean sheets, filthy.
details: The laundry landed face-down in the one muddy patch in the yard.
Rewashing everything took the rest of the morning. Time I'd set aside for other work.
reactions: Wire line from now on. They can chew on wire and see how they like it.

statCheck.pass: The rope absorbs soap residue from the damp laundry and develops a salty mineral taste they seek out. A wire line coated in wax has no flavor and no appeal.
statCheck.fail: Something about the rope draws them. I don't know what: but wire should solve it.

entries.intro: {mob_type} rolled through the herb garden in the night. The basil is flat, the mint is crushed, but the rosemary survived.
details: They trampled the same patch where the fragrant herbs grow. The scent must attract them.
The soil is packed from their weight. I'll have to loosen it before replanting.
reactions: I replanted the basil. They'll flatten it again, but at least I tried.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} tore the shutters off one window and left them leaning against the house next door.
details: The hinges are ripped from the frame. The holes in the wood need filling before new ones go on.
The neighbor brought the shutters back. They're intact: just violently removed.
reactions: New hinges, new screws, same shutters. The wood is fine: only the attachment failed.

statCheck.pass: Shutters that rattle in the wind drive them to pull at the source of the noise. Securing the shutters flush so they can't vibrate stops the behavior.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they had against the shutters. The window was closed.

entries.intro: I watched a {wildlife_type} carry a walnut to the path, set it down, and wait for the cart to roll over it. Cracked it perfectly.
details: It placed the nut in the wheel rut and stepped back. Waited with visible patience.
After the cart passed, it collected the kernel. No wasted effort.
reactions: I've been cracking walnuts with a hammer like a fool. The creature found a better way.

entries.intro: {mob_type} peeled the bark off the fence posts. Every post, stripped clean.
details: The posts are white and naked now. They'll rot faster without the bark layer.
Bark shavings everywhere: in the garden, in the path, against the house.
reactions: I'll tar the posts. It's uglier, but it protects the wood and tastes terrible.

statCheck.pass: Bark beetles burrow between bark and heartwood, and the creatures strip bark to get at them. Removing the bark yourself before installation eliminates the food source.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they targeted the bark specifically. The posts are ruined either way.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps pulling threads from the drying rack and weaving them into its nest nearby. Colorful nest.
details: I can see blue and red threads woven into the sticks and grass. My laundry, in miniature.
The creature only takes bright colors. Leaves the brown and white alone.
reactions: The nest is honestly beautiful. I'd rather not lose more shirts to it, though.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} stacked rocks against the chicken coop door so it can't open outward. The chickens were trapped until I noticed.
details: Six stones, each the size of a fist, wedged tight against the base of the door.
The chickens inside were complaining loudly. That's how I knew something was wrong.
reactions: I moved the rocks. The door opens inward now: problem solved.

statCheck.pass: They block coop doors to keep the birds inside until the creatures are ready to dig under the walls. Switching to an inward-opening door defeats the strategy entirely.
statCheck.fail: Whether it was deliberate or coincidence, the chickens didn't appreciate it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been grooming the horse's mane while it sleeps in the field. The horse doesn't seem to mind.
details: The creature picks through the mane, pulling out burrs and tangles. Methodical.
I've saved time on grooming. The creature does half the work before I get there.
reactions: Free grooming service. I won't complain about that.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} rolled every turnip out of the garden and down the hill. Found them at the bottom in a pile.
details: They didn't eat them: just relocated them. Every turnip, rolled individually.
Carrying them back uphill took longer than growing them.
reactions: The turnips are replanted. I'm building a low wall at the garden's edge.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been napping inside the watering can. I pick it up to water the plants and it pours out along with the water.
details: The creature crawls in through the spout somehow. Comes out the same way: headfirst and damp.
It shakes itself off, gives me a look, and wanders away. Back in the can by the next morning.
reactions: I turn the can upside down at night now. The creature sits on top of it instead.

statCheck.pass: The metal can stays cool in the heat and the narrow opening limits airflow, so the inside is cooler than anywhere else in the yard. It's not being stubborn: it's being practical.
statCheck.fail: I have no idea why it squeezes in there. Can't be comfortable.

entries.intro: {mob_type} trampled the herb patch so thoroughly it looks like it was plowed. Not a single plant standing.
details: Weeks of growth, gone in a single night. The soil is packed hard from their weight.
I'll have to loosen the soil and start over. The roots are crushed beyond saving.
reactions: At least the parsley always grows back. Everything else: replanting.

statCheck.pass: Aromatic herbs draw insects at night, and they stomp the plants to scatter the swarm and feed in the confusion. Growing herbs in raised beds above their stomping height avoids the damage.
statCheck.fail: I don't think they came for the herbs. They just didn't care what was underfoot.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} sat on the freshly poured candles and left perfect paw prints in every one. Twelve candles, twelve prints.
details: The wax was just soft enough to hold the impression. Each print is clean and detailed.
The candles still burn fine. They just have a signature now.
reactions: I'm keeping one as a curiosity. The other eleven are getting remelted.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pushed the gate open wide enough for the goats to escape. The goats were halfway to the {poi_type} before I noticed.
details: Rounding up goats is not how I planned to spend my afternoon.
The gate latch was intact: they just lifted it. Clever and destructive.
reactions: I've added a pin latch that needs fingers to operate. The goats are home.

statCheck.pass: They lift latches by hooking a claw under the bar and pulling up. A drop pin that falls back into place after the gate closes is the only latch they can't defeat.
statCheck.fail: I didn't think they could work a latch. I was wrong.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps bringing dead beetles and leaving them on the doorstep in a neat row. A daily offering.
details: Six beetles this morning. All the same species, all belly-up, evenly spaced.
I sweep them away and more appear by evening. The creature is diligent.
reactions: I suppose I should be grateful. It clearly thinks it's helping.

entries.intro: {mob_type} tore the trellis down and the climbing beans collapsed into a tangled heap.
details: The vines are bent and broken. Some can be saved if I get them upright today.
The trellis poles are snapped in half. Not pushed over: snapped.
reactions: New trellis goes up this afternoon. The beans can't wait.

statCheck.pass: They push trellises to shake loose the insects that hide in the vine canopy. A freestanding trellis with weighted feet is harder to topple than a staked one.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they leaned on it, maybe they pushed it deliberately. Same broken trellis.

entries.intro: {mob_type} took the gloves I left on the fence. Both of them: gone by morning.
details: I found scraps of leather near their tracks by the hedge. Chewed to nothing.
Those were my only work gloves. Bare hands until I can replace them.
reactions: I bring everything inside at night now. Nothing survives on the fence.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} chewed through the garden hose at three different points. Three clean cuts: perfectly spaced.
details: The hose is in four pieces now. Patching three breaks is more work than replacing the whole thing.
They left the nozzle end alone. Just the middle sections, evenly apart.
reactions: I'm saving up for a proper pipe system. Hoses are temporary with these creatures around.

statCheck.pass: They chew hoses for the residual water trapped in the low points between supports. Draining the hose flat after each use removes what attracts them.
statCheck.fail: Three perfect cuts in a straight hose: I can't explain it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been sleeping in the flour sack and leaving a body-shaped impression in the flour. Like a snow angel.
details: It comes and goes through a gap in the pantry door I didn't know existed.
The flour is still usable: just needs sifting. And accepting.
reactions: I sealed the gap. The creature now sleeps on top of the sack instead.

statCheck.pass: Flour absorbs moisture and stays dry, which keeps its fur from matting. It's not sleeping in the sack for warmth: it's using the flour as a desiccant.
statCheck.fail: Maybe it likes the softness, or maybe the pantry is warm. I don't really know.

entries.intro: {mob_type} dug up the newly planted fruit tree and dragged it twenty paces from the hole. Roots and all.
details: The tree was barely established: new roots, still fragile. Now it's lying on its side in the grass.
Replanting it might work if I get it back in the ground today. If the roots haven't dried.
reactions: I replanted it and piled stones around the base. Digging through stones is harder than soil.

statCheck.pass: Fresh-turned soil and bone meal attract them: they dig where they smell recent disturbance. Watering the area heavily after planting washes the bone meal scent deep and makes the soil less interesting.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they wanted with the tree. They didn't eat it: they just moved it.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got into the knitting I left on the chair and dragged yarn across the entire yard.
details: Fifteen rows unraveled. The yarn is dirty and tangled beyond saving.
They chewed through three skeins. Wool scraps everywhere: fence, bushes, path.
reactions: I'm knitting indoors from now on. The porch is not a safe workspace.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} have been scratching at the cellar door every night. They can't get through, but I can't sleep through the noise.
details: The scratching goes on for an hour or two, then stops. Every night around midnight.
There are deep claw marks in the wood now. Another week and they'll be through the surface layer.
reactions: I nailed sheet metal over the lower half of the door. The scratching is louder, but they make no progress.

statCheck.pass: They can smell the stored root vegetables through the wood grain. A layer of pitch on the door seals the scent and they lose interest within days.
statCheck.fail: Something in the cellar interests them. I just wish they'd be quiet about it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been placing small stones on the doorstep in a line. A new stone every day, always at dawn.
details: The line curves slightly: it's following the angle of the morning sunlight.
Twelve stones so far. Each one smooth and flat, about the size of a coin.
reactions: I don't move them anymore. Whatever the project is, I want to see it finished.

entries.intro: {mob_type} shoved the cart into the ditch overnight. All four wheels off the ground.
details: The cart is on its side in the drainage ditch, one wheel spinning lazily.
Pulling it out needs at least two people and probably a rope.
reactions: The cart is back on the path. I'm chocking the wheels at night from now on.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been drinking from the cat's water bowl and the cat just watches. No territorial instinct whatsoever.
details: They drink at the same time sometimes, one on each side of the bowl. Peaceful coexistence.
The cat has stopped drinking from the bowl entirely now. Given it up.
reactions: I put out a second bowl, but the creature uses both and the cat stares at me like it's my fault.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} ripped the rain gutters off the workshop. Twenty feet of tin, bent and lying in the yard.
details: Without gutters the rain pours off the roof in sheets right where I walk in.
The tin is bent but salvageable. Straightening it and rehinging it is a half-day job.
reactions: I'd rather spend the half-day on anything else, but water damage is worse than lost time.

statCheck.pass: Tin gutters hum and rattle in a frequency they find irritating: they pull them down to stop the noise. Wrapping the gutter brackets in leather dampens the vibration.
statCheck.fail: Something about the gutters bothered them enough to tear them down. I'll never know what.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps catching fireflies and tucking them into the hedge. The hedge glows at night now.
details: It catches them one at a time and pushes them into the leaves. Very deliberate.
The glow is soft and scattered through the branches. Actually quite pretty.
reactions: I've started sitting outside after dark just to watch the hedge. The creature may be an artist.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pulled the lid off the well and dropped sticks into it. Had to fish them out before the water was drinkable.
details: A dozen sticks, some with leaves still on. Floating in circles at the bottom.
The lid is heavy oak. They must have worked together to move it.
reactions: I'm bolting the lid down with a lock. The well is not a toy.

statCheck.pass: They drop sticks into water to test the depth: if the stick floats, it's shallow enough to drink from. Covering the well completely stops the testing behavior.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if they were playing or trying to ruin the water. Same fix either way.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been sorting the pebbles in the garden path by color. Whites on one side, darks on the other.
details: The division is surprisingly clean. It must take hours of patient work.
Guests have commented on the decorative path. I take no credit.
reactions: The creature's taste in landscaping is better than mine. I'm leaving it.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} ate through the sack of seed corn and scattered the rest across the shed floor.
details: Half the seed is gone. The rest is mixed with dirt and chaff: maybe salvageable.
That seed was meant to plant the entire back field. I'll have to buy more.
reactions: Metal bin, tight lid, and stored inside the house. I'm not losing seed twice.

statCheck.pass: Dried seed corn concentrates its starch and emits a faint sweet smell through burlap. A sealed metal container eliminates the scent trail entirely.
statCheck.fail: They found it through the sack somehow. I thought burlap was thick enough.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has claimed the sunny spot on the front step. I have to step over it every time I leave the house.
details: It doesn't move. Not for me, not for the cat, not for anything.
The creature is there from mid-morning until the shadow reaches the step. Precisely timed.
reactions: I've adjusted my schedule around the creature. Easier than arguing with something that doesn't listen.

statCheck.pass: It's basking to regulate its body temperature after a cold night's foraging. On warmer days it arrives later and leaves sooner: once its core warms, it goes.
statCheck.fail: It just likes the sun, I suppose. Same as anyone.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got into the tool shed and rearranged everything. Every tool is in a different spot.
details: Nothing is missing: just moved. The hammer is where the saw was, the saw is where the nails were.
Finding anything takes twice as long now. My system is gone.
reactions: I put everything back. Took an hour to undo what they did in a night.

statCheck.pass: They move objects to check behind and beneath them for nesting insects. If you clear the walls and sweep regularly, there's nothing behind the tools to interest them.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they moved things without taking anything. Curiosity, maybe.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} fell asleep inside the helmet I left on the porch railing. I went to put it on and met resistance.
details: The creature was curled up in the crown, warm and deeply asleep.
I wore a hat that day instead. The helmet was occupied.
reactions: I bring the helmet inside now. Headwear is not a den.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pried the boards off the cold frame and the seedlings froze overnight. A whole tray, gone.
details: The seedlings were two weeks from transplanting. Brown and wilted by morning.
Starting over means two more weeks before the garden is planted. Everything is behind now.
reactions: I'm screwing the boards down and hoping screws are harder to pry than nails.

statCheck.pass: The cold frame traps warmth, and they can feel it radiating through the boards: they pry them to access the warm pocket underneath. Insulating the outside surface masks the heat.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they wanted the warmth, or maybe they just like pulling boards. Either way: dead seedlings.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps peeling strips of bark from the log pile and laying them flat on the ground in a circle.
details: The circle is about as wide as I am tall. The strips overlap neatly.
It works on this project every afternoon. Adds a strip or two, inspects the result, leaves.
reactions: I've been watching the circle grow for a week. I don't know the purpose, but I respect the commitment.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} knocked over the rain barrel and it rolled through the fence. Two problems in one.
details: The fence has a barrel-shaped hole in it now. The barrel is dented on one side.
The water soaked the ground into mud. The dog tracked it through the house.
reactions: Fence fixed, barrel back. I'm staking the barrel to the wall with a chain.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been using the stump in the yard as a dining table. Brings its food there, eats neatly, and leaves.
details: Shells, seed husks, and small bones accumulate on the stump surface. Meal remnants.
It brushes the scraps off with a paw before each new meal. Tidy creature.
reactions: I clear the stump occasionally. The creature looks at me as if I'm bussing its table.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} gnawed the rungs off the ladder leaning against the shed. Every rung, chewed through at the center.
details: The side rails are fine: just the rungs. Each one snapped in the middle.
Climbing to the roof now means borrowing a ladder. Mine is a frame without steps.
reactions: New rungs, lag-bolted this time. And the ladder stays inside when I'm done with it.

statCheck.pass: Horizontal rungs under tension from the rails develop micro-splits that collect moisture, and they gnaw damp wood to extract minerals. Rails without tension don't split and get ignored.
statCheck.fail: Every rung, perfectly centered. I almost admire the consistency.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} discovered the wind chimes and has been batting them with its paw all morning. Relentless jingling.
details: It sits under the chimes and swats each tube in sequence. Left to right, over and over.
The chimes are meant for a gentle breeze: not a creature with a schedule.
reactions: I moved the chimes higher. The creature stood on its hind legs and reached them anyway.

entries.intro: {mob_type} ate the cork stoppers out of every bottle on the shelf. Left the bottles standing: just the corks gone.
details: The preserved fruit is exposed to air now. It'll spoil within a week if I don't re-seal.
Finding cork is easy enough. Finding time to re-stopper twenty bottles is another matter.
reactions: Wax seals over the corks from now on. They can chew through cork but not wax.

statCheck.pass: Cork absorbs fruit vapors and develops a sweet taste over time, so they pull corks for the flavor, not for what's inside. Dipping the cork tops in wax masks the scent.
statCheck.fail: I can't fathom the appeal of eating cork. But twenty missing stoppers suggest the appeal is real.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} knocked the spice box off the shelf and scattered pepper across the kitchen floor.
details: There's pepper everywhere: the counter, the floor, the windowsill. The whole room smells sharp.
The spice box is cracked. Half the peppercorns rolled under the stove.
reactions: The pepper is mixed with dirt and fur. Throwing it out and buying fresh.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} shoved the outhouse door shut while I was inside. From the outside.
details: The door swings outward and they wedged a rock under it. I was trapped for an hour.
The neighbor finally heard me yelling and kicked the rock away.
reactions: I installed a latch on the inside. Never again.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has learned to tap on the window when the kitchen is warm. Just sits there, tapping, until I open it.
details: One tap every few seconds, patient and persistent. It knows I'll give in eventually.
It comes in, sits by the stove until it's warm, then leaves. Every cold evening.
reactions: I've stopped fighting it. When the tapping starts, I open the window.

statCheck.pass: It can feel the heat radiating through the glass and associates the tapping with the window opening. If you leave a warm stone outside the window, it'll warm itself there instead.
statCheck.fail: Smart creature. It knows where the warmth is.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pulled every stake out of the bean poles and the plants are lying flat. A whole row, collapsed.
details: The stakes are scattered across the field. The vines are tangled in the dirt.
Re-staking the beans while they're this tangled is slow, careful work.
reactions: Deep-driven stakes this time. If they pull those out, I'll grow something that doesn't climb.

statCheck.pass: They grip stakes and rock them to test for soil looseness, then pull the ones that give. Driving stakes a full arm's depth into undisturbed soil makes them immovable.
statCheck.fail: They pulled them out like weeds. I don't know how they get the leverage.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pulled up the garden markers I use to label plantings. I no longer know what's growing where.
details: The markers are scattered across the yard. Carrots, beans, onions: all out of order.
They chewed the stakes to points. The painted labels are tooth-marked.
reactions: I'll find out what's what when things sprout. The labels are meaningless now.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} haven't come near the garden since I started hanging old shirts on the fence. Apparently, laundry is terrifying.
details: The shirts flap in the wind and the creatures give the whole area a wide berth.
I've been adding more shirts. The fence looks ridiculous: the garden has never been safer.
reactions: A scarecrow with ambition, that's what I've built. And it works.

statCheck.pass: The intermittent flapping mimics wing movements, and the human scent in the fabric lingers for weeks. Together, it reads as a large, unpredictable presence they'd rather avoid.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why shirts work better than a scarecrow. But I'm not questioning it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been methodically plugging the drainage holes in the flower pots. Pushes pebbles into every one.
details: The plants are waterlogged: roots sitting in water with nowhere to drain.
I clear the holes, the creature plugs them again by the next day.
reactions: I'm drilling the drainage holes larger. The creature's pebbles fall through now.

statCheck.pass: It plugs holes to create small puddles underneath that attract insects, then feeds on whatever gathers at the drip spots. Remove the puddles and the plugging stops.
statCheck.fail: I can't imagine why it does this. Seems like a lot of effort for no reward.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pulled the chimney cap off and it landed on the cabbage patch. Crushed half the row.
details: The cap is cast iron. Threw it off the roof like it weighed nothing.
Smoke pours into the house now instead of up the chimney. Can't light a fire until it's fixed.
reactions: The cap is back on: bolted this time. The cabbages are less fortunate.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been methodically removing every worm from the garden and relocating them to the yard. One at a time.
details: It carries each worm gently in its mouth and drops it in the grass. No harm done to the worm.
The garden soil is losing its best workers. The yard is gaining them.
reactions: I put the worms back and the creature moves them again: a slow, polite war.

entries.intro: {mob_type} overturned the woodpile and scattered the logs across the yard. Three weeks of stacking, undone.
details: Logs everywhere: on the path, against the house, in the garden. A mess.
The logs are waterlogged from lying in the grass. They'll need to dry before they burn.
reactions: I'm re-stacking. Slower this time: tighter, against the wall, with bracing.

statCheck.pass: They topple woodpiles to flush out mice and insects hiding between the logs. Stacking tight against a wall with no gaps removes the shelter the pests need.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they were looking for something. Or maybe woodpiles are just easy to knock over.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been leaving one feather on the pillow every morning. Just one, always in the same spot.
details: It comes in through the gap above the bedroom door and places the feather before I wake.
Seven mornings, seven feathers. I have a small collection now.
reactions: I've started keeping the feathers in a jar. I don't know why: it feels wrong to throw them away.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} chewed through the rope that ties the gate shut and the pigs got out. Took all afternoon to round them up.
details: The pigs went straight for the neighbor's vegetable patch. Did significant damage before I caught them.
The rope was new: thick hemp. They bit through it like thread.
reactions: Chain and a padlock. Expensive for a pig gate, but cheaper than replacing the neighbor's vegetables.

entries.intro: I watched a {wildlife_type} spend twenty minutes trying to pick up a flat stone that was flush with the ground. Couldn't get its claws under it.
details: It tried every angle: scraped around the edges, pushed from the sides. The stone didn't budge.
Finally it gave up and sat on the stone instead. Looked defeated.
reactions: I know that feeling. Sometimes the thing you want just won't cooperate.

entries.intro: {mob_type} knocked the drying rack over and the jerky fell in the dirt. A week of curing, wasted.
details: The jerky is gritty with sand. Can't wash it: the moisture would undo the cure.
The rack was sturdy. They hit it hard enough to snap the crossbar.
reactions: I'm hanging the jerky from the rafters inside. Nothing gets cured outdoors anymore.

statCheck.pass: They can smell drying meat from a considerable distance, and the scent is strongest downwind at dusk. Curing indoors with the windows shut contains the smell entirely.
statCheck.fail: The smell probably drew them. Can't blame them for wanting jerky: I wanted it too.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pulled every peg off the clothesline. The laundry ended up in the mud.
details: The pegs are chewed and scattered. Some in the yard, some gone entirely.
The sheets were nearly dry. Now they need washing again.
reactions: I switched to metal clips. They can pull those off too, probably, but at least they can't chew them.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} flipped the water trough in the yard. The ground is a swamp and the trough is upside down.
details: The mud is ankle-deep where the water pooled. Walking to the shed is an expedition.
The trough is stone. Flipping it back is a two-person, lever-and-rope operation.
reactions: It's back in place and wedged with stones. The mud will dry in a few days.

statCheck.pass: They flip troughs to drink from the shallow puddle underneath, which tastes of minerals leached from the stone. Keeping the trough on a raised platform removes the accessible puddle.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if they wanted the water or just liked the destruction. Probably both.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has claimed the old boot by the door as a den. I can see its tail poking out the top.
details: The boot was too worn to wear anyway. Now it's insulated with grass and fur.
The creature hisses softly if I reach toward the boot. Territorial over my own footwear.
reactions: I moved the boot to the corner of the porch. The creature moved in and adjusted the furnishings.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} stripped every leaf off the bottom half of the hedge. Clean as a barber's work.
details: The hedge looks like it's wearing trousers: bare legs below, green top above.
The leaves are gone entirely. Not chewed and dropped: actually eaten.
reactions: The hedge will fill in again. It looks absurd in the meantime.

statCheck.pass: They eat the lower leaves in autumn because the sugar content increases as the plant pulls nutrients down for winter. Once the leaves are fully green in summer, they lose interest.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they stopped at halfway. Maybe they couldn't reach higher.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been filing its teeth on the whetstone I keep by the workbench. Wears it down faster than I do.
details: There's a groove in the stone where the creature works. A smooth channel worn into the surface.
The creature uses it in the early morning before I'm up. I only know from the fresh groove.
reactions: I flip the stone after it visits. We're sharing the whetstone, whether I agreed to it or not.

statCheck.pass: Its teeth grow continuously and need grinding down to stay even. The whetstone's grit is the right coarseness: too smooth and it wouldn't work, too rough and they'd avoid it.
statCheck.fail: I suppose creatures need to sharpen their teeth too. The whetstone is just convenient.

entries.intro: {mob_type} broke into the smokehouse and ate half the hanging sausages. Left the other half dangling by threads.
details: They bit through the strings: some sausages fell on the floor, some are still hanging by a fiber.
The smokehouse door is splintered at the bottom where they forced their way in.
reactions: Metal door, heavy latch. The sausages are worth more than the upgrade.

statCheck.pass: Smoke scent dissipates after a few days, but the fat renders and drips near the door, and they follow the grease trail. Collecting the drippings in a pan inside eliminates the trail.
statCheck.fail: Smoked meat smells good: I can't blame them. But I can build a better door.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been washing its food in the dog's water bowl before eating. The dog watches this with visible offense.
details: Berries, roots, a bit of something unidentifiable: all rinsed carefully in the bowl.
The water is murky by the time the creature is done. The dog refuses to drink it.
reactions: I change the water three times a day now. The creature's hygiene standards require it.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pulled the iron hinges off the barn door. Not the door: just the hinges.
details: The door is leaning in the frame, held up by nothing but its own weight.
Replacing iron hinges means a trip to the forge. There's nothing that heavy in my scrap pile.
reactions: The door leans and the barn is open. The hinges are somewhere in the field.

statCheck.pass: Iron hinges develop a mineral crust from weather exposure that they lick for nutrients. Oiling the hinges regularly prevents the crust and removes the attraction.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they wanted with iron hinges. They can't eat metal.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} sits on the fence post and screams at the sunrise every morning. Every single morning.
details: It starts the moment the light hits the post. One long, terrible scream.
The neighbors think it's my creature. It's not mine: it just lives on my post.
reactions: I've stopped hearing it, mostly. The neighbors have not.

entries.intro: {mob_type} left claw marks on the front door deep enough to read by touch. Three parallel lines, waist height.
details: The wood is gouged through the paint and into the grain. Sanding won't fix it.
The marks are old enough that the wood has dried around them. Nothing to do but fill and repaint.
reactions: I'm leaving the marks. Repainting a door for creatures to scratch again is a waste of paint.

statCheck.pass: They scratch doors to test for hollow spaces behind them: if the scratching sounds hollow, they investigate further. A solid-core door produces a dead sound and they move on.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they were sharpening their claws, maybe marking territory. The result is the same.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been pulling snails off the garden wall and stacking the shells in a pile by the gate. Efficient pest control.
details: The shell pile is growing daily. Must be thirty or forty in there.
The creature eats the snails and leaves the shells behind like receipts.
reactions: The lettuce has never looked better. I owe the creature a debt.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} dug under the fence and came up inside the chicken run. The chickens didn't lay for three days.
details: The hole is right under the fence line: a clean tunnel. They went in and came out the same way.
Nothing was taken or harmed. The chickens are just traumatized.
reactions: I'm burying stones along the fence line. They can dig through dirt, not through rock.

statCheck.pass: They tunnel under fences where the soil is soft from water runoff. Packing gravel along the drip line under the fence edge makes the ground too hard to dig.
statCheck.fail: I didn't think they'd tunnel under. Over or through: those I planned for.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been depositing pine needles on the welcome mat. A fresh layer every morning.
details: The mat is buried under needles. Sweeping it clear takes longer than the greeting it provides.
The nearest pine is fifty paces away. The creature carries them one mouthful at a time.
reactions: I've accepted the pine-needle mat as the new normal. It smells nice, at least.

entries.intro: {mob_type} ate the leather patches I'd prepared for the boot repair. Two pieces of good leather: gone.
details: The leather was oiled and cut to size, ready to sew. They ate it off the bench.
More leather means more tanning. More tanning means more days in broken boots.
reactions: I do my leather work inside now. The bench is for things I don't mind losing.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} stole the thimble right off my finger while I was sewing on the porch. Didn't feel a thing.
details: The creature was sitting beside me the whole time. Waited until I looked away.
I found the thimble later, wedged into a crack in the stone wall. A tiny silver treasure.
reactions: The creature has expensive taste and quick hands. I keep the thimble in my pocket now.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} pushed a dead tree over and it fell across the path to the {poi_type}. Nobody can get through.
details: The tree is too heavy to move without a saw and at least four hands.
People are going around through the field. Adds ten minutes to the walk.
reactions: We'll cut it up for firewood. At least something useful comes from the detour.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been visiting the garden at the exact same time every afternoon. I could set a clock by it.
details: It arrives, eats three leaves from the same plant, and leaves. Three: exactly.
The plant is thriving despite the daily harvest. Three leaves is apparently sustainable.
reactions: I've started having my afternoon tea at the same time. We keep each other company.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} ripped the scarecrow apart. The head is in the garden, the arms are in the field, the body is on the roof.
details: The straw is scattered like confetti. Rebuilding it means re-stuffing from scratch.
The old shirt I dressed it in is torn to ribbons. The hat survived: it's on the chimney.
reactions: The scarecrow didn't scare them. It offended them.

statCheck.pass: Stationary scarecrows lose their effect within a week as the creatures learn it doesn't move. Moving the scarecrow to a new position every few days keeps the illusion fresh.
statCheck.fail: The scarecrow was supposed to keep them away, and instead they destroyed it. Point: them.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has claimed the top of the scarecrow as its favorite perch. Sits there all day looking official.
details: The scarecrow's hat is slightly tilted from the creature's weight. Gives it a jaunty look.
Other creatures avoid the scarecrow now. A live sentry is more convincing than a stuffed one.
reactions: The creature does the scarecrow's job better than the scarecrow. Promotion earned.

entries.intro: {mob_type} ate through the wax seal on the preserved fruit jars. Every jar: lid popped, seal gone.
details: The fruit is exposed and already fermenting. The whole pantry smells like vinegar.
I sealed those jars last autumn. Five months of preservation undone in one night.
reactions: I'm moving the jars to a higher shelf. If they can reach those, I'll move to a tower.

statCheck.pass: Beeswax seals soften slightly in warm weather and release a faint honey scent they eat for the honey content. Paraffin seals have no scent and no food value.
statCheck.fail: They went through every jar like a checklist. Methodical creatures.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been carrying small flowers and dropping them into the rain barrel. A floating garden.
details: The barrel surface is dotted with petals and small blooms. New ones every day.
The water is tinted faintly yellow from the pollen. Still drinkable: just floral.
reactions: I've been scooping water from under the flowers. The barrel is prettier than it's ever been.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} have been digging up the stone path between the house and the shed. Pried out three flagstones.
details: The gaps are full of loose dirt and tripping hazards. Especially in the dark.
The flagstones are in the yard: too heavy to steal, too heavy to casually replace.
reactions: I set the stones back and packed gravel underneath. Harder to pry up from a gravel bed.

statCheck.pass: Moisture collects under flagstones and breeds the grubs they eat. Setting the stones on gravel instead of soil drains the moisture and starves the grub population.
statCheck.fail: I can't figure out why they dig up stones specifically. Maybe they like the weight.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} figured out the pantry latch and has been letting itself in at night. I find crumbs and a satisfied look.
details: It takes exactly one item per visit, never more: a polite thief.
I've tried three different latches. It learns each one within a day.
reactions: I've accepted a small pantry tax. The creature is smarter than my locks.

statCheck.pass: It watches you operate the latch and memorizes the motion. If you change latch type and operate it when the creature isn't watching, it takes much longer to learn.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how it keeps figuring the latches out. I'm running out of designs.

entries.intro: {mob_type} gnawed the handle off the shovel. Not the blade, not the shaft: just the grip, chewed to a nub.
details: The shaft is smooth where the handle used to be. No grip means blisters.
I wrapped the end in leather. Not as good as a proper handle, but functional.
reactions: A new handle means a trip to the woodworker. For now: leather and calluses.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been rearranging the stones around the fire pit into a spiral. Adds a stone every evening.
details: The spiral is getting larger: started tight and now it's as wide as my arm span.
The creature works after sunset, placing each stone with care.
reactions: I don't use those stones for anything else. The spiral can stay.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} snapped the axle pin on the wheelbarrow. It collapsed under a full load of manure.
details: The manure is in a pile where the wheelbarrow gave out. Halfway between the barn and the garden.
The pin was iron. They bent it until it cracked from metal fatigue.
reactions: I shoveled the manure the rest of the way by hand. The wheelbarrow needs a blacksmith.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} dug a small hole in the garden, laid a single acorn in it, and covered it up. Patted the dirt flat afterward.
details: I watched the whole operation from the window. Precise and methodical.
If I leave it alone, I'll have an oak tree in the carrot patch.
reactions: I moved the acorn to a better spot. The creature can have its tree: just not in my garden.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} ate every last berry off the bushes the night before I planned to harvest. Every bush, stripped clean.
details: I'd been watching those berries ripen for a week. The timing was perfect: theirs, not mine.
The bushes are bare: not a single berry left. Even the green ones are gone.
reactions: Next year I'm picking them one day early. One day is the margin between harvest and loss.

statCheck.pass: They smell the sugar increase as berries ripen and time their visits to peak sweetness. Picking at the first blush of color, even slightly early, beats them to the window.
statCheck.fail: They knew exactly when to come. I don't know how: they just did.

entries.intro: {mob_type} packed the drainage ditch with sticks and mud. The yard is slowly flooding.
details: The blockage is packed solid: sticks, dirt, and debris jammed tight.
I cleared it yesterday. They packed it again by morning.
reactions: I'm rerouting the ditch. They can block whatever they want: the water will go elsewhere.

statCheck.pass: They dam drainage channels to create shallow pools for drinking. A grated culvert lets water through but prevents them from packing debris.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they gain from flooding my yard. But they seem committed to it.

entries.intro: {mob_type} broke into the root cellar and ate half the stored onions. Left the potatoes untouched.
details: Onion skins everywhere: papery thin layers scattered across the floor.
The cellar door held, but they came through a gap in the stone wall I didn't know existed.
reactions: The gap is mortared shut. The remaining onions will have to stretch.

statCheck.pass: Onions release sulfur compounds that most creatures avoid, but these ones seek it out: the sulfur acts as a natural dewormer they instinctively crave. Potatoes don't have the compound.
statCheck.fail: They ate the onions and left the potatoes. I'd have done it the other way around.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps sitting on the eggs when the hen leaves the nest. Warms them faithfully until the hen returns.
details: The hen seems unbothered by the arrangement. Leaves the creature in charge and goes about her business.
The eggs are fine: warm and undamaged. The creature is surprisingly gentle.
reactions: I've got a deputy hen. Unpaid, unnamed, and oddly reliable.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pulled the pins from the gate hinges and the whole gate fell flat when I leaned on it.
details: The pins were on the ground, two paces apart. Like they were collected and placed.
I landed face-first on the gate. Bruised my knee and my dignity.
reactions: Welded hinges. They can't pull pins that don't come out.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been polishing a particular stone in the wall by rubbing against it every day. The stone gleams.
details: It spends about ten minutes on the stone each morning. Rubs its side back and forth.
The surrounding stones are dull and rough. The polished one stands out like a gem.
reactions: The creature has a favorite stone. I never thought I'd understand the appeal, but the shine is nice.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} tore the oilskin cover off the firewood and shredded it. The wood got soaked in the next rain.
details: Wet firewood won't burn properly for a week. Smoky fires and cold rooms until then.
The oilskin was new: bought specifically because the old one wore out.
reactions: I'm building a proper woodshed. A roof they can't tear off: that's the plan.

statCheck.pass: Oilskin traps warmth from sun-heated wood underneath and they pull it off to reach the warm surface. A rigid roof with airflow underneath doesn't create the same heat pocket.
statCheck.fail: Something about the cover bothered them. Or they just like shredding things.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been sitting perfectly still on the fence post every sunset, facing west. Every evening, same spot.
details: It arrives just before the light goes gold and stays until the last glow fades.
The creature doesn't eat, doesn't move. Just watches the sky.
reactions: I've started watching the sunset too. The creature might be onto something.

entries.intro: {mob_type} got into the ash bucket and tracked grey footprints through the entire house. Every room.
details: The footprints go from the kitchen to the bedroom to the front door and out.
Cleaning ash off floorboards takes lye and scrubbing. My hands are raw.
reactions: The ash bucket has a lid now. A heavy lid with a latch.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been carefully peeling the label off the jam jar and carrying it away. Just the label.
details: It picks at the corner until the paper comes free, then trots off with it.
Three jars, three missing labels. I no longer know which flavor is which.
reactions: I'm carving the names into the lids instead. The creature can't steal that.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} shoved the wheelbarrow into the pond. It's sitting at the bottom, visible through the water.
details: The wheelbarrow is too heavy to drag out without wading in.
I can see it down there, upright, as if parked underwater. Mocking me.
reactions: I waded in and got it. The water was cold, the mud was deep, and the morning was long.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps knocking on the door with its beak at the same time every morning. Better than any clock.
details: Three sharp taps, a pause, then two more. The same pattern every day.
If I don't acknowledge it, the tapping gets louder. It has expectations.
reactions: I open the door, nod, and close it. The creature seems satisfied with our routine.

entries.intro: {mob_type} ate the twine I was using to tie up the tomato plants. The plants are sprawling on the ground now.
details: The twine was hemp: apparently delicious to them.
Tomatoes on the ground rot faster. I'm losing fruit to mud contact.
reactions: Wire ties from now on. Less pleasant to work with, but inedible.

statCheck.pass: Hemp twine absorbs plant sap where it's tied and the fermented sap has a sweet taste. Cotton twine doesn't absorb the same way and gets left alone.
statCheck.fail: They eat rope. I don't know what else to say about it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been leaving a single berry on the windowsill every morning. Different kind each time.
details: Today it was a blackberry. Yesterday, something red I didn't recognize.
The berries are always ripe and perfect. The creature has good taste.
reactions: I eat them while the creature watches through the window, and I think it approves.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} tore the straw padding out of the cart seat and used it to line a burrow nearby.
details: The burrow entrance is ten paces from where the cart was parked. They didn't go far.
The cart seat is bare boards now. Every bump on the road goes straight to the spine.
reactions: I'll re-pad the seat. And park the cart in the shed from now on.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} got trapped under an overturned basket in the yard and just stayed: didn't try to escape.
details: I found it sitting calmly under the basket an hour later. Looking around like it was in a room.
When I lifted the basket it didn't run. Just blinked at me and walked away.
reactions: The creature has an untroubled soul. I almost envy it.

entries.intro: {mob_type} chewed through the fence and got into the orchard. Ate every low apple and left the cores hanging on the branches.
details: The cores are dangling from stems: bitten clean, nothing left but the seed case.
The low branches are stripped. Anything above their reach is fine.
reactions: I'm pruning the low branches. If they can't reach it, they can't eat it.

statCheck.pass: They eat the fruit flesh but spit the core back onto the branch because the seeds are bitter. The height of the lowest fruit is the only defense that consistently works.
statCheck.fail: They ate what they could reach. Simple as that.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps sheltering under the same leaf during rain. The same specific leaf, on the same plant.
details: It runs to the plant the moment the first drop falls. Tucks itself under the leaf and waits.
When the rain stops, it shakes once and goes about its day. Very orderly.
reactions: The creature has a rain plan. I usually just get wet.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been turning over every flat stone in the yard and leaving them flipped. Looking for grubs, probably.
details: The yard looks churned. Every stepping stone, every border stone: upside down.
Putting them back means lifting each one and resetting it in its bed. Dozens of them.
reactions: The stones are back but my back is not. Fair trade for a tidy yard.

statCheck.pass: Grubs concentrate under flat stones where moisture and warmth collect. Setting stones on a gravel layer drains the moisture and the grub population drops to nothing.
statCheck.fail: Grubs, I'm guessing. What else would be under a flat stone?

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been herding ants away from the sugar jar on the windowsill. Stands guard and swats them back.
details: The creature eats the ants it catches, so it's not pure altruism.
The sugar jar has never been cleaner of pests. An effective partnership.
reactions: I leave the jar where it is. The creature gets ants: I get pest-free sugar.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} ate the beeswax I'd melted for sealing. The pot, the wick, the whole pour: consumed.
details: The wax pot is licked clean. Not a trace left inside.
I was planning to seal twelve jars today. Now I'm rendering more wax instead.
reactions: The beeswax gets rendered indoors from now on. The smell travels too far outside.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been tucking grass blades into the gaps between the porch boards. Filling every crack.
details: Each blade is pressed in vertically, tight against its neighbors. The porch looks woven.
The creature works on it every evening for about an hour. Methodical.
reactions: The porch drafts less now. I'm not sure if that was the creature's intent, but I'll take it.

entries.intro: {mob_type} trampled the newly laid path and pushed every stone out of alignment. The whole thing is crooked now.
details: I spent a full day leveling those stones. The mortar hadn't set yet.
Every stone is shifted an inch or two: the path weaves like a drunk now.
reactions: I'm relaying it. This time I'll wait for the mortar to cure before leaving it unguarded.

entries.intro: I watched a {wildlife_type} try to carry a mushroom twice its size across the yard. Pure determination.
details: It dragged, rolled, and shoved that mushroom for twenty minutes. Made it about ten paces.
Eventually it ate some on the spot and carried the reduced remainder. Problem solving.
reactions: I respect the commitment. Sometimes the load is too big: you eat part of it and move on.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got tangled in the fishing nets hung up to dry and thrashed until the whole lot came down.
details: The nets are torn in a dozen places. Mending them takes hours of patient knotwork.
They freed themselves eventually and left. The nets did not fare as well.
reactions: I'm drying nets on the roof from now on. Harder to reach means fewer entanglements.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been collecting dandelion seeds and piling them against the base of the house. A growing mound of fluff.
details: The pile is soft and white: looks like a tiny snowdrift against the stone wall.
The creature adds to it every afternoon. Brings seeds one mouthful at a time.
reactions: I'll have a forest of dandelions next spring. The creature is planting my entire yard.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pushed the garden bench over and it cracked down the middle. Split right along the seat.
details: The bench was old and the wood was dry: it didn't take much force.
Two halves of a bench are not a bench. They're firewood.
reactions: I'll build a new one. Heavier wood this time: something that fights back when pushed.

statCheck.pass: They push over benches and furniture to check underneath for burrowing insects. Raising the bench on stone footings removes the dark, damp space underneath that attracts the insects.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they pushed it. Could've walked around it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} figured out the bread box latch and has been taking exactly one crust per night. Never the whole loaf.
details: I've been finding the loaf with a missing end every morning. Clean bite, no mess.
The latch is lifted, the box is opened, one crust is taken, and the box is closed. Routine.
reactions: I bake a slightly larger loaf now. The creature gets its crust: I get mine.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} scattered the compost pile across the entire back yard. Eggshells and vegetable scraps everywhere.
details: The compost was almost ready to use: well-rotted and rich. Now it's spread thin over twenty paces.
Raking it back into a pile is unpleasant work. The smell doesn't improve with distribution.
reactions: I'm building a compost bin with walls. Open piles invite trouble.

statCheck.pass: They scatter compost to reach the beetles and worms concentrated at the bottom. A sealed bin with no ground access starves the insects and removes the incentive.
statCheck.fail: They tore through the pile like it contained treasure. Maybe to them, it did.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps picking burrs off the dog and dropping them in a neat pile. The dog holds still for the whole operation.
details: The burr pile grows after every session. The dog looks relieved afterward.
The creature is more thorough than I am: gets the ones behind the ears and under the collar.
reactions: The dog now goes to the creature before it comes to me. I've been replaced.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} gnawed the spoke of the cart wheel until it snapped. The wheel collapsed when I loaded the cart.
details: The load shifted and half the cargo fell off the side. Took two trips to recover it.
The spoke was chewed to a point: they worked on it from both sides.
reactions: I check every spoke before loading now. A habit I resent but can't afford to skip.

statCheck.pass: Wooden spokes under compression develop hairline splits that trap moisture, and they chew at the damp spots. Sealing the spokes with linseed oil prevents the moisture and the interest.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why that spoke in particular. Maybe it was the easiest to reach.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} stole the coin I left on the windowsill as a good-luck charm. Took it right off the ledge.
details: It was a copper piece: nothing valuable, but it was there for years.
I found the coin in the creature's stash under the hedge. Among buttons, bits of glass, and a key I lost last year.
reactions: I got the key back, so the trade was in my favor.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pulled the tin flashing off the base of the chimney. Water gets in when it rains now.
details: The flashing is crumpled in the yard: thin metal, easy to bend, apparently easy to pull.
Water runs down the inside of the chimney wall and pools on the hearth. Slow damage.
reactions: The flashing is back on: nailed and sealed with tar. The roof shouldn't be a creature playground.

statCheck.pass: Tin flashing conducts heat from the chimney and they press against it for warmth. Securing it with concealed fasteners keeps it flat and unpullable.
statCheck.fail: Something about the flashing drew them. I just want it to stay put.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} learned that the milk jug sweats in heat and licks the condensation off the outside. Doesn't touch the milk.
details: It sits beside the jug on warm afternoons and licks the beaded water. Delicate work.
The jug is cleaner on the outside than anything I own.
reactions: I can't fault the creature's restraint: it wants water, not milk.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} chewed the bell off the goat's collar. The goat wanders silently now and I can't find it by sound.
details: The bell is in two pieces: they bit through the mounting ring.
The goat doesn't seem to miss the bell. I miss the bell enough for both of us.
reactions: New bell, stronger ring, and a leather guard over the mount.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been standing guard over the vegetable patch at night. Nothing else comes near.
details: I don't know what's in it for the creature. Maybe the bugs, maybe the warmth of the mulch.
The garden has had zero pest damage since the creature took its post.
reactions: I leave scraps by the garden edge as payment. The arrangement seems fair.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} broke the handle off the pump. The well pump: the only source of water for two households.
details: Carrying water from the stream until the handle is fixed. Three trips a day, each one uphill.
The handle was cast iron. They bent it past the breaking point.
reactions: The blacksmith promised a new handle by tomorrow. Today: the stream.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been arranging fallen leaves into a pattern on the front step. New pattern every day.
details: Some days it's a circle, some days a line, some days something I can't describe.
The creature selects each leaf carefully: holds it, considers it, places it.
reactions: I step over the patterns when I leave. Feels rude to walk through someone's work.

entries.intro: {mob_type} ate the tallow candles off the outdoor table. All six of them, wicks and all.
details: There's wax residue on the table but the candles themselves are gone entirely.
I was using those for evening light while working late. The yard is dark now.
reactions: Beeswax candles are more expensive, but less tasty to creatures. Apparently.

statCheck.pass: Tallow candles are rendered animal fat: to them it's a dense, shaped food block with a convenient string handle. Beeswax doesn't register as food.
statCheck.fail: They ate candles. I didn't think candles were food: the creatures disagree.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been carefully peeling moss off the stone wall and draping it over the fence to dry.
details: Long strips of moss, laid flat like laundry. It comes back for them when they're dry.
I don't know what it does with dried moss. Nesting material, probably.
reactions: The wall is cleaner than it's been in years. The creature is maintaining my property.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} tore the netting off the berry bushes and ate everything underneath. The entire harvest: gone.
details: The netting is tangled in the branches. Removing it without cutting the bushes will take patience.
Not a single berry left. They were thorough.
reactions: Sturdier netting, staked into the ground. If they get through that, I'll grow something they don't like.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} found the mirror I propped against the wall for shaving and has been staring into it every morning.
details: It approaches slowly, looks at its reflection, and tilts its head. Same routine daily.
The mirror has nose prints on it now. The creature gets very close.
reactions: I wipe the mirror after the creature's visit and before my own. We share the morning routine.

entries.intro: {mob_type} gnawed through the rope handles on the storage chest. The chest is effectively sealed now: can't open it without tools.
details: The ropes were the only way to lift the lid. Without them, the lid sits flush and unmovable.
I had to pry it open with a chisel to get at the contents.
reactions: Iron ring handles, bolted through. They can gnaw iron if they want: good luck.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been visiting the compost heap and eating only the apple peels. Picks through the scraps, takes the peels, leaves the rest.
details: The compost looks slightly sorted after its visits. Peels removed, everything else untouched.
It's the pickiest eater I've ever seen. Even among creatures.
reactions: I've started putting the apple peels on top where they're easy to reach. Saves the creature the trouble of digging.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} scratched the painted sign right off the front of the workshop. Name, trade, everything: scraped to bare wood.
details: The paint is in curls on the ground. Years of weather hadn't faded it: one visit from them erased it.
Repainting means buying pigment, mixing it, and spending a day with a fine brush.
reactions: I'll repaint. A workshop without a sign is just a shed.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been lining its nest with wool from the sheep fence where fleece catches on the wire.
details: Every tuft of wool gets collected within hours of appearing. The fence is spotless.
The creature's nest must be the softest thing in the countryside.
reactions: Free wool, free cleaning. The creature and the fence have a good arrangement.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} chewed the rope off the bell that summons people for meals. No one came for dinner.
details: I was ringing a silent bell for five minutes before I looked up and saw the rope was gone.
The bell is still on the post. The rope is in shreds on the ground.
reactions: I walked to every door and knocked. Slower than a bell, but effective.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} found the ball of yarn I dropped and dragged it across the entire yard.
details: There's a yarn trail from the porch to the hedge, around the well, and back to the fence.
The yarn is chewed in several places and tangled through the bushes.
reactions: I salvaged what I could. Most of it is ruined: stretched, dirty, and bitten through.

entries.intro: {mob_type} knocked the sundial off its pedestal and used the flat disc as a resting spot. Found them sleeping on it.
details: The dial face is scratched from their claws. The hour markings are half-gone.
The pedestal is fine: the dial just lifted off. Apparently it wasn't as heavy as I thought.
reactions: I cemented the dial to the pedestal. They can sleep somewhere else.

entries.intro: {mob_type} have been packing debris into the rain gutter. Sticks, leaves, and stones wedged tight.
details: The gutter is blocked solid. Water runs over the edge instead of through the downspout.
I cleaned it out last week and it's full again. They work fast.
