reactions: I'm fitting mesh covers. Let them try to pack debris through wire.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} trampled the seedbed I'd just raked smooth. Footprints everywhere: deep ones.
details: The soil is compacted where they stepped. Seeds in those spots won't sprout properly.
Re-raking and re-seeding half the bed is the only option. The prints are too deep to ignore.
reactions: I'll plant at dawn when they've gone. Timing is everything now.

statCheck.pass: Freshly turned soil releases a mineral scent that signals easy digging to them. Watering the seedbed immediately after raking washes the scent into the ground.
statCheck.fail: Fresh soil must interest them somehow. Or they just don't look where they walk.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has discovered that the chimney is warm in winter and sleeps pressed against the stones above the fireplace.
details: It's there every cold morning, flattened against the warm stones. Perfectly content.
Soot collects on its fur. It doesn't care.
reactions: I check for the creature before I stoke the fire now. Don't want surprises for either of us.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} dragged the welcome mat off the porch and halfway across the yard. Dropped it in the flower bed.
details: The mat is muddy and crumpled. The flowers underneath are crushed flat.
I don't know what use they had for a mat. They left it when they got bored.
reactions: The mat is washed and back. The flowers are not as easily recovered.

entries.intro: {mob_type} have been wallowing in the water trough every night. The water is filthy by morning.
details: Mud, fur, and debris floating on the surface. The livestock won't drink from it.
I scrub the trough and refill it. By the next morning: same mess.
reactions: I'm building a cover that lifts for the animals but stays shut at night.

statCheck.pass: They wallow to cool down and dislodge parasites: the water temperature drops at night, which is when their skin itches most. A shallow decoy trough away from the livestock one splits their attention.
statCheck.fail: They need the water more than the livestock do, apparently.

entries.intro: {mob_type} broke the latch on the smokehouse, and the smoke escaped before the meat was cured. A batch of half-smoked fish.
details: The fish isn't spoiled, but it's not properly preserved either. Needs eating soon.
The latch was iron: they pounded on the door until the bracket cracked.
reactions: I'm smoking the next batch with the door braced from inside. More work: better results.

statCheck.pass: The smoke scent intensifies in the first hour and that's when they investigate. Starting the smoke at dusk when they're less active gives the scent time to dissipate before they notice.
statCheck.fail: The smoke drew them. I can't smoke meat in secret, apparently.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} found the seed I planted in the pot and dug it up, examined it, and replanted it upside down.
details: The seed was a thumb's depth when I planted it. Now it's at the surface, inverted.
The creature patted the soil back down after its inspection. Tidy vandalism.
reactions: I fixed the seed. The creature will probably fix it back.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} haven't touched the garden since the cat started sleeping out there. First quiet season in years.
details: The cat does nothing but sleep in the sun. But something about its presence works.
There are tracks that approach the garden edge and turn away. The cat doesn't even wake up.
reactions: The cat's laziness is paying off more than my fencing ever did.

statCheck.pass: The cat's scent marks the garden perimeter and signals a territorial predator to anything passing through. As long as the cat refreshes the scent, the boundary holds.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why a sleeping cat works better than a fence. But I'll take it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been placing a single flower on the grave marker in the yard every few days. Different flowers each time.
details: The flowers are always fresh: wildflowers from the field beyond the fence.
The creature places each one carefully, then sits for a moment before leaving.
reactions: I don't know whose grave the creature thinks it's honoring. But I'm glad someone tends it.

entries.intro: {mob_type} dug under the henhouse and scared the chickens so badly they stopped laying for three days.
details: The tunnel came up right under the roost. Feathers everywhere the next morning.
Three days without eggs means three days of buying from neighbors. Expensive panic.
reactions: I buried flat stones under the henhouse floor. Digging through those will take more than one night.

statCheck.pass: They dig toward the ammonia scent of droppings because it means insects underneath. A gravel layer below the coop drains the moisture and kills the insect population.
statCheck.fail: Something about the henhouse draws them. I just want them to stop digging.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been sorting the buttons in my sewing basket by color. I didn't ask for the help.
details: I left the basket open on the porch and came back to find bone buttons separated from brass ones.
The creature does this every time I leave the basket out. It ignores everything else.
reactions: My buttons are more organized than they've ever been. I can't complain about the method.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pulled the clothesline down and dragged three shirts into the mud. My good shirts.
details: The line was strung between two posts: they walked right through it at chest height.
Mud stains on linen don't wash out easily. The shirts might be done for.
reactions: Higher line, stronger posts. And the good shirts dry indoors from now on.

statCheck.pass: They walk the same path every night: the clothesline is at exactly the height where their backs brush it. Moving the line six inches higher clears their route.
statCheck.fail: They walked through it. That's all I know.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps stealing the soap off the washbasin ledge. Comes back for a new bar every week.
details: I've lost four bars this month. The creature carries them off like prizes.
I found one bar half-eaten under the hedge. Apparently soap is an acquired taste.
reactions: I keep the soap in a tin now. The creature sniffs the ledge and leaves disappointed.

entries.intro: {mob_type} knocked over the rain barrel and two days of collected water soaked into the ground.
details: The barrel is heavy when full. They must have really leaned into it.
The garden needed that water. Now I'm hauling buckets from the well instead.
reactions: The barrel sits in a cradle now, braced on three sides. Harder to topple.

statCheck.pass: The barrel collects warmth from the sun all day and radiates it at night: they lean against it for the heat. A stone barrier between them and the barrel stops the contact.
statCheck.fail: They bumped it. Whether on purpose or accident, the result is the same.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been sleeping in my hat every night. I find it curled up inside on the hook by the door.
details: The hat is warm and shaped like a bowl: I understand the appeal.
There's fur in the brim every morning. I brush it out before wearing it.
reactions: I hung a second hat for the creature. It still prefers mine.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} stripped the bark off the fence posts. Every post, all the way around.
details: The posts are smooth and pale now: peeled clean like they were freshly milled.
Bark protects the wood from rot. Without it, the posts will need replacing in half the time.
reactions: I'm tarring the posts. Less appetizing, and they'll last longer.

statCheck.pass: The cambium layer under the bark ferments slightly and sweetens over summer. They strip bark from the sun-facing side first where the sugars concentrate.
statCheck.fail: I've no idea why they'd eat bark. Maybe they're not picky.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been carefully unraveling the edge of the rug I hung to air out. One thread at a time.
details: It sits on the ground and pulls threads with its teeth. Very patient work.
The rug's border has retreated an inch since the creature started.
reactions: I moved the rug indoors. The creature sat where it used to hang and looked confused.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} ate the leather hinges right off the garden gate. The gate swings free now.
details: Leather hinges are cheaper than iron: that's why I used them. Lesson learned.
The gate flaps in the wind. Everything I was keeping out can walk right in.
reactions: Iron hinges this time. More coin, but creatures don't eat iron.

statCheck.pass: Leather dried in open air develops a salt crust from the tanning process that they lick and chew through. Wiping the hinges with vinegar dissolves the salt.
statCheck.fail: Leather and creatures don't mix. I know that now.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pushed the entire woodpile over. Logs rolled across the yard like it was a bowling green.
details: The stack took an afternoon to build: split, sorted, and arranged by size.
Three logs ended up against the front door. I had to shove my way out in the morning.
reactions: I'm stacking against the wall from now on. One fewer side to topple.

statCheck.pass: They topple woodpiles to reach the insects nesting in the gaps between logs. Stacking tightly with no air gaps removes the insect habitat.
statCheck.fail: They pushed it over. That's all there is to it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been weaving grass into the handles of every tool I leave outside. A little bow on each one.
details: The shovel, the rake, the hoe: each one decorated with twisted grass.
The bows are tight enough that I have to pick them off before using the tool.
reactions: It's the thought that counts. Even if the thought is entirely the creature's.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been rolling in the freshly turned garden beds every morning. The rows are flattened.
details: I rake the rows smooth, and by dawn they're body-shaped impressions in the soil.
Seed placement means nothing when the ground's been compacted by a creature nap.
reactions: I plant after their morning roll now. Saves raking the same row twice.

statCheck.pass: Turned soil holds warmth longer than packed ground: they roll in it the way you'd lie on warm stones. Once the soil cools by midmorning, they lose interest.
statCheck.fail: They seem to enjoy it. That's all I can say.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} learned how to flip the lid off the butter crock and has been taking one lick each visit.
details: Just one lick: a small divot in the butter surface. Then the lid goes back on.
I know it's happening because the lick mark is the same shape every time.
reactions: I've accepted the arrangement. One lick is a reasonable toll.

entries.intro: {mob_type} chewed through the strap on the well bucket. The bucket's at the bottom of the well now.
details: I could hear it hit the water. A long drop and a hollow splash.
Fishing a bucket out of a well with a hook on a rope takes more patience than I have today.
reactions: Chain instead of leather from now on. An expensive lesson in creature-proofing.

statCheck.pass: Wet leather straps expand and develop a scent of rot at the stress point: they chew where the smell is strongest. Oiling the strap keeps the moisture out.
statCheck.fail: They chewed through it. Leather doesn't last outside.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been carrying pebbles up to the roof and dropping them down the chimney one at a time.
details: I hear a small click in the fireplace every hour or so. Another pebble arrives.
There's a growing pile of smooth stones on the hearthstones. Dozens now.
reactions: I sweep the pebbles out each evening. The creature restocks by morning.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} found the salt lick I put out for the goats and ground it down to nothing overnight.
details: The salt block was meant to last a month. Gone in one night.
The goats stood there the next morning licking the empty spot where the block used to be.
reactions: Salt blocks aren't cheap. I'm mounting the next one on a post the goats can reach but the pests can't.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps placing a specific leaf over the keyhole of the front door. Same leaf, same spot, every morning.
details: If I remove it, a new one appears within the hour. The creature watches from the fence.
The leaf is always the same kind: broad, green, freshly picked.
reactions: I've stopped removing it. The creature has opinions about my door, and I've learned not to argue.

entries.intro: {mob_type} tore the thatch off the corner of the shed roof. Rain gets in now and the tools are rusting.
details: The thatch is scattered across the yard: pulled out in clumps.
I covered the tools with a canvas sheet, but the shed needs proper patching.
reactions: Rethatching takes a day's work and a bundle of reeds. Another task for the list.

statCheck.pass: They pull thatch where it's already loosened by wind or rot: the weakest corner is always the first to go. Pinning the thatch with wire at the edges prevents them from getting a grip.
statCheck.fail: They wanted the thatch for something. Or they just like pulling things apart.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} figured out that tapping on the window gets my attention. Now it taps whenever it wants scraps.
details: Two taps means it's there. If I don't respond, it taps harder.
The window has tiny scratch marks from its visits. A record of every request.
reactions: I put scraps out before the tapping starts now. Easier for both of us.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} ate every single carrot top right down to the soil line. Left the roots underground, untouched.
details: A row of green stumps where the tops used to be. The carrots below are fine but they won't grow much bigger now.
They ate the greens so neatly it looks like someone trimmed the row with shears.
reactions: I'm harvesting what's left early. Better small carrots than no carrots.

statCheck.pass: Carrot greens are sweeter than most leaf crops and the sweetness intensifies in cool mornings. Harvesting the greens for my own use before the creatures come removes the target.
statCheck.fail: They like carrot greens. Simple as that.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} dragged my reading glasses off the table and buried them in the garden. Found them three days later.
details: Three days of squinting at everything. My head ached from the effort.
The glasses were in a shallow hole between the bean rows: lenses down, arms folded.
reactions: The glasses are fine, dirty but intact. The creature's motives remain unclear.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} shoved rocks into the spokes of the cart wheel. Had to pry each one out before I could move it.
details: The rocks were wedged tight: packed in like they meant it.
Took half an hour with a chisel. I should have been on the road by dawn.
reactions: I park the cart under cover now. If they can't reach it, they can't pack it.

statCheck.pass: They wedge rocks into moving parts to hold their scent longer. Greasing the spokes prevents the rocks from staying.
statCheck.fail: Rocks in spokes. I don't know their reasoning.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} have been using the scarecrow as a scratching post. It's leaning at an angle now.
details: The straw is falling out of the shirt where they've rubbed against it.
The scarecrow scares nothing when it's halfway to the ground.
reactions: I propped it back up. Give it a week.

entries.intro: {mob_type} broke into the root cellar and trampled every basket of turnips. Didn't eat them: just stomped around.
details: Turnips everywhere, cracked and bruised. The baskets are kindling now.
The cellar door was latched. They shouldered it open: the wood is splintered at the frame.
reactions: Iron brace on the door. And the turnips get eaten before they get trampled again.

statCheck.pass: Root cellars leak cold air under the door that draws them in hot weather. A tight-fitting sweep on the door stops the air leak.
statCheck.fail: They shouldered the door open. I need a stronger door.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been drinking from the flower vase on the windowsill and ignoring the bowl of water I set out for it.
details: The vase water is stale and full of stem bits. The bowl is clean and fresh.
Every morning the vase level drops and the bowl stays full.
reactions: The creature has preferences, and they don't align with common sense.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} peeled the oilcloth off the outdoor table and carried it away. The table is bare and weather-stained now.
details: The oilcloth was pinned down with four stones. They moved every stone first.
Oilcloth is hard to come by: I traded for that piece last autumn.
reactions: I'll use bare wood until I can get another piece. The table doesn't know the difference.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been stacking acorns on top of my boots every night. A small tower on each one.
details: I find two neat piles of acorns in my boots every morning. Six or seven each.
The creature is precise: the piles are always the same height.
reactions: I dump the acorns, put on my boots, and the creature starts collecting again. We have a routine.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got into the grain silo through a gap in the boards and ate their fill. Left droppings in the rest.
details: The grain they didn't eat is contaminated. Can't use it for bread, might work for animal feed.
The gap was barely hand-width, but they squeezed through or widened it.
reactions: I patched the silo and sorted the clean grain from the spoiled. Half a day gone.

statCheck.pass: They can compress themselves through gaps you'd think impossible. Any opening wider than a fist needs blocking: they test every crack along the ground level.
statCheck.fail: They got in somehow. I patched what I could see.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} found the wind chime and learned that nudging one tube makes a sound. It nudges the same tube every visit.
details: One note, over and over. The creature has a favorite pitch.
It sits beneath the chime and bats the tube with one paw, listens, and bats it again.
reactions: I've grown fond of the single note. It means the creature is visiting.

entries.intro: {mob_type} chewed through the rope holding the gate shut and let all the goats out. Took me an hour to round them up.
details: The goats scattered in every direction. Two ended up in the neighbor's yard.
The rope was thick hemp: they went through it like thread.
reactions: Chain and padlock from now on. The gate stays shut whether they like it or not.

statCheck.pass: Hemp rope absorbs animal scent and becomes a chew target once the scent saturates. Chain doesn't absorb anything and gets ignored.
statCheck.fail: They chew rope. That's the whole story.

entries.intro: {mob_type} polished the brass doorknob right off its mounting by rubbing against the door all night.
details: The knob is on the ground. The screws are stripped out of the wood.
I can't open the front door properly until the knob is remounted.
reactions: Longer screws, deeper holes. The knob will survive the next visit.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} trampled every seedling in the cold frame. Walked right over the glass and the frame collapsed under them.
details: The glass panes are shattered: the seedlings are crushed under broken shards.
I started those seedlings six weeks ago. Can't replace them this late in the season.
reactions: Next year the cold frame goes on the roof. They're not climbing up there.

statCheck.pass: Glass in sunlight concentrates warmth on the soil beneath: they walk across warm glass the way you'd step onto a heated stone. Cold frames without glass lids don't attract them.
statCheck.fail: They walked on it and it broke. The why doesn't matter much now.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has learned to untie the knot on the bread bag. Takes it about three minutes.
details: I watched it work the knot with its teeth and paws: patient and methodical.
It takes one piece of crust and leaves the bag open. I retie it: it unties it again.
reactions: I've switched to a clasp. The creature is studying the new mechanism.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got into the dye vats behind the workshop. There are blue footprints across half the settlement.
details: The trail goes from my workshop to the well, around the square, and into the field.
The dye is good quality: the footprints aren't fading. They might be permanent.
reactions: I covered the vats and the footprints will wear off eventually.

statCheck.pass: Dye vats left uncovered release a fermented smell at night that draws them from a distance. A wooden lid weighted with a stone contains the scent.
statCheck.fail: They found the vats. I should have covered them.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been using the broom as a scratching post. The bristles are bent sideways.
details: It backs up against the broom and rubs until the bristles splay out.
The broom still sweeps: mostly. It pushes dirt to the left now.
reactions: I bought a second broom. One for sweeping, one for the creature.

entries.intro: {mob_type} tipped the smoking rack and all the fish fell into the dirt. A week's worth of curing, wasted.
details: The fish were almost done: one more day and they'd have been stored safely.
Brushing dirt off smoked fish doesn't make it edible. The grit gets into everything.
reactions: The rack is bolted to the wall now. Nothing short of pulling down the house will tip it.

statCheck.pass: Smoked fish on an open rack releases oil droplets into the air: they smell it from a distance. An enclosed smokehouse with a proper door contains the scent during curing.
statCheck.fail: The smoke smell drew them. There's no hiding smoked fish.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps tucking its head under the edge of the blanket on the drying line. Just the head: the body stays outside.
details: It stands beneath the blanket and lifts the corner over its face. Stays like that for minutes.
When I take the blanket down, the creature moves to the next piece of hanging laundry.
reactions: I left a scrap of cloth hanging low for it. The creature accepted the substitute.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} pulled up every stake marking the new fence line. I spent all morning setting those.
details: The stakes are scattered around the field. Some were chewed to points.
The string I tied between them is tangled in a ball somewhere near the hedge.
reactions: I'll set them again. Deeper this time, pounded down flush with the ground.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been leaving chewed pinecone cores on the doorstep. A tidy pile of them, growing daily.
details: It eats the seeds off the scales and deposits the bare core on my step. Like a gift.
The pile is up to a dozen now. I'd move them, but they keep arriving.
reactions: I use them for kindling. The creature provides, I suppose.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} ate the wax seal off every preserve jar in the pantry. The preserves are still sealed, barely.
details: The wax is licked clean off the rims. Jar lids are loose and the seal is broken.
I need to re-melt and re-seal thirty jars before anything spoils.
reactions: Thirty jars and thirty wax seals: the afternoon is spoken for.

statCheck.pass: The preserving wax has sugar and fruit juice worked into it from the sealing process. Mixing a little ash into the wax before sealing adds a bitter taste they won't touch.
statCheck.fail: They like wax, apparently. I didn't know that was a thing.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been pestering the cat. The cat hides under the bed now and won't come out until evening.
details: They chase the cat across the yard every time it goes outside.
The cat used to keep mice out of the shed. Now it won't leave the house.
reactions: The cat's schedule revolves around theirs. Mice are winning.

entries.intro: {mob_type} got into the tool shed and scattered everything. Hammers, saws, nails: all over the floor.
details: Finding a specific nail in that mess took longer than the repair I needed it for.
They knocked the saw off its hook and it gouged the workbench on the way down.
reactions: I reorganized and put a proper latch on the door. Should have done that months ago.

statCheck.pass: Metal tools hold a residual warmth from the day's sunlight that they can feel through walls. Storing tools in an unheated shed with stone floors dissipates the warmth.
statCheck.fail: They got in and made a mess. The usual.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps lining up snail shells along the top of the garden wall. A perfect row of empties.
details: The shells are placed upright, open side down. Ten of them so far, evenly spaced.
The creature adds one every day or two. It selects each shell carefully before placing it.
reactions: Fewer snails in the garden and a decorated wall. The arrangement works for me.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} gnawed the legs of the outdoor bench until they're uneven. Sitting on it now is a balancing act.
details: One leg is a good inch shorter than the others. The bench rocks with every shift.
They chewed the legs at night: I sat down the next morning and nearly tipped over.
reactions: I shimmed the short leg with a flat stone. It wobbles less now, but dignity was lost.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} found the open jar of honey and left a single pawprint on the surface. Didn't take any: just touched it.
details: One perfect print in the honey: four toes and a pad. Pressed lightly and withdrawn.
I can't eat the honey now, but I almost don't want to wipe the print away.
reactions: I sealed the jar and the honey goes on bread tomorrow, print and all.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} knocked the lantern off the porch post and the glass shattered. No evening light for the front step.
details: The glass was beveled: took a craftsman half a day to make. The iron frame is fine but useless without glass.
I sat on the dark porch last night and listened to them rummaging through the yard.
reactions: New glass is on order. Until then, a candle in a jar does the job.

statCheck.pass: Lantern light attracts moths at night, and the moths attract them. Switching to a hooded lantern that casts light downward instead of outward cuts the insect draw.
statCheck.fail: Something about the lantern drew them. I'll hang the replacement higher.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been sitting on the exact center of the outdoor table every morning. Doesn't move when I come out.
details: It sits there like it owns the table. Looks at me as though I'm intruding.
I've started eating breakfast standing up. The creature has the table until it's ready to leave.
reactions: I tried sitting at the table once. The creature stared at me until I moved.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pulled the corks out of three bottles of vinegar on the back shelf. The whole pantry reeks.
details: The vinegar pooled on the shelf and dripped onto the flour sack below. Both ruined.
They didn't drink the vinegar: just pulled the corks and left. Mischief for its own sake.
reactions: The pantry gets a door now. A real door, with hinges and a lock.

statCheck.pass: Vinegar fumes escape around cork stoppers and they investigate the sharp smell. Sealing the corks with wax eliminates the scent leak.
statCheck.fail: They pulled the corks. Curiosity, probably.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been carefully placing feathers in a line from the hedge to my front door. One feather per day, extending the path.
details: The feathers are spaced evenly: about a handspan apart. Different kinds each day.
The path is twelve feathers long now. The creature adds one every morning.
reactions: I don't know where the path is going, but the creature clearly has a destination in mind.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} ate the seedlings right out of the window box. Every one: down to the stems.
details: The window box is on the second floor. They climbed for those seedlings.
Bare stems in a row like tiny sticks. The flowers never had a chance.
reactions: I'm replanting. And wondering how to creature-proof a second-floor window box.

statCheck.pass: Fresh seedlings have a sweet, tender stem that older plants lose as they toughen. Waiting until the stems harden before putting the box outside removes the temptation.
statCheck.fail: They climbed and they ate. The cycle continues.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} swept everything off the porch: pots, tools, the broom itself. Cleared like a table after dinner.
details: The pots are cracked, the broom handle snapped, and the tools are scattered in the yard.
They didn't take anything. Just knocked everything off and left.
reactions: I gathered what survived and stored it inside. The porch stays bare now.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} broke into the cold stores and trampled half the cheese wheels. Hoofprints in aged cheddar.
details: The cheese is cracked and dirty. Some wheels can be trimmed: others are a loss.
The door was barred from the outside. They came through a ventilation gap I thought was too small.
reactions: I'm covering the vent with iron grate. No gap is too small, apparently.

statCheck.pass: Aging cheese releases a strong scent through any opening: they follow the smell to the source. Charcoal filters over the vents absorb the odor before it escapes.
statCheck.fail: They found the cheese. I don't know how, but they found it.

entries.intro: {mob_type} have been walking along the rain gutter every night. The tin is dented and pulling away from the roof.
details: The gutter sags in two places where their weight bent it.
Water drips through the gaps when it rains. The wall underneath is staining.
reactions: I'll rehang the gutter with heavier brackets. The dents stay.

entries.intro: {mob_type} ate the leather washers off the pump handle. The pump leaks from every joint now.
details: Water sprays sideways instead of into the bucket. I get soaked every time.
Replacing leather washers means taking the whole pump apart. Half a day's work.
reactions: I ordered copper washers. Less comfortable to chew, I'd imagine.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been catching flies on the windowpane from the outside. Picks them off one by one.
details: It watches through the glass until a fly lands, then snatches it from the outer surface.
I can hear the tiny thump of its paw against the pane. A soft, regular rhythm.
reactions: Fewer flies inside and entertainment for the creature. I clean the paw prints off the glass once a week.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} tore the scarecrow apart. Pulled the stuffing out and left the clothes in a pile.
details: The shirt is ripped, the hat is chewed, and the straw filling is scattered across two garden beds.
The scarecrow was new this season. Lasted three weeks.
reactions: I'll stuff the next one with something they don't want to pull apart. If such a thing exists.

statCheck.pass: They go after straw stuffing because field mice nest in it: they're hunting the mice, not destroying the scarecrow. Stuffing with wool or rags instead removes the mouse scent.
statCheck.fail: They pulled it apart because they could. That's all the reason creatures need.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps wedging a stick into the latch of the mailbox so it stays open. Every day after I close it.
details: The same stick, too: I recognize it by the bark pattern. The creature retrieves it each time.
I don't know what the creature wants with an open mailbox. Maybe it likes the look of it.
reactions: I've stopped closing the mailbox. The creature won.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} gnawed the rungs off the ladder leaning against the barn. Three rungs gone: can't climb it now.
details: The rungs were pine: soft wood, easy chewing. The side rails are untouched.
Without those rungs there's a gap I can't step across. The hayloft is unreachable.
reactions: Oak rungs replacing pine. They can try their teeth on hardwood.

statCheck.pass: Pine rungs swell and shrink with humidity: the movement loosens them in the mortises, creating a gap that releases sap scent. Pegging the rungs through the rails locks them tight.
statCheck.fail: Soft wood and sharp teeth. The combination was inevitable.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been inspecting every nail head on the porch railing. Taps each one with its nose, moves to the next.
details: It goes left to right, never skipping one. The whole railing takes about ten minutes.
If a nail sticks up slightly, the creature spends extra time on it. Quality control.
reactions: I hammered down the loose nails after watching it. Felt like I was being supervised.

entries.intro: {mob_type} dug up the herbs I planted along the path. Pulled every one by the roots and left them wilting in the sun.
details: Rosemary, thyme, sage: all of it uprooted and tossed aside.
The roots were exposed too long. Most won't survive replanting.
reactions: Starting over with new cuttings. I'll plant them inside pots sunk into the ground this time.

statCheck.pass: Strong-scented herbs confuse their sense of smell near pathways they use as routes. They remove the plants to clear their familiar scent trail.
statCheck.fail: I suppose herbs aren't welcome in their path. Noted.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has taken to sitting on the journal I leave on the porch. Won't move when I reach for it.
details: It settles down on the leather cover and tucks its legs in. Very comfortable.
The pages are getting warped from the creature's weight. The binding is loosening.
reactions: I read indoors now. The creature can have the porch and its favorite seat.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} knocked the beehives over during the night. The bees are furious and the yard is a no-go zone.
details: I can hear the buzzing from inside the house. The swarm is thick around the toppled hive.
The honey frames are exposed and cracked. Half the season's honey is soaking into the grass.
reactions: I'll right the hive at dusk when the bees calm down. Until then, the yard belongs to them.

statCheck.pass: They knock hives for the brood comb underneath: the larvae are high in fat and protein. Setting hives on raised platforms with smooth metal legs prevents them from getting the leverage to tip them.
statCheck.fail: Honey or mischief: I don't know which drew them. Either way, the bees are angry.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been rearranging the stones around the fire pit into a wider circle. A little bigger every day.
details: The circle started as an arm's width and it's now twice that. The creature pushes one stone outward each visit.
The fire pit is becoming a fire arena at this rate.
reactions: I moved the stones back once. The creature moved them out again by morning.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pulled the nails out of the fence boards and the whole section fell flat. Just dropped into the grass.
details: The boards are fine: the nails are scattered in the weeds. Took an hour to find them all.
I don't know how they pull nails: teeth, probably.
reactions: Screws instead of nails this time. Let them try to twist those out.

statCheck.pass: Nails in fresh wood create a gap as the wood dries, and they grip the raised head. Countersunk nails sit flush and can't be gripped.
statCheck.fail: They pull nails. I can't explain the mechanics.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been building a dam across the drainage ditch with twigs and mud. Surprisingly effective.
details: The water is pooling behind the dam and the garden is getting soggy.
I cleared the dam yesterday and the creature rebuilt it overnight. Same spot, same design.
reactions: We're at an impasse: I clear it and the creature rebuilds it.

entries.intro: {mob_type} shredded the canvas cover on the hay wagon. The hay got soaked in last night's rain.
details: Wet hay molds fast. I'm spreading it to dry but half of it might be lost.
The canvas is in ribbons: they went through it like it was paper.
reactions: I'm getting a heavier tarp. Canvas was never designed for this kind of abuse.

statCheck.pass: Canvas coated with animal fat for waterproofing develops a rancid smell that attracts them. Plant-oil treated tarps have the same waterproofing without the animal scent.
statCheck.fail: They tore through canvas. I need something tougher.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been carrying exactly one berry to the doorstep every evening and waiting until I take it.
details: If I don't come out, the creature sits with the berry for an hour and then eats it itself.
The berries are always ripe. The creature picks well.
reactions: I try to come out promptly now. It seems rude to make the creature wait.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} chewed through the rope bridge over the irrigation ditch. It dropped into the water while I was crossing.
details: I went in up to the knees. The bridge planks floated downstream.
The ropes were gnawed halfway through: held until weight went on them.
reactions: I fished the planks out and rebuilt with chain. My boots are still drying.

statCheck.pass: They chew rope at the point where friction from movement has frayed and softened the fibers. Replacing rope with chain at any point that moves or flexes eliminates the weak spot.
statCheck.fail: The rope looked fine. It wasn't.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} found the pile of clean rags and has been nesting in them. The rags are no longer clean.
details: Fur, mud, and bits of leaf mixed into the cloth. I'll have to wash them all again.
The creature looks very comfortable. Chose the softest rags for its bed.
reactions: I moved the rags into a drawer. The creature sat on the empty shelf and looked betrayed.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} trampled the medicinal herbs I was drying on the rack outside. Ground them into the dirt.
details: Three days of picking and drying: gone. The herbs are mixed with soil and useless now.
I can still smell the mint and chamomile. That's the only thing left of the batch.
reactions: I'll dry the next batch in the attic. More stairs, fewer creatures.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps hiding behind the water jug and jumping out at the cat. The cat is not amused.
details: The creature crouches, waits, and springs. The cat yowls every time.
This happens three or four times a day. The cat has started avoiding the kitchen.
reactions: I moved the jug to the counter. The creature found a new hiding spot behind the bread box.

entries.intro: {mob_type} bent the weather vane until it points permanently east. The wind can't move it anymore.
details: The rod is kinked where they bent it. The vane is frozen in place.
Everyone who walks by asks why my weather vane says the wind is always from the east.
reactions: I'll straighten it when I get the ladder back. Until then, it's always easterly at my house.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been removing thorns from the rose bushes along the path. One thorn at a time, very carefully.
details: It grips each thorn and pulls it free, then drops it in the grass. Patient work.
The lower branches are smooth now. The upper ones still have thorns.
reactions: I haven't scratched my hands on the roses in weeks. The creature is earning its keep.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} ate the cork float off the fishing line. The hook sank and snagged the bottom.
details: I lost the hook, the float, and twenty paces of good line pulling it free.
They chew cork for the texture, not the taste: at least that's my theory.
reactions: Wooden float from now on. Harder to chew and cheaper to replace.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been standing on the fence post every dawn and calling once. Just once: then silence.
details: One clear call at first light. The same post, the same direction, every morning.
I've started waking to the sound. It's better than any bell.
reactions: I don't know what the creature is announcing. But I appreciate the consistency.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pushed the trough up against the gate so it can't open. Had to climb the fence to get into my own yard.
details: The trough is stone: too heavy to shove back without help.
I spent five minutes straddling the fence in my nightclothes. The neighbors watched.
reactions: Got a neighbor to help move the trough. It's chained to a post now.

statCheck.pass: They push heavy objects along the ground to scrape parasites off their underbellies. A rough-surfaced log set nearby gives them an easier target to push against.
statCheck.fail: They're strong enough to move stone troughs. That's the lesson.

entries.intro: {mob_type} packed sawdust and wood shavings into the door lock. The key won't turn.
details: I spent twenty minutes picking debris out of the mechanism with a needle.
The lock still sticks. There's sawdust deep inside that I can't reach.
reactions: I'm covering the keyhole with a flap. Simple problem, simple fix.

entries.intro: {mob_type} trampled the newly planted onion sets. Every single one pushed deeper into the soil or kicked out entirely.
details: Some onions are buried four inches deep: too far to sprout. Others are lying on the surface, dried out.
I replanted what I could. Maybe half will come up.
reactions: Onions are forgiving plants: they'll grow if they can, and I'm counting on that stubbornness.

statCheck.pass: Freshly planted onion sets release sulfur compounds through the soil that mask the scent trail they use through the garden. They trample the planted area to clear the smell.
statCheck.fail: They walked through without a care. Onions aren't sacred to them.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps balancing a small stone on top of the gatepost. Every time the wind knocks it off, it puts it back.
details: I've watched the stone fall three times in one afternoon. Each time the creature climbs up and replaces it.
The stone is smooth and round: chosen for the task, I think.
reactions: I glued a stone to the gatepost to save the creature the trouble. It placed a second stone next to mine.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} ate every flower head off the marigolds and left the stems standing. A row of green sticks.
details: Marigolds are supposed to repel pests. These pests ate the repellent.
The stems look ridiculous standing there headless. The garden has no color now.
reactions: I'll replant. Different flowers this time: something less delicious.

statCheck.pass: Marigold petals contain repellent oils, but the petals themselves are sweet and soft. Lavender has a stronger deterrent scent that they actually avoid.
statCheck.fail: They ate the pest-repelling flowers. Irony isn't lost on me.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} figured out the water pump and drinks from it by pressing the handle down with its weight.
details: It stands on the handle until water comes, drinks, then hops off. Gravity does the work.
The pump squeaks every time. I hear the creature's visits from inside.
reactions: I oiled the pump. The visits continue but at least they're quieter.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pried the lid off the pickle barrel and drank all the brine. Left the pickles dry and exposed.
details: Pickles without brine are just cucumbers with regrets. They're softening already.
The barrel lid was weighted with a stone. They moved the stone, pried the lid, and drank.
reactions: I'm making fresh brine. The pickles might survive if I act fast enough.

statCheck.pass: Salt brine is a mineral source they can't get easily elsewhere. A shallow dish of salted water near the barrel satisfies the craving without them breaking into the stores.
statCheck.fail: They wanted the brine. The pickles were just in the way.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has taken over the old boot I use as a doorstop. Sleeps inside it every afternoon.
details: The boot is on its side and the creature fits perfectly: head poking out the top.
The door swings freely now. I use a rock instead.
reactions: The creature gets the boot and I get the rock. We've both made our choices.

entries.intro: {mob_type} tore the mesh off the window screen and came inside. Found them sleeping on the kitchen floor.
details: The mesh is shredded from top to bottom. Every insect in the county is welcome now.
They left when I opened the door. Didn't seem bothered by my presence.
reactions: New mesh, tacked from the inside this time. Let them try to pull it inward.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps turning the sundial's gnomon a few degrees every visit. The time is always slightly wrong.
details: It nudges the gnomon with its nose. The shadow falls differently each day.
I was late to three things last week before I realized the sundial was being adjusted.
reactions: I've cemented the gnomon in place. The creature presses its nose against it and nothing happens.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} have been using the garden path as a thoroughfare every night. Muddy tracks from one end to the other.
details: The tracks are deep and overlapping: a well-used route.
I sweep the path clean every morning. By the next morning, it's a mud trail again.
reactions: I've accepted the commute. They go their way: I sweep mine.

statCheck.pass: They follow scent trails along the path of least resistance: a clean garden path is easier walking than brush or uneven ground. Laying rough gravel makes the path less comfortable.
statCheck.fail: They use the path because it's there. Can't blame them.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} found a loose thread on my scarf and pulled it until the whole bottom row unraveled.
details: The creature ran off trailing two arm-lengths of yarn behind it.
The scarf is an inch shorter now and fraying at the edge.
reactions: I trimmed the edge and knotted it. The scarf is functional, if less dignified.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} chewed the bottom rail of the garden trellis until it snapped. The whole thing folded over onto the beans.
details: The bean plants are pinned under the fallen trellis. Some stems snapped.
Rebuilding the rail means cutting new wood, fitting it, and retraining the vines.
reactions: The trellis is back up with the bottom rail wrapped in tin.

statCheck.pass: The bottom rail sits in damp soil and develops a soft, spongy texture they can gnaw easily. Raising the rail an inch above ground level keeps it dry and hard.
statCheck.fail: They chewed the bottom rail. It was the easiest to reach.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} collected cobwebs from the shed and draped them over the drying laundry. Every piece is sticky now.
details: The cobwebs are matted into the fabric. Picking them out takes forever.
I washed those shirts yesterday. Now they need washing again.
reactions: The laundry dries inside until the creatures find a new hobby.

entries.intro: {mob_type} ate the linseed oil coating right off the axe handle. The wood is rough and splintering now.
details: I oiled that handle last week. They licked it clean in one night.
Swinging an axe with a splintering handle means blisters by midday.
reactions: I'll re-oil and keep the axe indoors. The shed is too accessible.

statCheck.pass: Linseed oil cures with a sweet, nutty flavor that intensifies as it dries. Tung oil has the same protective quality but a bitter taste they avoid.
statCheck.fail: They licked the oil off. I don't pretend to understand their appetites.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been pressing flowers into the wet clay on the side of the house. A growing mural.
details: Each flower is pressed flat and stuck firmly. The pattern grows by one or two per week.
The clay holds the flowers well. The wall looks almost decorated on purpose.
reactions: I've started pointing out which flowers I like. The creature may or may not be taking requests.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} pulled every cork stopper out of the drying rack and buried them in the yard. Twenty corks, all gone.
details: The corks were drying for reuse. They're in the dirt now, somewhere.
I found three by stepping on them. The rest are still missing.
reactions: I'm buying new corks. Cheaper than excavating the yard.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has learned that the loose board on the porch makes a sound when stepped on. Bounces on it every dawn.
details: The board creaks rhythmically: the creature bounces on it like a musician.
It does this at dawn. Every dawn.
reactions: I nailed the board down. The creature found the next loose one.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} knocked every pot off the kitchen windowsill from the outside. Herbs, soil, and ceramic everywhere.
details: The pots shattered on the floor. Soil mixed with broken pottery and crushed leaves.
The basil I'd been growing all season was in one of those pots.
reactions: I'm moving the pots to the inside sill. Farther from reach, closer to my cooking.

statCheck.pass: Pots on exterior windowsills vibrate in wind and the sound draws them to investigate. Felt pads under the pots eliminate the vibration.
statCheck.fail: They knocked everything off. The usual mischief.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} discovered the wind-up music box on the shelf and has been turning the key with its teeth. Same tune, every visit.
details: The creature turns the key three times: always three. Then sits and listens until the tune ends.
The mechanism is wearing from use. The notes are getting slower.
reactions: I oil the mechanism. The creature gets its music: the music box gets a longer life.

entries.intro: {mob_type} got into the laundry basket and tangled themselves in the bedsheets. Dragged the whole lot through the yard.
details: The sheets are grass-stained, torn at the corners, and wrapped around three fence posts.
It took an hour to untangle everything. Some of it can't be saved.
reactions: The laundry basket stays indoors now. The yard is not a safe place for clean linen.

statCheck.pass: Damp linen releases a sour, milky scent as it dries that they find irresistible. Rinsing laundry in water with a splash of vinegar neutralizes the scent during drying.
statCheck.fail: They got into the laundry. I'll keep it inside.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been stealing single socks from the drying line. Always the left one.
details: Four left socks, gone over two weeks. The right ones hang undisturbed.
I found the socks in a pile behind the woodshed. The creature has a collection.
reactions: I retrieved the socks. The creature will probably take them again.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} pushed through the hedge and flattened a section wide enough to walk through. My privacy hedge has a doorway now.
details: The gap is at the same spot every time: they've made it a regular path.
Years of growing that hedge and they opened a passage in one night.
reactions: I'll fill the gap with thorny bushes. Something that discourages walking through.

statCheck.pass: They use the same path every night: the hedge gap is at the narrowest point on their route. Planting thorny species at that exact spot forces them to detour.
statCheck.fail: They pushed through. Hedges aren't walls.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps burying nuts in my seedbed and then digging them up a week later. The seeds get churned with every visit.
details: Tiny holes appear and disappear throughout the bed. My planted rows look randomized.
The creature remembers exactly where it buried each nut. I wish I had that memory.
reactions: The seedbed produces plants in no particular order now. I've given up on tidy rows.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pulled the horseshoe off the barn door where I'd nailed it for luck. Found it in the field, bent double.
details: The horseshoe was wrought iron, heavy and solid. They bent it like it was tin.
The nail is still in the door: they pulled the shoe right off it.
reactions: I'll hammer the horseshoe straight and nail it higher. My luck needs better placement.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been leaving small, smooth stones in a line along the garden wall. The line gets longer every day.
details: Each stone is about the same size and color: carefully selected.
The line is at ankle height along the base of the wall. Almost like a border.
reactions: The garden wall has never looked more intentional. I'm letting the creature finish its project.

entries.intro: {mob_type} ate through the bottom of the grain bag while it was hanging from the hook. A trail of grain across the shed floor.
details: The hole was chewed from below: they didn't even take the bag down.
Half the grain is on the floor, mixed with dust and droppings.
reactions: Metal bin for the grain. Bags are just wrapping paper to these creatures.

statCheck.pass: Grain stored in fabric absorbs moisture from the air and develops a warm, yeasty smell that penetrates the bag. Metal bins with sealed lids trap the scent inside.
statCheck.fail: They smelled the grain through the bag. Nothing stops that.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been rolling in the herb patch and then sitting on the porch smelling like lavender.
details: The creature smells wonderful. The herbs are flattened.
It rolls on one herb at a time: lavender one day, mint the next. Systematic.
reactions: My herb patch is a spa for wildlife and I can't even be annoyed about it.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} trampled the clay patch I'd laid out to dry for bricks. Footprints baked into every single one.
details: The bricks are usable, but every one has a creature's foot stamped into the surface.
I needed forty smooth bricks. I have forty bricks with decorative footprints instead.
reactions: I'm using them. The wall will have character whether I wanted it or not.

statCheck.pass: Wet clay holds the day's warmth longer than dry ground: they walk across it for the heat underfoot. Covering drying clay with a canvas shade cools it and removes the draw.
statCheck.fail: They walked through wet clay. I didn't think to cover it.

entries.intro: {mob_type} tore bark off the firewood stack and scattered the pieces across the yard.
details: Bark fragments everywhere: in the garden, on the path, stuck in the hedge.
The firewood underneath is exposed to rain now. It'll take longer to season.
reactions: I raked the bark up and re-covered the stack with canvas. Extra work for no gain.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got tangled in the grape vines and tore half the trellis down thrashing around.
details: The vines snapped in a dozen places. The grapes that were forming are scattered on the ground.
The trellis is listing sideways: the supports are cracked but not broken.
reactions: I braced the trellis and tied the broken vines. Some might recover: most won't.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} discovered that the copper bowl rings when tapped and visits every evening to tap it three times.
details: Three taps, then it sits back and listens to the ring fade. Then it leaves.
The bowl has tiny dents where the creature's paw hits. A record of every concert.
reactions: I've started listening for the three taps. It means the day is ending.

entries.intro: {mob_type} chewed through the hinge leather on the chest lid and now it won't stay open. Falls shut on my hands every time.
details: The leather was thick cowhide: they went through it at the thinnest point.
I prop the lid with a stick while I dig through the chest. An annoying workaround.
reactions: Iron hinges on order. The stick works for now, barely.

statCheck.pass: Leather hinges develop a salt crust in the fold where sweat collects, and they always chew at that point. Metal hinges have no fold and no salt.
statCheck.fail: Leather hinges and creatures: a losing combination.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps moving the garden markers to different positions. The labels no longer match what's planted.
details: The carrot marker is over the beans. The bean marker is in the herb row.
I fixed them yesterday. Today they're scrambled again.
reactions: I memorized where everything is planted. The markers are decorative now.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} scratched deep grooves into the front door overnight. The wood is scarred from bottom to handle height.
details: The scratches are deep enough to catch a fingernail. They weren't casual: they were persistent.
I just painted that door last month. The paint is curled up in ribbons.
reactions: I'll sand it down and repaint. Maybe a metal kick plate at the bottom this time.

statCheck.pass: They scratch doors at threshold height to mark territory with scent glands on their paws. A vinegar wash on the door base masks the existing scent and removes the marking incentive.
statCheck.fail: They scratched and scratched. Maybe they wanted in, maybe they were bored.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been tucking seeds into every crack in the stone wall. The wall will be a garden by spring.
details: It selects each crack, pushes a seed in with its nose, and moves on.
Some of the seeds from last month are already sprouting. The wall has green tufts.
reactions: I'm curious what grows. The creature's garden plan is out of my hands.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} got under the porch and have been digging. The boards are sagging where they've hollowed out the earth beneath.
details: I can feel the give underfoot: the soil is gone from under two boards.
Dirt piles at each end of the porch where they've been pushing excavated earth out.
reactions: I'm filling the space with gravel and sealing the gaps. The porch isn't a burrow.

statCheck.pass: They dig under structures for the stable temperature: it's cooler in summer and warmer in winter beneath a covered floor. Blocking all ground-level access forces them to find natural shelter.
statCheck.fail: They like the space under the porch. I'd rather they didn't.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been carefully picking aphids off the rose stems. The roses are thriving.
details: It works one stem at a time, from bottom to top. Methodical and thorough.
The creature eats each aphid and moves to the next. A living pesticide.
reactions: My roses haven't looked this good in years. The creature expects no thanks.

entries.intro: {mob_type} knocked the chimney cap off and now rain falls straight into the fireplace. Can't light a fire until it's fixed.
details: The cap landed in the garden: cracked the ceramic edge but still usable.
Getting it back up there means climbing the roof in damp weather.
reactions: The cap is back on, mortared this time. My knees didn't enjoy the roof work.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has taken to bringing earthworms to the doorstep. Leaves them in a neat pile and sits back expectantly.
details: Three worms this morning, laid out side by side. The creature watched me discover them.
I don't want the worms but I don't want to seem ungrateful either.
reactions: I move the worms to the garden. The creature seems to think I'm saving them for later.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} tore the rope lashing off the hay bales and the hay expanded everywhere. The entire barn floor is covered.
details: Loose hay is useless for transport: it's everywhere and impossible to rebale by hand.
The ropes are in pieces: each one bitten through in multiple spots.
reactions: I forked the loose hay into the loft and it took all morning.

statCheck.pass: Hay bale lashing traps fermenting plant matter between the rope and the bale surface: they chew the rope to reach the sweet residue. Wire lashing doesn't trap the same material.
statCheck.fail: They chewed through the ropes. They always chew through the ropes.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} scattered my pile of flat paving stones across the yard. I'd spent an afternoon sorting them by size.
details: The stones are heavy: they rolled them. The garden has dents where each one landed.
Sorting and stacking them again is a full day's work.
reactions: The stones go back in the pile. I'm paving sooner, before they scatter them again.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} dragged the milking stool out of the barn and left it in the middle of the field.
details: The stool is sitting upright in the grass like someone placed it there for a view.
The barn door was shut: they went through the window to get the stool.
reactions: I brought the stool back. The creatures will probably relocate it again tonight.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been catching moths and lining them up on the kitchen windowsill. Dead moths in a row, every evening.
details: The moths are arranged by size. The creature is meticulous about placement.
I don't know if it's eating them later or just displaying them. The collection grows.
reactions: I clear the windowsill with a rag. The creature restocks by the next night.

entries.intro: {mob_type} ate the whittling I'd been working on for a week. Just the finished parts: left the raw block alone.
details: I was carving a bird. The beak and the tail were done and they ate both.
The raw wood is untouched. They only wanted the parts I'd smoothed and oiled.
reactions: I'm carving indoors from now on. My hobbies don't survive the outdoors.

statCheck.pass: Oiled, smooth wood tastes different from raw: the oil soaks in and sweetens with handling. Unfinished pieces get ignored because they still taste like bark.
statCheck.fail: They have opinions about woodworking. Specifically, they eat the good parts.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been covering the sleeping cat with leaves. One leaf at a time, very gently.
details: The cat wakes up buried under a layer of leaves and shakes them off. The creature watches and starts over.
This has happened every afternoon for a week. Neither animal shows any sign of stopping.
reactions: The cat accepts its fate. The creature has a calling.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} piled mud against the base of the front door. Couldn't open it until I shoveled it clear.
details: The mud was packed solid: not just splashed, but deliberately pressed into place.
Took twenty minutes of shoveling before the door would swing free.
reactions: I cleared it, but I'm sure it'll be back.

statCheck.pass: They pack mud against doors to mark the threshold as territory: the mud holds their scent for weeks. Washing the door base with lye removes the scent signal.
statCheck.fail: Mud against the door: I don't know why they do it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps cleaning the outside of the window glass with its tongue. Streaks, but cleaner.
details: It licks in long vertical strokes: top to bottom, methodically.
The glass is smeared but the dirt is gone. An imperfect service.
reactions: I wipe the streaks off from inside. Between the two of us, the window stays clear.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pulled the spigot out of the cider barrel and the cider ran all over the floor. A week's pressing, lost.
details: The cellar smells wonderful and the floor is sticky. That's all I have to show for the batch.
The spigot was wooden: they pulled it out in one go.
reactions: Metal spigot, threaded and sealed. And the cellar door gets a better lock.

statCheck.pass: Cider fermenting in the barrel releases gas through the spigot seal: they smell it and investigate. A sealed airlock on top vents the gas upward where they're less likely to notice it.
statCheck.fail: They smelled the cider. I don't blame them: the whole cellar smelled of it.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been carefully stacking flat stones into a tiny tower on the fence post. Rebuilds it when it falls.
details: The tower reached five stones high before the wind took it. The creature started over immediately.
Each stone is tested for balance before being placed. The creature has standards.
reactions: I find myself checking the tower every morning. Rooting for it, almost.

entries.intro: {mob_type} chewed the handle off the water dipper. It's just a bowl on the end of a ragged stick now.
details: The dipper was a nice piece: carved from one piece of wood with a smooth grip.
Now it's a splinter-filled stump that barely holds together.
reactions: I'll carve a new one. And hang it indoors where teeth can't reach.

statCheck.pass: Carved wood develops a sweet patina from hand oil over time, and they chew the most-handled section first. Unfinished handles get left alone.
statCheck.fail: The handle was the tastiest part, apparently.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been leading ants away from the pantry by placing crumbs in a trail toward the garden.
details: The crumbs form a neat line from the doorstep into the grass. The ants follow it reliably.
The pantry has fewer ants than any other room. The creature's redirection works.
reactions: I leave a few crumbs by the door to help the creature's project. Cooperation.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} pulled the weather stripping out from under the door. Every draft the house had kept out is back.
details: The strip is in chewed pieces on the porch. They extracted it in one clean pull.
The cold air under the door is a constant now. My feet haven't been warm since.
reactions: New weather stripping, nailed from the inside. They can pull at it but they won't get a grip.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} tangled themselves in the horse's mane through the fence and spooked it. The horse kicked out a rail.
details: The rail is split down the middle. The horse is skittish around that section now.
Fixing the rail means calming the horse first, then carpentry. Both take patience.
reactions: New rail, and I'm patching the fence gap with thorny brush in the meantime.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} ate the beeswax candles I'd just dipped. The whole drying rack, stripped clean.
details: Twenty candles, still warm from dipping. They ate the wax right off the wicks.
The wicks are hanging bare on the rack like tiny ropes. Not a speck of wax left.
reactions: I dip candles indoors now. The drying time is longer, but the yield is higher.

statCheck.pass: Warm beeswax on the wick releases a honey scent that travels far on still air. Dipping candles indoors traps the scent: dipping outdoors broadcasts it.
statCheck.fail: They ate fresh candles. The wax hadn't cooled enough to lose the scent.

entries.intro: {mob_type} filled the drainage ditch with sticks and mud until it dammed. The garden flooded overnight.
details: The water pooled around the root vegetables and turned the soil to swamp.
Clearing the dam was messy work: knee-deep mud and tangled branches.
reactions: I cleared it out, but they'll probably rebuild it by morning.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pulled the shutters open in the middle of the night. I woke up to moonlight in my face.
details: The latch was lifted from outside: they're dexterous when they want something.
I closed the shutters and latched them. Twenty minutes later: open again.
reactions: I bolted the shutters from the inside. The moonlight stays where it belongs.

statCheck.pass: They open shutters that radiate kitchen warmth: the wood holds heat from the hearth fire on the other side. A shutter with an interior bolt eliminates the exterior grip point.
statCheck.fail: They opened the shutters. I don't know why they'd bother.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} discovered the pen pot on the writing desk and has been stealing quills one at a time.
details: It takes them through the open window: one quill per visit, always the longest.
I've gone from six quills to two. The creature is building something that requires feathered shafts.
reactions: I close the window when I'm not at the desk. The remaining quills are under guard.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} got into the charcoal bin and tracked black smudges through every room. The house looks like a crime scene.
details: Black footprints on the floorboards, the rug, and up the stairs. They explored thoroughly.
Charcoal dust doesn't sweep up easily: it smears first, then slowly comes clean.
reactions: Three hours of scrubbing. The charcoal bin has a lock now.

statCheck.pass: Charcoal absorbs kitchen grease from the air and the grease makes the charcoal smell like food. Storing charcoal in a sealed metal bin prevents the absorption.
statCheck.fail: They got into the charcoal. I didn't think charcoal was interesting to creatures.

entries.intro: {mob_type} ripped the straw stuffing out of the scarecrow and used it to line the mud under the hedge.
details: The scarecrow is an empty set of clothes on a stick. Effective as a coat rack, not as a deterrent.
The straw is trampled into the mud now: can't reuse it.
reactions: I'll restuff the scarecrow. The creatures will probably unstuff it again.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} ate the seed potatoes I was saving for next season's planting. Every one: gone from the cellar shelf.
details: The cellar window was left open a crack for ventilation. Just enough for them.
Seed potatoes aren't cheap and they're hard to replace this time of year.
reactions: I'm buying new seed stock. The cellar window gets a mesh cover.

statCheck.pass: Potatoes left in a dark, slightly damp space develop a starchy sweetness as they prepare to sprout. They can smell the sugar change through small gaps.
statCheck.fail: They found the potatoes. I thought the cellar was safe: it wasn't.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} pulled every thistle I'd cleared and dragged them back onto the path. Prickles everywhere again.
details: I spent an afternoon clearing that path. By morning, every thistle was back.
They must have pulled them from the waste pile. Deliberate replanting.
reactions: I burned the waste pile. They can't put back what's ash.

entries.intro: {mob_type} used the freshly tarred fence as a scratching post. There's fur embedded in the tar and gouges in the wood.
details: The tar is a mess: smeared, clumped, and covered in coarse hair.
I just finished tarring that section yesterday. The tar wasn't even fully set.
reactions: I'll re-tar when it's dry. And hope they find somewhere else to scratch.

statCheck.pass: Fresh tar is warm and slightly soft: they press against it because it eases skin irritation the way a poultice does. Once the tar hardens fully they lose interest.
statCheck.fail: They like rubbing against things. The tar was just in the wrong place at the wrong time.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} found the loose floorboard and has been storing acorns in the gap underneath.
details: I hear a rolling sound under the floor every time it makes a deposit.
There must be fifty acorns under there by now. The floor rattles when I walk over them.
reactions: I'll leave the acorns until winter. The creature planned ahead and I can respect that.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} knocked the stepping stones out of the stream crossing. Every one is downstream now.
details: The stones are heavy: they must have pushed hard. I can see them under the water twenty paces down.
Crossing the stream means getting wet until I fish them out and reset them.
reactions: I spent the afternoon wading in cold water retrieving stones. My joints remember it.

statCheck.pass: Flat stones in running water develop a slippery algae layer: they push the stones to scrape the algae off and eat it. Rougher stones resist the algae growth.
statCheck.fail: They pushed the stones out. I don't know if it was deliberate or accidental.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been leaving a single feather on the pillow every night. Through the open window, onto the bed.
details: Always one feather, always on the pillow. The creature enters and exits without a sound.
The feathers are different types: some downy, some long and rigid.
reactions: I have a collection of feathers now. The creature is generous, in its way.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} ate the soap I left out for washing. The basin, the brush, and the drying rack are untouched: just the soap.
details: It was good soap too: rendered from tallow with rosemary mixed in.
The teeth marks on the edge of the soap dish tell the whole story.
reactions: I keep the soap in a tin box now. The basin stays outside: the soap doesn't.

statCheck.pass: Tallow soap has a rendered fat scent that intensifies in damp air. Soap made with plant oil instead of tallow doesn't attract them.
statCheck.fail: They ate the soap. I didn't know soap was food.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been pulling sticks out of the kindling box and scattering rejects across the floor.
details: The kindling ends up better organized after its visit: sorted by length, more or less.
The creature rejects some sticks entirely and sets them aside on the floor.
reactions: I sweep up the rejects. The creature has opinions about my firewood and I can't change them.

entries.intro: {mob_type} trampled the lettuce bed flat. Not a single head left standing.
details: The lettuce is ground into the soil: a green smear where rows used to be.
I don't think they ate any. They just walked through, oblivious.
reactions: I'll reseed and border the bed with stones. A physical reminder that the path is elsewhere.

statCheck.pass: Lettuce has a high water content that releases a faint sweet scent at ground level: they follow the scent trail right across the bed. Bordering with aromatic herbs masks it.
statCheck.fail: They walked through and flattened everything. The lettuce was collateral.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} has been polishing a specific stone on the garden path by rubbing its belly on it. The stone gleams.
details: It lies on the stone and rotates slowly. The friction has given the surface a smooth sheen.
No other stone gets this treatment. This one must feel right.
reactions: I have one polished stone among dozens of dull ones. It's becoming a landmark.

entries.intro: The {mob_type} broke the clay pipe that drains the roof water. The wall is stained with runoff.
details: The pipe cracked where it joins the wall: a clean break from something leaning against it.
Without the pipe, rainwater runs down the face of the house and pools at the foundation.
reactions: I'll fit a new pipe: ceramic again, but braced with iron straps this time.

statCheck.pass: Clay pipes absorb warmth from roof runoff and radiate it at the wall joint: they lean against the warm spot. An insulated bracket between pipe and wall removes the heat transfer.
statCheck.fail: They leaned on it and it broke. Clay isn't built for that.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} keeps delivering live beetles to the cat. The cat doesn't know what to do with them.
details: The beetle sits on the floor, the cat stares at it, and the creature watches both. A triangle of confusion.
Three beetles this week. The cat has batted none of them.
reactions: I relocate the beetles outside. The creature will bring more: the cycle is established.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} pulled the measuring string out of the garden and wound it around a tree. Took ten minutes to untangle.
details: The string was taut between two stakes. Now it's wrapped tight around an oak trunk.
The stakes are pulled out and thrown aside. My planting guide is ruined.
reactions: I re-measured and re-staked. Deeper stakes, heavier string.

entries.intro: Some {mob_type} knocked the birdbath off its pedestal. The basin cracked in half on the flagstones.
details: The pedestal is fine: the basin is in two pieces. Water won't hold in halves.
The birds circle overhead looking confused. Their water source is rubble.
reactions: I'll mortar a new basin. The old one served well until the creatures found it.

entries.intro: {mob_type} pried the nails out of the fence repair I did yesterday. The board is hanging by one nail again.
details: Three nails, removed cleanly. The board swings in the breeze.
I hammered those nails flush. They pulled them out straight: no bending, no breaking.
reactions: Countersunk screws from now on: the fence stays fixed or I lose my mind.

statCheck.pass: New nails in softwood leave a resin scent at the head that attracts them: they grip and pull toward the smell. Coating the nail heads with a drop of tar masks the resin.
statCheck.fail: They pull nails. I don't understand it: I just have to outsmart it.
